item: picker re-derives Peak C/D formulas inline: c_C_const L775-781, base_D L821-833, third Peak-D FFT copy L835+L850-851
evidence: Verified term-by-term that the copies are currently CONSISTENT: c_C_const (L776-780) = _peak_C_fit_one_rchunk persistent dict (L396-406) exactly [4*c128(nk,ns,mu,nb_total)/p_xy + c128(nq,mu,mu)/p_xy + c128(nq_disk,mu,ngkmax)/p_xy + sphere_idx]; base_D (L826-833) = _peak_D_accumulate persistent (L462-472) + zeta_chunk transient (L501-502); and fft_per_row*cs (L835, L850-851) = fft_box_bytes (L482) since _bytes_c128(cs,n_rtot) = cs*_bytes_c128(n_rtot) (both are 16*cs*n_rtot*factor_D, no sharding divisor on either). So no bug today — but any Peak C/D formula change must be made in 2-3 places, and L849 admits it ('replicated here as a quick estimate'). Fix: have the picker call the peak functions with the scaled variable zeroed (e.g. r_chunk=0 / gflat_chunk_size=0) to obtain the constant part, making peak fns the single source of truth. Violates the no-redundancy rule.

item: N_SPHERE_IDX_BUFFERS both =1 (L207-208) + is_bispinor branch selecting equal constants + stale '8 buffers / 3' comment (L723-725)
evidence: L207-208: both constants are 1; L726-727 branch is a no-op today. The comment at L723-725 ('At bispinor 4-channel V_q we measured 8 buffers; non-bispinor charge-only is 3') is pre-Round-4 history stated in present tense — stale narrative, should say 'historically'. However the two-named-constants scaffold is EXPLICITLY intentional: L204-206 'We keep these as named constants so future calibration / agent probes have a single source of truth', and tests pin both independently (test_planner_refit test_sphere_idx_replicated_in_every_peak uses ..._BISPINOR, test_non_bispinor_uses_fewer_sphere_buffers imports ..._CHARGE and its docstring records the 3->1 history). Fix the L723-725 comment tense; keep the constants.

item: legacy alias fft_box_factor overwriting fft_box_factor_A (L647, L704-706); sole production caller uses legacy name (gw_init.py:620)
evidence: L704-706: if fft_box_factor is not None: fft_box_factor_A = float(fft_box_factor). gw_init.py:620 passes fft_box_factor=4.0, which equals the fft_box_factor_A default (L648), so collapsing to one name is behavior-neutral: change gw_init to fft_box_factor_A=4.0 (or drop the arg entirely) and delete L647+L704-706. No other caller uses either spelling. Classic old/new parallel kwarg path — exactly what the project's no-redundancy rule forbids.

item: target_utilization docstring vs gw_init hardcoded 0.80 (gflat_memory_model.py:687-689 vs gw_init.py:610-620)
evidence: The docstring L687-689 claims 'target_utilization <- cohsex.in chunk_target_utilization (default 0.94 here; cohsex.in default 0.97)' under the 'User-overrideable kwargs' heading — this wiring DOES NOT EXIST. Verified: gw_init.py:617-620 hardcodes target_utilization=0.80 with an explicit comment that chunk_target_utilization feeds only the LEGACY compute_optimal_chunks path — confirmed at gw_init.py:1198-1200 (compute_optimal_chunks receives target_utilization=mem.chunk_target_utilization). So three numbers coexist by design (0.94 planner default used only by tests that omit the kwarg; 0.80 hand-tuned production; 0.97 cohsex legacy default), and the 0.80 is intentional per its call-site comment ('Wire it through MemoryConfig only when we add a separate gflat_target_utilization knob'). Defect = misleading planner docstring that tells a user the cohsex knob controls the gflat planner. Fix docstring now; adding gflat_target_utilization is the stated future path.

item: magic constants: GFLAT_CHUNK_SIZE_CAP=100 (L170), GFLAT_CHUNK_FLOOR=4/bc_floor_factor=4 (L818-819), 0.5*target in band_chunk cap (L882)
evidence: Cap=100: exhaustively documented empiricism (L159-170: cs=1414 OOM at production CrI3 80Ry, cuFFT plan-algorithm crossover ~cs 1000, cs=1 vs cs=360 perf within 15% per agent_d M3) and pinned by test_gflat_chunk_size_capped_at_100_regardless_of_budget. Floor=4: documented as cuFFT plan amortisation (L817-819, L872-874), pinned by test_gflat_chunk_size_picks_multiple_of_4_below_cap. The 0.5 at L882 (bc_cap = 0.5*target/per_unit_bc) is the one undocumented number: it reserves half the target for Peak A's non-FFT-box terms (phase_table c128(nk,n_rtot) at L320 is the big one — 0.65 GB at CrI3, but scales with nk*n_rtot) rather than solving Peak A's headroom exactly like steps 1-2 do for C/D. Deliberate conservatism, but deserves a one-line comment; not a bug (band_chunk then gets pow2-rounded down and mesh-floored, and Peak A is re-evaluated in full at L905-910 and included in the HWM max, so an over-large bc would still show up in the reported HWM).

item: nq = meta.nk_tot q-grid==k-grid assumption (L711/714), Peak E sphere-idx call passes nq=nk (L587), ngkmax fallback 0.06*n_rtot (gw_init.py:597)
evidence: q == k-grid is a codebase-wide invariant, not planner-local: the whole zeta/V_q pipeline builds q as k-k' differences on the same Gamma-centered mesh (v_q_g_flat.py IBZ q-loop over the k-mesh; n_q_full == nk_tot throughout symmetry_maps unfold). Keeping nk and nq as separate variables makes the byte formulas self-documenting (which axis is which) even though they are numerically equal; a hypothetical q!=k grid would invalidate far more than this planner. The ngkmax fallback `int(getattr(meta,'ngkmax',0)) or int(0.06*meta.n_rtot)` at gw_init.py:597 is a sphere/box volume-ratio heuristic; it only fires when meta lacks ngkmax and mainly scales gflat_acc (nq_disk,mu,ngkmax) — a real estimate but explicitly labeled `_ngkmax_est`, and the same 0.06 ratio is used by tests/_fake_meta. Fragility to note, not a bug.

item: Peak E uses mu_L=mu_R=mu and worst-case TT-off-diag; no 640:200 charge:transverse split (L936-966)
evidence: Verified: both _peak_E_v_q_per_tile_transient calls pass mu_L=mu, mu_R=mu (L937, L946) with mu = meta.n_rmu_padded (L713) — a single centroid count — and the bispinor binding tile is TT-off-diag with same_zeta=False (L945-951, so zeta_R_all fires at full (n_q_ibz, mu, ngkmax)). The production plan is ~640 charge : ~200 transverse centroids (3:1), so a TT tile is really (mu_T=200)^2-shaped, not mu^2: if meta carries the charge mu, TT terms are over-estimated ~3x (conservative); if it carries the transverse mu, the CC tile is under-estimated. Consistent with the empirically observed planner inaccuracy for bispinor (empirical band ceilings ~3x below non-bispinor while the planner stays conservative). Not a correctness bug — direction is conservative in the production configuration — but modeling the two-mu split (mu_C, mu_T as separate inputs to Peak E and Peak C) is the right refactor and dovetails with making the peak functions the single source of truth.

item: V_acc_full_BZ 'subtracted to avoid double-counting' comment vs code that never adds it (L977-982)
evidence: Code verified: E_total = E_per_tile + E_lorentz (L980-982) where E_per_tile = sum(peak_E.values()) and peak_E comes from _peak_E_v_q_per_tile_transient only — peak_E_unfold's E.V_acc_full_BZ enters peak_E_all (L969-971), which feeds ONLY the peak_components display dict (L993-995), never a total. So no subtraction exists; the comment describes an older implementation (or a plan) while the current mechanism is 'display-only, excluded from the sum'. Net accounting is correct in the default full-BZ regime (n_q_ibz=nq, so unfolded V_acc == per-tile V_acc size and the alias claim L599-603 holds). Edge worth one comment line: with n_q_ibz < nq the unfolded (n_q_full,mu,mu) buffer is nq/n_q_ibz times LARGER than the per-tile V_acc it supposedly aliases, so E_total slightly under-counts the unfold moment — mitigated because zeta_L_all is freed before unfold (L594-596). Fix the comment; optionally max(per-tile, unfold-moment) for the IBZ regime.

item: Optional[int] on gflat_chunk_size (L217), silent except-pass on AOT failure (L498-499), fft_grid cube-root fallback (L721-722)
evidence: L217: the field's own comment ('always int after the 2026-05-17 cap'), the constructor (L1001 int(gflat_chunk_size)), and test_gflat_chunk_size_above_cufft_floor's not-None assertion all contradict Optional[int] — stale type annotation, one-line fix. L498-499: except Exception: pass is inside the use_query_fft_peak_bytes branch that no caller enables (see dead verdict above) and is a documented deliberate fallback to the constant formula (L487-489); dies with that branch or gets a warning print if kept. L721-722: the cube-root fft_grid fallback is ACTUALLY EXERCISED — tests/test_band_chunk_size_floor.py _fake_meta (L32-42) omits fft_grid, so all 6 tests in that file hit it; its comment already says it exists to keep SimpleNamespace test metas working. Test-supporting convenience, not production risk (production MetaInfo sets fft_grid); tightening would mean fixing _fake_meta first.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py — SCState, gw_iteration_map (claimed externally-unused exports)
evidence: Both are load-bearing via a live entry point: gw_jax.py:482-484 imports make_initial_state_from_dft/run_self_consistency; those construct/return SCState (sc_iteration.py:164, 328, 590) and call gw_iteration_map (sc_iteration.py:434, 473, 548); gw_jax.py:552/569/576-582 then consume SCState attributes (.last_sigma_result, .H_qp_dft — the latter feeds the sigma_total override). Gate is config.self_consistent (gw_config.py:1072; documented docs_gwjax/COHSEX_INPUT.md:109). Enumerated call mechanisms: no getattr/dict/string dispatch, no console_scripts/python -m entry, no refs in tools/, scripts/, docs usage, lorrax_sandbox skills/, templates/, or runs/ (all 442 cohsex.in files set self_consistent = false). Claim of zero test coverage is CONFIRMED (only tests/test_band_partition.py covers the adjacent partition primitive; nothing under tests/ mentions sc_iteration). So: not confirmed-dead, not test-only — alive but exercised only by manual QSGW runs; __all__ over-advertising is a doc nit only.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:89-94 vs :114 — SCInputs docstring claims partition defaults to BandPartition.all_protected(nb_active); field is required
evidence: Docstring stale, confirmed. sc_iteration.py:114 `partition: BandPartition` has no default and band_partition.py:76-79 (all_protected docstring) repeats the same stale claim ('Used as the default in sc_iteration.SCInputs'). A dataclass default is structurally impossible: all_protected(nb_active) needs a runtime band count, and partition (line 114) precedes two required fields (115-116). Sole production caller gw_jax.py:516-528 always passes partition explicitly, so no runtime effect. Bonus finding: BandPartition.all_protected itself has ZERO production callers — only tests/test_band_partition.py:41 — the 'default' it was written for never landed. Fix = delete the two stale docstring sentences (and consider all_protected's fate).

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:730 — E_qp_nk = enk_qp_ry * 0.5 magic Ry→Hartree constant
evidence: Conversion is CORRECT and matches the consumer contract: file_io/qp_wfn.py:38 documents E_qp_nk as 'in Hartree atomic units', and qp_wfn.py:56-57 stores E_qp_nk_hartree=E and E_qp_nk_rydberg=E*2.0 — so passing Ry*0.5 = Hartree is exactly right (1 Ha = 2 Ry). Downstream consumer is the offline CLI src/postprocess/rotate_wfn_to_qp.py, which reads the h5 datasets by unit-labeled name. Line 730 already carries an inline '# Ry → Hartree' comment. Only cleanup value is a named RY_TO_HARTREE constant; no bug.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:209-221 — _KSHARD_EIGH_CACHE module-global jit cache keyed by id(mesh_xy)
evidence: The first-pass 'stale kernels after GC id-reuse' hypothesis is WRONG: the cached values are jitted closures from _make_kshard_eigh that capture NamedSharding(mesh_xy, ...) objects (sc_iteration.py:183-185), and NamedSharding holds a strong reference to the mesh — so the module-global dict pins every keyed Mesh alive and its id() can never be recycled to a different Mesh. Stale-kernel return is impossible. Actual defects are minor: unbounded per-mesh growth (a leak in hypothetical multi-mesh test loops — and no tests exercise sc_iteration anyway) and unnecessary id() keying since jax.sharding.Mesh is hashable and could key the dict directly. Production uses one mesh per process → single entry.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:539-565 — _run_rcrop.residual_fn mutates closure lists; SCState.iteration counts pipeline calls not rCROP iterations
evidence: Confirmed impure by read, but it is the deliberate adapter shape: mixing/acceleration.py:812-827 rcrop_nojit demands a bare f(x)->residual and is explicitly documented 'for use when residual_fn contains JIT'd code' — the closure lists (_last_sigma, _e_history, _iter_idx, rms_history) exist to capture the last SigmaResult for the final writer and to print per-call RMS, which cannot travel through the solver's flat-vector interface. The 2-map-calls-per-rCROP-iteration semantics are explicitly documented at sc_iteration.py:513-516. The iteration-count mismatch is harmless: grep of src/ shows SCState.iteration has NO consumers outside sc_iteration.py (gw_jax reads only .last_sigma_result and .H_qp_dft; the printed count uses len(sc_rms_history)). Mild refactor appetite (return bookkeeping from a richer solver callback) but nothing wrong today.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:735-739 — try/except Exception: pass around multihost sync_global_devices barrier
evidence: The docstring guarantee (sc_iteration.py:709-712, 'caller can rely on both files existing on every rank') is technically weakened, but the guarantee is not load-bearing in-run: grep shows the ONLY consumers of WFN_qp.h5/qp_wfn_rotations.h5 are the offline postprocess CLI (src/postprocess/rotate_wfn_to_qp.py, invoked as a separate `python -m` run per src/postprocess/__init__.py:9) — no code later in the same gw_jax run reads them. The except covers the real case: single-process/local runs where jax.distributed is uninitialized and the barrier can raise, and where the guarantee holds trivially (rank 0 is the only rank; write completes before return). Silent swallow of a genuine multihost barrier failure is theoretically possible but such failures kill the job anyway. Worth narrowing the except and softening the docstring; not a live bug.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:40-55 — metals NOT robust; TODO diagonal-Σ for inactive in-grid bands (design 2026-05-08)
evidence: Confirmed a documented, dated scope limitation, not a hidden bug: lines 32-47 spell out exactly which assumptions hold (insulator + sorted bands robust; metals need re-sort + re-occupy because chi0's val/cond slicing assumes strict ordering, line 43-44) and lines 49-55 flag the undecided straddling-band case 'for a separate design pass'. Consistent with the rest of the module (midgap E_F at lines 235-237 is insulator-only by construction) and with the codebase's insulator-first status. Correct classification is deferred design work, honestly advertised.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:191/199, 544, 589 — triple Hermitisation
evidence: Each site guards a distinct entry path: (1) kernel-level Hermitise (191/199) is the universal guard — it also covers H_qp_dft_new produced by apply_band_partition and the linear-mixing path, which do NOT pass through line 544; (2) line 544 protects gw_iteration_map's input after rCROP's mixing combinations, with an explicit comment (541-543) that rCROP linear combos drift off the Hermitian manifold; (3) line 589 Hermitises the final rCROP x ON THE CARRIED STATE, which matters beyond eigh because gw_jax consumes state_final.H_qp_dft directly (sigma_total override, gw_jax.py:569/606 region), not only through the (self-Hermitising) eigh kernels. Yes, 544→191 is redundant on that one path, but Hermitisation is an idempotent O(nk·nb²) projection — noise next to the Σ pipeline. Defensive, cheap, each occurrence justified.

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:664-681 — dump_sigma_omega_h5_final calls private ppm_pipeline._write_sigma_omega_h5 with SimpleNamespace(sigma_kij_h5_path=None) stub
evidence: Confirmed safe today by branch analysis: ppm_pipeline.py:258 branches on `sigma_c_omega is not None` → in-memory write_sigma_omega_h5; sigma_omega.sigma_kij_h5_path is consulted ONLY in the elif at ppm_pipeline.py:269 (streamed-fallback copy path). dump_sigma_omega_h5_final guards at sc_iteration.py:662 (returns None unless state.last_sigma_result.sigma_c_omega_kij_ry is present), so the in-memory branch is always taken and the stub attribute is never read — exactly as the inline comment (669-672) claims. But the seam is fragile: sc_iteration imports a private underscore function and must hand-model its two-backend branch structure; any reordering of _write_sigma_omega_h5's branches silently breaks the stub contract. Split the in-memory writer into a public helper both callers use (fits the no-parallel-paths rule).

item: src/gw/sc_iteration.py:241-244 — _rotate_to_dft_basis einsum 'kmp,kpq,knq->kmn' over (U, O_qp, conj(U)); chi_from_dipole.py:37 says OPPOSITE direction
evidence: Verified by explicit index math, not string-matching. Convention: U[k,m,n]=⟨DFT_m|QP_n⟩ (sc_iteration.py:9-10 and final_qp_eigenstates docstring :629), so |QP_n⟩=Σ_m|DFT_m⟩U[m,n]. Then O_DFT[m,n]=⟨DFT_m|O|DFT_n⟩=Σ_pq⟨DFT_m|QP_p⟩·O_QP[p,q]·⟨QP_q|DFT_n⟩=Σ_pq U[m,p]·O_QP[p,q]·conj(U[n,q]) — element-for-element identical to einsum('kmp,kpq,knq->kmn')(U, O_qp, conj(U)) and to the docstring formula at line 243. chi_from_dipole.py:30-40 describes the DFT→QP direction for the velocity operator, V^QP=U†·V^DFT·U ('kpm,akpq,kqn' with conj(U) on the LEFT) — that is the inverse transform for the opposite basis change under the SAME U; the two are mutually consistent, and the comment is a deliberate signpost warning future SC-dipole work, not evidence of a sprung trap. This is also NOT the BGW p−vNL vs p+vNL sign convention (that lives in the dipole driver, per project memory); both files here share one U convention. Both usages verified: gw_jax.py:600-608 rotates QP-basis SigmaResult fields back to DFT with the converged U — the correct direction for that data flow.

item: src/common/wfn_transforms.py: to_rbox
evidence: Repo-wide grep (src/tests/tools/scripts + sandbox skills/ and runs/): only callers are tests/test_wfn_transforms.py:144,167,186 (reference oracle for to_rmu/to_rchunk tests). Non-call references are its own module docstring/__all__ (wfn_transforms.py:33,53), a cross-ref in to_rmu's docstring (:417), the sign-convention comment (:1253), and a pipeline-map mention in file_io/wfn_loader.py:44. No string dispatch reaches it (only dynamic getattr in tree is postprocess/__init__.py:23, scoped to rotate_wfn_to_qp); console scripts (pyproject.toml:32-35) route to gw_jax/kmeans_cli which never import it. It serves as the tests' ground-truth full-box transform, so test-only, not deletable without rewriting tests.

item: src/common/wfn_transforms.py: to_rmu_inner
evidence: Only references: tests/test_wfn_transforms.py:22,291,319 and its own __all__ (wfn_transforms.py:54). Its intended consumer gflat_to_rmu does NOT call it — the scan body (wfn_transforms.py:941-957) inlines _box_kernel+ifftn+gather but deliberately diverges: it applies the Bloch phase post-gather on the (cs, ns, n_rmu) samples only (lines 947-957, scratch n_rmu vs n_rtot per the comment at 762-765), whereas to_rmu_inner phases the full FFT box (line 694-695). So it is not even a factorable body of the production kernel. Contrast: sibling to_rchunk_inner IS production-alive via isdf_fitting.py:140,743. Zero references in sandbox skills/runs.

item: src/common/gvec_fft_box.py: make_fft_box_kernel
evidence: Zero live references: defined gvec_fft_box.py:69, listed in __all__ (:26) and its MD doc (GVEC_FFT_BOX_GATHER.md:58); wfn_transforms.py:186 mentions it only in a comment naming _box_kernel as the algorithm's replacement. No test imports gvec_fft_box at all (grep over tests/tools/scripts empty); the only live export of the module is build_g_index_for_fft_box (imported at file_io/wfn_loader.py:428). Sandbox runs/ hits (runs/Si/B_compile_floor_check/*/compile.log, .mlir) are 2026-04-25 compile-log artifacts of an old lorrax_B build, i.e. historical output, not callers. Not reachable via console scripts or string dispatch.

item: wfn_transforms.py:627,638,703,865,952 — docstrings reference nonexistent gflat_to_rchunk; gflat_memory_model.py:304 stale line numbers
evidence: git log -S'def gflat_to_rchunk' shows it existed: added d7eaf1c ('wfn_transforms: gflat_to_rchunk forward helper, Path D §4') then removed in f567aa0 ('isdf_fitting: scan-inside-shard_map z_q_from_psi_sm, Round 6') — absorbed into isdf_fitting's production scan exactly as the first-pass reader hypothesized (gw_init.py:644 comment confirms: 'Round 6 deleted the gflat_to_rchunk_chunk_size cohsex knob — the new z_q_from_psi_sm streaming-scan body...'). Stale mentions also in psi_G_store.py:371 and aot_memory_model/kernels/fit_one_rchunk.py:464. gflat_memory_model.py:304 cites 'gflat_to_rmu._kernel (wfn_transforms.py:611-851)' but _kernel is now at 921-965 and gflat_to_rmu at 711. Pure documentation rot; no code path affected.

item: wfn_transforms.py:121,869,873,1144-1145 — hash(arr.tobytes()) as content identity in jit-cache keys
evidence: Two distinct exposure classes. (a) _GINDEX_DEV_CACHE (lines 121-127): key ('g_index_dev', hash(bytes), shape); a same-shape 64-bit SipHash collision returns a WRONG device buffer whose values feed the gather — silent wrong wavefunctions. (b) _cached_jit keys with baked closures: gflat_to_rmu bakes phase tables phx/phy/phz_rmu_all into the closure (lines 886-898) keyed by kvecs_id/r_mu_id content hashes (869,873); accumulate_rchunk_to_gflat bakes sphere_c and qvec phases (1161-1166) keyed by sphere_id/qvec_id (1143-1145) — collision reuses stale phases/sphere. PYTHONHASHSEED salting is irrelevant intra-process (caches are process-local); risk is only the ~2^-64 same-shape collision, unmonitored. Code self-flags the qvec case as 'a latent correctness hazard' (1136-1142). Replace hash() with hashlib.blake2b digest or key on raw bytes — trivial, closes the acknowledged hazard.

item: wfn_transforms.py:175 — cache_id = ('jax_id', id(g_index), shape) for jax.Array inputs
evidence: The docstring's stated hazard (150-153: stale 'baked-in indices') is itself stale. Since Round 6, gflat_to_rmu threads g_index through shard_map in_specs as a runtime argument (gidx_spec at 913-919; fn(psi_G, g_index_dev_canonical) at 970) — nothing g_index-content-dependent is baked into the cached closure (only shapes, norm, and kvecs/r_mu phase tables keyed separately by content hash). So an id()-reuse-after-GC collision would at worst reuse a shape-compatible compiled kernel and still receive the correct g_index values at call time: no wrong-values path exists at the current call sites (the other documented consumer, accumulate_rchunk_to_gflat, doesn't use _resolve_gindex_dev; its sphere_c is content-hashed at 1145/1161). The id() key is defensive vestige plus a misleading docstring; harmless today, but the docstring should be corrected so a future refactor doesn't reintroduce closure capture believing id() protects it.

item: wfn_transforms.py:221 (jnp.take mode='clip') and :1221 (take_along_axis mode='promise_in_bounds')
evidence: In-bounds is provable from the single in-tree builders. (a) _box_kernel: build_g_index_for_fft_box (gvec_fft_box.py:55,65) initializes g_index to sentinel ngkmax and writes g_locals in [0, ngk[k]); so g in [0, ngkmax]. flat_index = k*(ngkmax+1) + g in [k*(ngkmax+1), k*(ngkmax+1)+ngkmax] ⊂ [0, n_k*(ngkmax+1)) — exactly the padded axis length after the zero-slot concat (wfn_transforms.py:207-208); sentinel gathers the appended zero. clip is a value-level no-op; comment at 216-220 documents the motive (killed 8 _where retrace cache misses, MoS2 3x3). (b) sphere_idx: compute_per_q_bare_coulomb_components pads with sentinel Miller (nx/2,ny/2,nz/2) — 'a valid (non-OOB) flat-FFT index; the corresponding ζ coefficient is zeroed by the writer' (coulomb_sphere.py:133-136, 210, 223). Both index sources have exactly one constructor each, so the 'foreign g_index without sentinel convention' failure mode has no in-tree instance. Documented perf decision resting on an enforced invariant.

item: wfn_transforms.py:934-935, 1205-1206 — scan pad rows clip k_row/q_row to last index
evidence: Per-element math shows the reader's contamination hypothesis is wrong. gflat_to_rmu: row j<N has k=j//nb_local in [0,nk); pad row j>=N gives j//nb_local>=nk, clipped to nk-1 — the clip ONLY sanitizes the g_index_[k_row] and phase-table gathers (940, 954-956) against OOB; it never redirects output. Each chunk writes its samples to out_flat[i0:i0+cs) via dynamic_update_slice_in_dim (958-959) — pad rows land exclusively in slots [N, N+pad_N), which are dropped by out_flat[:N] (963-964). Even hypothetically nonzero pad data could only corrupt rows that get truncated, never the real k=nk-1 rows (disjoint slots, no accumulation across rows). Same structure in accumulate_rchunk_to_gflat: rch_flat/acc_flat pad rows (1184-1186) produce contrib added only into acc rows [i0,i0+cs) (1222-1224), pad region dropped at acc_flat[:N] (1228-1229). Correct by construction: zero-pad is a belt on top of the truncation suspenders, not a load-bearing assumption.

item: wfn_transforms.py:472-478 — to_rchunk_inner docstring: 'Not yet wired into the production fit kernel ... deferred to a follow-up session'
evidence: Stale note, confirmed. isdf_fitting.py:140 imports to_rchunk_inner and :743 calls it inside the production ζ-fit pair pipeline scan body (the exact §4c consumer refactor the docstring says is deferred — rewrite of c_q/z_q with lax.scan inside shard_map, landed in commit f567aa0 'Round 6'). isdf_fitting.py:496 even documents the wiring. Docstring paragraph (Path D scaffolding note dated to reports/zeta_rchunk_memory_model_2026-05-13) should be reduced to the historical pointer; no functional issue.

item: wfn_transforms.py:890 vs 1165; 1274-1275 — Bloch phase sign split (+2πi k·r post-IFFT for ψ, −2πi q·r pre-FFT for ζ)
evidence: Single physical convention, not BGW-vs-physical: ψ_nk(r)=e^{+2πi k·r}u_nk(r) (standard Bloch, applied after IFFT of u's G-coefficients, sign=+1 at 890-892 and apply_bloch_phase default sign=1 at 1264). For ζ the code computes analysis coefficients on the (q+G) sphere: for f(r)=Σ_G c_G e^{2πi(q+G)·r}, c_G = FFT_{r→G}[e^{−2πi q·r} f(r)] — the −2πi at 1164-1166 (and module comment 999-1003) is the mathematically forced conjugate of the same e^{+2πi k·r} convention, on the forward transform instead of the inverse. apply_bloch_phase(sign=±1) at 1259-1296 parameterizes it correctly. One caveat supporting the reader's fragility note: the comment at 1251 ('this is the ONLY place in LORRAX where the Bloch-phase formula lives') is factually violated — inline _ph lambdas at 890-892 and 1164-1166 re-derive the 1D factor tables inside the two scan-kernel builders (for legitimate scratch-size reasons, cf. 762-765), so a future sign audit must check 4 sites, not 1. Sign values themselves verified consistent across all 4.

item: wfn_transforms.py:411, 719 — norm='backward' defaults on to_rmu/gflat_to_rmu
evidence: Confirmed: every production caller of the IFFT family overrides to 'ortho' — load_wfns.py:258,278 (to_rchunk), :489 (gflat_to_rmu), centroid/pivoted_cholesky.py:119 (to_rmu), isdf_fitting.py:746 (to_rchunk_inner). The only production 'backward' is accumulate_rchunk_to_gflat at isdf_fitting.py:2618, passed explicitly and belonging to the forward-FFT ζ writer (its own default, wfn_transforms.py:1015). So the 'legacy default' (docstring 796-799 admits 'centroid-load callers typically pass ortho') protects nothing in-tree and is a silent 1/√N-vs-1/N scale trap for any new caller who omits the kwarg. Making norm keyword-required (or defaulting to 'ortho') would eliminate the trap with a one-line change per signature; no behavior change for existing call sites.

item: wfn_transforms.py:259 — _maybe_constrain unconditionally applies with_sharding_constraint
evidence: Vestige confirmed by history: git show d1552d3 (original P3 commit) and 58d928f both define _maybe_constrain(arr, sharding: NamedSharding | None) with 'if sharding is None: return arr'. The mesh-optional branch was deleted when the module standardized on 'every public transform requires a mesh argument ... no mesh is None branch anywhere downstream' (current comment at 226-228), but the 'maybe' name survived. Zero functional impact — all call sites pass a concrete NamedSharding built by _output_sharding. Rename-or-inline candidate only.

item: src/gw/coulomb/bulk_3d.py — Bulk3D.v_qG
evidence: Enumerated all mechanisms: no `.v_qG(` attribute call anywhere in src/tests/scripts/tools/misc; no string dispatch ('v_qG' as string appears only in __init__.py:13 docstring); console scripts (pyproject.toml:32-35) route to gw.gw_jax:main / centroid.kmeans_cli:main which never touch it; tests import common.coulomb_sphere only; sandbox skills/ and runs/ contain no invocation (runs/ hits are XLA HLO dump artifacts whose metadata points at bulk_3d.py:52 and base.py:127-143 — lines inside q0_average/sample_minibz_qpoints, the alive path, from a lorrax_B checkout). The Bulk3D CLASS is alive via get_kernel().q0_average (gw_jax.py:160 → head_correction.py:134/177 → vcoul.py:190-191), but the v_qG method has zero callers. Live V(q+G) body kernel is gw/compute_vcoul.py (make_v_munu_chunked_kernel, called from gw_init.py:1037).

item: src/gw/coulomb/slab_2d.py — Slab2D.v_qG
evidence: Same exhaustive sweep as Bulk3D.v_qG: zero attribute calls, zero string-dispatch hits, no test/script/skill/run references. item: src/gw/coulomb/slab_2d.py — Slab2D._vq_2d
evidence: Repo-wide grep for _vq_2d hits only its definition at slab_2d.py:40. Within its own class, q0_average (slab_2d.py:50) defines and uses the local closure _vq_sobol (slab_2d.py:70, used at :79 and :86) and never calls self._vq_2d. No external, test, string, or CLI references. Verified the two are algebraically identical at qz=0 (4π·2(1−e^{−zc·kxy}) = 8π(1−e^{−zc·kxy}·cos(0))), so deletion is behavior-preserving.

item: src/gw/coulomb/box_0d.py — Box0D.v_qG
evidence: Same sweep: zero callers. The live 0D body path is compute_vcoul.py sys_dim==0 branch (lines 269-291) via compute_sqrt_vcoul_0d → compute_vcoul_0d.compute_vcoul_box. Even the 0D check script scripts/checks/w_from_eps0_0d_check.py bypasses Box0D.v_qG and calls compute_vcoul_box directly (its _build_v_0d_from_repo, lines 113-119). item: src/gw/coulomb/__init__.py:9-18 — driver-pattern docstring showing kernel.v_qG(...)
evidence: Confirmed aspirational: the documented pattern `v_qG = kernel.v_qG(...)` is performed nowhere; the only live use of the package is vcoul.py:190-191 calling q0_average. The V(q+G) body kernel never migrated out of gw/compute_vcoul.py (√v·phase factory make_v_munu_chunked_kernel, consumed at gw_init.py:1037). Package was created in commit d5b5119 ('per-dimension package'). Either wire the body kernel through v_qG or delete the three dead v_qG methods and fix the docstring — as written it misdirects readers to a nonexistent call path.

item: src/gw/coulomb/base.py:150-152 — bare `except Exception` around Sobol path
evidence: base.py:146-152: the try block covers not just `from scipy.stats import qmc` but the entire 10-rep Sobol loop including wrap_points_to_voronoi and three matmuls; ANY error (shape bug, dtype, device) silently swaps the estimator to single-batch uniform MC with a fixed seed and nsamples bumped to max(nsamples, 2_500_000) — no warning is printed, so a real bug would manifest only as silently different head statistics. The 2_500_000 constant is bit-compat with the legacy uniform sampler (commented minibz_mc, vcoul.py:124 uses (2_500_000, 3)), so the fallback numerics are intentional; the breadth of the except is the defect. Fix: narrow to ImportError on the scipy import + emit a print/warning.

item: src/gw/coulomb/base.py:122-124 — comment about shifts @ bvec.T bug, lazy import of wrap_points_to_voronoi
evidence: Verified the index math: sample points are built as cart = (bvec.T @ U.T).T = U @ bvec (base.py:143), i.e., row i of bvec is reciprocal vector b_i in Cartesian, so a lattice shift n must be n @ bvec — exactly what the production wrap_points_to_voronoi does (vcoul.py:35 `shifts @ bvec_j`). A local reimplementation using shifts @ bvec.T would contract the wrong index (sum_j n_j·bvec[i,j] picks components of each b instead of summing vectors) and wrap to a wrong Voronoi cell. The comment is an accurate battle-scar enforcing single-source-of-truth for the wrap helper; matches the sandbox no-redundancy rule. Keep it.

item: src/gw/coulomb/base.py:155 — fixed jax.random.PRNGKey(0) in uniform fallback
evidence: Deterministic reproducibility, bit-compat with the legacy path: the commented-out historical minibz_mc was invoked as minibz_mc(jax.random.PRNGKey(0), ...) (vcoul.py:142). The Sobol path is likewise deterministic (seed=rep for rep in 0..9, base.py:141). Reproducible mini-BZ averages are desirable when diffing LORRAX vs BGW heads across runs; the ~2.5M-sample MC error is far below the head-correction accuracy target.

item: src/gw/coulomb/bulk_3d.py:24 vs 44-72 — v_qG has 1/Ω, q0_average does not, Protocol docstring claims 1/Ω applied
evidence: Traced the live consumer: q0_average outputs flow to head_correction.py where the volume factor is applied downstream — compute_static_head_terms pref = 1/(Ω_cell·N_k) (head_correction.py:520), PPM sigma_diag R_h/(Ω·Nk) (head_correction.py:699), rank-1 injection inv_V = 1/Ω (head_correction.py:750). Volume-free ⟨8π/q²⟩ heads match the BGW convention (vcoul_generator returns vcoul without 1/Ω; sigma applies the 1/(Ω·Nk) fact), and diagnostics print them as 'a.u.' raw heads (gw_jax.py:336-338). So live behavior is consistent and deliberate. Residual defect is documentation only: the CoulombKernel Protocol docstring (base.py:44-48) says 'outputs are in Rydberg with 1/Ω_cell already applied' without restricting the claim to v_qG — a latent trap if the dead v_qG is ever resurrected or a new q0_average consumer trusts the docstring.

item: src/gw/coulomb/bulk_3d.py:61-68 — epshead fallback: hardcoded q0_crys=(0.001,0,0), wcoul0 from batches[-1] only
evidence: This branch is reached only when S_cart is None AND wcoul0_source='epshead' — a non-default legacy path (default is 's_tensor', gw_config.py:285) explicitly 'kept for back-compat with older runs' (bulk_3d.py:57-59). q0=(0.001,0,0) crystal is the standard BGW epsilon.inp q0 shift at which epshead is computed, so the gamma evaluation point matches how eps0mat.h5 was generated in this sandbox. batches[-1]-only for wcoul0 vs all-rep average for vc0_mean is a transcription artifact of the legacy single-batch minibz_mc (commented vcoul.py:120-142 used one 2.5M batch for both); statistically weaker (√10 larger rep noise on wq mean) but one 2^18-point scrambled-Sobol batch is already converged well below head accuracy. Not worth touching unless the epshead path is revived; note the hardcoded q0 would be wrong for a run whose epsilon used a different q0 shift.

item: src/gw/coulomb/bulk_3d.py:21 — magic zero-threshold denom < 1e-12
evidence: q+G=0 detection in |q+G|² (Bohr⁻²). The smallest physically occurring nonzero |q+G|² is (|b|/N_k)² ~ 1e-2..1e-5 Bohr⁻² for any realistic cell/k-grid — 7+ orders above 1e-12 — while exact q+G=0 gives denom identically 0.0 (integer G plus wrapped q=0 in float64). Same idiom used in the live compute_vcoul.py kernels. Threshold choice is safe and conventional; zeroing the head here matches the package contract that the head is injected separately (base.py:41-43, HeadResolver).

item: src/gw/coulomb/slab_2d.py:29,59 — zc = pi/bvec[2,2] uses only the [2,2] element
evidence: For the standard slab setup (a3 = c·ẑ, a1/a2 in-plane), b3 = (2π/c)ẑ so zc = π/b3z = c/2 — exactly the Ismail-Beigi half-height truncation distance. This mirrors BGW's cell_slab_truncation, which likewise requires the truncation direction along z with the third lattice vector perpendicular to the plane (BGW errors out otherwise; LORRAX doesn't guard, so a monoclinic slab cell would be silently wrong — but the identical zc = π/bvec[2,2] convention is used in the LIVE 2D kernel too, compute_vcoul.py:303, and in the legacy code, so this package is faithfully consistent, not the place the assumption was introduced). An assert on bvec[2,:2]≈0 would be a cheap hardening, but the convention itself is deliberate and BGW-matching.

item: src/gw/coulomb/slab_2d.py:40-48 vs 70-77 — dead _vq_2d (4π×2(1−exp)) beside live _vq_sobol (8π×(1−exp·cos))
evidence: Enumerated the algebra: sampler guarantees qz=0 (base.py:145-146,159-160), so cos(qz·zc)=cos(0)=1 and _vq_sobol = (8π/q²)(1−e^{−zc·kxy}); _vq_2d = (4π/q²)·2·(1−e^{−zc·kxy}) — identical per element for every sample point. Classic parallel old/new duplicate with zero callers of the old one (only definition at slab_2d.py:40). Deleting _vq_2d is behavior-preserving by construction; keeping the cos term in _vq_sobol is documented bit-identity with the legacy minibz_mc (vcoul.py:132 has the same 2·(1−exp·cos) structure). Violates the sandbox no-redundancy rule; delete.

item: src/gw/coulomb/slab_2d.py:92-106 — epshead fallback: 8π outside, q0len>0 guards on hardcoded nonzero q0, batches[-1]
evidence: Verified the 8π placement is algebraically identical to the 3D 8π-inside form, not a convention bug: with ṽ = v/8π, gamma here is computed from the un-prefactored vc_q0 (slab_2d.py:95-99) so gamma = 8π·gamma_full, and 8π·⟨ṽ/(1+ṽ·kxy²·gamma)⟩ = ⟨v/(1+v·kxy²·gamma_full)⟩ — the 8π cancels inside the denominator. It is moreover a line-for-line transcription of the legacy minibz_mc (commented vcoul.py:135-140: identical vc_qtozero, gamma, wq, wcoul0 = 8π·mean(wq)), i.e., proven bit-compat. The jnp.where(q0len>0) and where(vc_q0>0) guards are unreachable defensive cruft (q0 hardcoded to (0.001,0,0) two lines above → q0len>0 always); batches[-1] same benign transcription artifact as bulk_3d. Cosmetic unification with bulk_3d is optional; the numbers are right.

item: src/gw/coulomb/box_0d.py:29,51 — Box0D q0_average divides by cell_volume while Bulk3D/Slab2D q0_average do not
evidence: This is a real convention split with a live wrong-answer path. compute_vcoul_box returns v(G) in the BGW volume-free 8π/|G|² convention (compute_vcoul_0d.py:26-28 'Units: ... v(G) = 8π/|G|² for the untruncated case'), so box_0d.py:51 hands the shared consumers vc0/wcoul0 with 1/Ω pre-applied, while Bulk3D/Slab2D hand them volume-free. The consumers do NOT branch on sys_dim: compute_static_head_terms applies pref = 1/(Ω·Nk) uniformly (head_correction.py:520), apply_q0_head_rank1 applies inv_V = 1/Ω (head_correction.py:750), and gw_jax gates the static head only on config.do_G0 (gw_jax.py:351-354), not sys_dim. So a 0D run through the standard driver gets head shifts a factor Ω too small (double 1/Ω). Mitigating context: (a) the only currently exercised 0D consumer, scripts/checks/w_from_eps0_0d_check.py, uses the values in ratios (w_head/v_g0 with v_g0 also 1/Ω-divided, lines 113-146) where Ω cancels — that script is why the 1/Ω looks deliberate; (b) for 0D the body kernel already includes the finite G=0 element un-zeroed (compute_vcoul.py:270-291, compute_sqrt_vcoul_0d docstring 'The G=0 component is finite'), so injecting a separate static head for 0D double-counts regardless of the volume factor — the whole 0D-through-generic-head-machinery path is incoherent, not just the factor. Also deviates from the BGW reference its own docstring cites (vcoul_generator.f90 wcoul0=vcoul0 in the volume-free convention). Unify to volume-free q0_average outputs + a sys_dim gate on head injection.

item: src/gw/coulomb/box_0d.py:24-30 — v_qG ignores qvec_wrapped, recomputes dense-grid FFT per call, no cache
evidence: Ignoring qvec is physically defensible — cell-box truncation is defined at q=0 only ('Only q=0 is valid', compute_vcoul.py:272; box systems are Γ-only), though silently accepting a nonzero qvec instead of asserting is sloppy. The no-cache recompute contrasts with the live path, which memoizes on cache_key (compute_vcoul.py:231). Both defects are moot: the method is confirmed-dead (zero callers; even the 0D check script calls compute_vcoul_box directly). If the coulomb-package v_qG API is ever wired in per the __init__ docstring, add an assert on qvec≈0 and a cache; until then this is dead weight to delete with the other v_qG methods.

evidence: Repo-wide grep (src/, tests/, tools/, docs/, pyproject.toml) hits only the definition at coulomb_sphere.py:39; zero hits in /pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox/skills and /pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox/runs. item: src/common/coulomb_sphere.py: import itertools (line 34)
evidence: grep -n itertools in the file hits only line 34; no 'itertools.' attribute access anywhere in the 254-line module (full file read). Pure unused import.

item: src/common/coulomb_sphere.py lines 15/133/166/210: sentinel docstring says (nx/2, ny/2, nz/2)
evidence: Index math: np.fft.fftfreq(N)*N for even N=6 is [0,1,2,-3,-2,-1], so flat position N//2=3 holds Miller -N/2 (negative); for odd N=5 position N//2=2 holds +(N-1)/2. Code is self-consistent regardless: lines 214-221 compute sentinel_flat from positions (nx//2, ny//2, nz//2) and read the components triple from the G_int fftfreq table, never from the prose. isdf_fitting.py:2231 and tests/test_per_q_sphere.py:8 state the (-nx/2,-ny/2,-nz/2) form (correct for even axes, the common FFT-grid case), and the test derives the expected sentinel from the fftfreq table itself (test_per_q_sphere.py:77-78). Stale/misleading docstring only; fix the sign in coulomb_sphere prose during refactor, no behavior change.

item: src/common/coulomb_sphere.py lines 49-61: _q_max_cart docstring memorializing the even-kgrid Wigner-Seitz-corner bug
evidence: Verified per-element: line 67 wrap where(qi > K/2, qi-K, qi) is bit-identical to the writer's _bgw_wrap_q (isdf_fitting.py:2198-2201). Even K=4: {0,1,2,3}->{0,1,2,-1}, max |q_frac|=2/4=1/2 (the unwrapped K/2 entry, exactly as the docstring says); odd K=3: {0,1,2}->{0,1,-1}, max 1/3=(K-1)/(2K). The old +-0.5/K corner bound (1/(2K)=0.125 at K=4) badly under-covers 1/2, confirming the memorialized bug. The superset guarantee (lines 22-24, sphere_r=sqrt(cutoff)+|q_max| at line 98) rests on this exact enumeration via triangle inequality |q+G|>=|G|-|q_max|. Load-bearing correctness argument; preserve through refactor.

item: src/common/coulomb_sphere.py lines 192-194: fully broadcast (n_q, n_rtot, 3) float64 host temporaries
evidence: Two (n_q, n_rtot, 3) float64 arrays (qG_frac line 192, qG_cart line 193) plus (n_q, n_rtot) qG_norm2. Host-numpy one-time setup, harmless at current IBZ sizes (e.g. 6 q x 1e6-pt box = ~150 MB each), but scales to multi-GB at large boxes x many q (36 q x 2e6 pts = ~1.7 GB per array). A per-q Python loop already exists immediately below (line 229); moving the mask computation inside it eliminates the n_q factor with zero added complexity. Not a correctness issue.

item: compute_vcoul.py:258 and isdf_fitting.py:2256: function-local imports of common.coulomb_sphere
evidence: Import-cycle hypothesis is FALSE: coulomb_sphere.py imports only numpy (and the dead itertools) - no path back into gw or common heavies. compute_vcoul.py already imports common at module scope (lines 31-32: from common import timing; from common.fft_helpers import make_sharded_fftn_3d), and isdf_fitting.py (itself in common) imports siblings at module top (lines 128-141), so both files' conventions permit a top-level import. The local imports are pure style/locality; lifting to module scope during refactor is safe.

item: src/common/coulomb_sphere.py: bvec argument expects blat-scaled Bohr^-1, no runtime unit check
evidence: Both live call chains pass blat-scaled bvec: gw_init.py:890 builds bvec = np.asarray(wfn.blat * wfn.bvec) feeding make_v_munu_chunked_kernel at compute_vcoul.py:977 and gw_init.py:1037; isdf_fitting.py:2257-2258 scales inline (wfn.blat * wfn.bvec). The per-q docstring documents the unit (line 143: '(3,3) reciprocal lattice rows (Bohr-1)'); compute_bare_coulomb_sphere_idx's docstring (lines 80-88) omits it - worth adding one line. Failure direction of an UNscaled bvec is over-inclusion (|q+G|^2 too small vs the Ry cutoff -> mask admits ~everything -> slow, not wrong), so the silent-wrongness risk is limited to a hypothetical over-scaled caller, which does not exist. blat*bvec is the standard LORRAX convention; an assert has nothing robust to check against (no independent unit carried on the array).

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py lines 159-216: masked-compute SPMD idiom (POTRF/TRSM/SYRK on all blocks, discard via jnp.where)
evidence: Required by shard_map SPMD: k is a traced lax.scan value (line 225), so per-device Python branching is impossible; ownership masks (is_diag_owner line 151, is_col_k_owner line 152) must be data-dependent. Verified NaN containment case-by-case: (1) non-owner devices compute jnp.linalg.cholesky of an unrelated/partially-updated block (line 160) which can be non-PD -> NaN, but line 166 'Lkk_contrib = jnp.where(is_diag_owner, Lkk_local, zeros)' discards it BEFORE the psum at line 167, so the psum'd Lkk = owner value + exact zeros; (2) TRSM result Lik (line 173) discarded by where at 174-176 when should_update is False; (3) SYRK update discarded at 214-216 unless i_glob>k, j_glob>k, i_glob>=j_glob. Numerically correct; confirmed jax_debug_nans-hostile (transient NaNs by design). The waste claim is also verified: do_syrk_col/do_syrk_row (lines 201-220) run full J_row x J_col block-GEMMs at every k instead of only the trailing (J-k)^2 submatrix, ~3x extra SYRK flops summed over k — a real inefficiency but inherent to fixed-shape fori_loop under scan; refactor would need masked-slice gymnastics for modest gain.

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py line 186: lax.pcast(..., ('x','y'), to='varying') VMA annotation
evidence: The pcast is spec-required: fill_panel (lines 189-195) writes per-device-conditionally (should_contrib depends on axis_index via is_col_k_owner/my_row_start), so the fori_loop carry must be device-varying; JAX 0.9 CPU enforces this strictly per the comment at lines 182-185. The claimed caller-side-only 1x1 workaround is confirmed: isdf_fitting.py factor_c_q has an explicit 1x1-mesh dense-Cholesky fallback whose comment quotes the exact failure ('scan body function carry input and carry output must have equal types ... varying manual axes do not match'), and tests/test_padding.py:287,351 document the kernel not lowering on the JAX 0.9 host-platform runtime. So the contract gap is real but deliberately owned by the sole production caller (factor_c_q) and documented in three places; fragile against JAX upgrades but not a bug today.

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py lines 64-68 dense_to_tiles upper-tile zeroing + dead call sites 317, 364
evidence: Index math done both ways. LIVE site is safe: isdf_fitting.py:1150 tiles the full Hermitian C_q and the kernel provably reads only tile (i,j) with i>=j — POTRF reads (k,k) (line 159), TRSM reads (i,k) for i_glob>k (line 173), fill_panel reads (i,k) for i_glob>=k (line 191-192), SYRK writes only i_glob>=j_glob (lines 210-213) — so zeroing tiles i<j is harmless there. DEAD sites are latently wrong: solve_triangular_2d line 317 and cholesky_solve_2d line 364 pass a GENERAL dense solution X/zeta through dense_to_tiles, whose mask 'i_idx >= j_idx' (line 66) silently zeroes every element X[mu,nu] with block-row(mu) < block-col(nu). Any resurrection of these paths returns a block-lower-triangular truncation of the answer with no error — the line 316 comment ('though result may not be triangular') acknowledges the shape mismatch yet zeroes anyway. Real correctness trap, currently confined to confirmed-dead code.

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py lines 141-143 right_solve_LH conj/transpose dance
evidence: Algebraically verified element-wise, not pattern-matched. Goal: X L^H = B. Code: Y = solve_triangular(L.conj(), B.T, lower=True, trans='N'), return Y.T. Then Y = (L*)^{-1} B^T, so X = Y^T = B ((L*)^T)^{-1} = B (L^H)^{-1}, hence X L^H = B exactly. The plain .T (no conj) on the result is correct precisely because the conjugation was applied to L rather than B; L* remains lower-triangular so lower=True is valid. Equivalent to the more conventional solve_triangular(L, B^H, trans='C')^H form. Not a BGW-vs-physical sign convention — pure linear-algebra identity, correct.

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py lines 186-198: full (J,b,b) panel replicated on every device via psum each column step
evidence: Verified over-replication with index math: after psum over ('x','y') (line 198), all P devices hold the full n x b panel, but device (px,py) only ever reads panel[j_glob] for j_glob in its J_col column range (line 203) and panel[i_glob] for i_glob in its J_row row range (line 207) — i.e. (J_row + J_col) blocks of J, an overfactor of Pr*Pc/(Pr+Pc) ~ sqrt(P)/2. A true 2D algorithm broadcasts the column panel only along mesh rows and the transposed row panel only along mesh columns, which also means the docstring's 'sqrt(P) factor less bandwidth than 1D' (line 12) is NOT realized by this both-axes psum (per-device traffic per step = full panel, same as 1D). Absolute size is small (J*b^2*16 B, ~10 MB at n=10k/b=64), so no OOM risk — but under the sandbox's zero-replicated-intermediates principle this is a replicated buffer plus redundant bandwidth: refactor-target, not a correctness bug.

item: src/common/cholesky_2d.py lines 27-29, 235, 291: perf folklore docstrings + never-done distributed-TRSM TODO
evidence: Checked the arithmetic: '1.6 GB/device' for the reshard strategy is plausible (n=10k c128 full matrix = 1.6 GB replicated), but '5 MB' understates the 2D-blocked per-device footprint — local tiles alone are n^2*16/P ~ 12.5 MB at P=128, plus the ~10 MB replicated panel per step — so the headline comparison is directionally right, quantitatively stale. The '~18x speedup' (line 235) is unverifiable from source (no benchmark in-tree; tests/archive/benchmark_cholesky_sharding.py benchmarks a different 1D implementation from test_blocked_cholesky.py). TODO line 291 confirmed never implemented; the gap is genuinely papered over elsewhere: production takes the ffi.cusolvermp potrf+potrs path (isdf_fitting.py:1100-1105) or the 1x1 dense fallback, and the functions containing the TODO are confirmed-dead. Docs drift with zero runtime impact; fix by deleting the dead functions and their claims together.

item: noncrossing_grids/noncrossing_imag_grids/crossing_grids silent return of last failing (t,w) at N_max exhaustion; unbound-variable crash if the range is empty (minimax.py:216, 571, 814)

For eps_q=1e-3 hgl, tau_max≈3.717, so any A_dim > ~427 (= 505*pi/3.717; A_dim = E_bw/xi_0, plausible for wide bandwidth / small broadening) gives N_lo > N_max, the for-loop body never executes, and line 814 raises UnboundLocalError on tau instead of a clean 'window too wide' error. noncrossing_grids is safe in practice (caller hardcodes N_start=2, N_max=max(4,..) at minimax_screening.py:477, 582).

item: try/except Exception: pass around warm-start solve (minimax.py:349-353, _nc_remez_at_R)
evidence: Confirmed at lines 349-353: wraps _nc_solve_at_R(N, R, s, lawson_iter=0) used only to polish the Remez initial s; on any failure it falls back to the unpolished sorted s_init, after which lines 355-357 compute LS weights and the error curve regardless — so semantically a deliberate warm-start fallback, though except Exception is over-broad (would mask e.g. scipy signature errors). item: undocumented 1e-12 L1 penalty on split weights in _cr_minimax_lp objective (minimax.py:648-651)
evidence: This IS live code (crossing_grids:809 -> solve_crossing:782,767 -> _cr_lp_backward_elim:690,701,711 and _cr_final_lp_weights:721 -> _cr_minimax_lp). Explicit index math: variables z = [t, w+ (K), w- (K)], all >=0 (line 661); constraints rows 0..M-1: -t + Phi(w+ - w-) <= g and rows M..2M-1: -t - Phi(w+ - w-) <= -g, i.e. |g - Phi w| <= t with w = w+ - w-. Without the penalty, (w+ + c, w- + c) for any c >= 0 leaves w, all constraints, and the objective t unchanged — the optimal set is a degenerate ray, and highs-ipm converges to an interior point of that face (both w+ and w- large, cancellation-prone). c[1:K+1] = c[K+1:] = 1e-12 adds 1e-12*sum(w+ + w-) = 1e-12*||w||_1 at the optimum, selecting the min-L1 representative (elementwise min(w+,w-) = 0). Magnitude check: K <= 500 (line 682), weights O(1) => penalty <= ~5e-10, negligible vs t_opt at target errors >= 1e-8..1e-6 used by the pipeline, and it stabilizes the argmin|w_S| drop rule in backward elimination (line 707). Standard degeneracy-breaking trick; deserves a one-line comment but is deliberate and correct.

item: magic slack factors: 1.3*tau_max candidate grid (679), 1.5*tau_max clip (742), 1.1*tau_max init (786), _CR_TAU_MAX*1.5 (883, 907), u_min=5.0 (867, 938)
evidence: Split live vs dead. Live (crossing path): line 679 tau_upper = tau_max_val*1.3 bounds the LP candidate frequency grid; line 742 tau_hi = tau_max_val*1.5 is the VarPro-LM clip bound; line 786 linspace(0.1, tau_max_val*1.1, N) is one of three inits. All three only define optimizer search regions slightly beyond the regularization weight's effective support tau_max(eps_q) (tau_max_hgl/fermi, lines 58-65); correctness is insensitive to the exact values because solve_crossing measures the true L-infinity error on the dense target grid afterwards (_eval_err, lines 744-745, applied at 767-773) and crossing_grids only accepts err < eps (line 811). Hand-tuned but safety-gated hyperparameters, not silent physics constants. Dead: _CR_TAU_MAX*1.5 (883, 907) and u_min=5.0 (867 default, 938 hardcode) live only inside _cr_solve_1overx/build_crossing_quadrature, confirmed-dead above.

item: screening.py:161,166 — chi0.block_until_ready() + del chi0 + W.block_until_ready() inside the request loop
evidence: src/gw/screening.py:159-167. Sequence: block on chi0 (forces compute_chi0 done, its internals freed) -> dispatch solve_w -> del chi0 (drops last Python ref so buffer frees when solve_w's consume finishes) -> block on W (next loop iteration's compute_chi0 cannot overlap the W solve). Net effect: at most one (nq,mu,mu) chi0 alive at a time across the multi-frequency loop, matching the suspect's peak-memory hypothesis. Consistent with the repo's memory discipline elsewhere (gw_jax.py:357 'import gc; gc.collect()' before sigma; ppm_sigma.py:531 donate_argnums for W_q). No inline comment, but requests are <=2 today (static+probe, screening.py:86-94) so lost overlap is negligible vs one chi0 of savings. Not a bug; deliberate serialization.

item: screening.py:144-149 — imag-vs-real axis chosen by nonzero component of omega_ry; mixed complex raises ValueError
evidence: src/gw/screening.py:144-157 plus request construction at :86-94: the only producers emit omega_ry = 0+0j (static, handled separately at :140), 1j*omega_p (GN-PPM), or complex(omega_p, 0) (HL-PPM) — pure-axis by construction, matching ScreeningRequest docstring :51-58 ('Pure real ... pure imaginary ... Both branches are supported'). Mixed complex fails LOUDLY with an explicit message, not silently — the correct behavior until a complex-contour scheme exists (module docstring :25-28 anticipates future roles). A landmine would be a silent wrong-branch pick; this is a guarded, documented two-branch dispatch.

item: gw_jax.py:417 — driver re-filters screening requests with r.role != 'static'
evidence: src/gw/gw_jax.py:417-418: `_requests = [r for r in screening_requests_for(mode, config) if r.role != 'static']   # static W already solved above`. The static W_q is indeed solved earlier at gw_jax.py:261-292 (solve_w / unfold_v_q, or W_q=V_q unscreened) because COHSEX and the head machinery need it before the PPM block. Contrast: sc_iteration.py:281-287 passes the unfiltered list and re-solves static every iteration (correct — wfns_qp change each iteration). The filter is correct today, but it duplicates knowledge of the 'static' role string in the driver and assumes the earlier W_q is identical to what compute_screening would produce for role='static'. Cleaner: pass the pre-solved W into compute_screening as a seeded {'static': W_q} so the role knowledge stays in screening.py. Correct as-is; mild abstraction leak worth folding in on the next touch.

item: gw_jax.py:511 — _protected = _in_range hardcoded, collapsing three-way partition to two-way
evidence: Confirmed at src/gw/gw_jax.py (~line 510-511): `# Default protected = in-range: ...` / `_protected = _in_range`, then BandPartition(protected_mask=_protected, in_range_mask=_in_range). Grep for any config knob feeding protected_mask: none — gw_config.py has no 'protected' field; band_partition.py:52 docstring says 'user-configured BandPartition' but no plumbing exists. The middle category (protected=False, in_range=True → keep H_full diag, zero off-diag) IS reachable and verified in tests/test_band_partition.py:51-76 (protected=[F,T,T,F,T,F], in_range all True) and via direct SCInputs construction, so the machinery is live and tested — but driver-unreachable. This is dead flexibility from the production path's perspective: either plumb a user knob (e.g. protected band range in cohsex.in) or document why the default is the only supported mode. Note also the docstring table row 'Non-protected in-range: Diagonal Σ at actual band energy' (band_partition.py:12) is not literally what apply_band_partition does — it keeps H_full's diagonal, whatever energy Σ was evaluated at upstream; harmless while the category is driver-unreachable, misleading if a knob is ever added.

item: band_partition.py:147-161 — masking via float multiplication with eye/mask outer products instead of jnp.where
evidence: Per-element math, src/gw/band_partition.py:144-161: p[m]=protected_mask[m] as 0.0/1.0; offdiag_keep[m,n]=p[m]*p[n]; offdiag_part[k,m,n]=H[k,m,n]*(1-delta_mn)*p[m]*p[n]; diag_kept[k,n]=in_range[n]?H[k,n,n]:scissor[k,n] (this half DOES use jnp.where, :155-158); out[k,m,n]=offdiag_part[k,m,n]+diag_kept[k,m]*delta_mn. Case m=n: (1-delta)=0 kills the first term -> out=diag_kept[k,n]. Case m!=n: out=H*p[m]*p[n], i.e. kept iff both protected. Identity case p=1,in_range=1: H*(1-delta)+diag(H)*delta=H exactly (multiplication by exact 0.0/1.0 introduces no rounding). Matches the documented spec and tests/test_band_partition.py:38-98 covers identity, off-diag masking, and scissor override. A boolean-mask jnp.where formulation would be equally jit-able and clearer, but the code is correct and small. Style nit only.

item: degen_average.py:62-70 — grouping via adjacent-difference assumes per-k ascending energies
evidence: src/gw/degen_average.py:62-70 groups bands where |e[k,i]-e[k,i-1]|<tol, contiguously. The module docstring :3-5 says it mirrors BerkeleyGW Sigma/shiftenergy.f90:86-122, which uses the same contiguous adjacent-comparison walk over band index — the sorted-input assumption is inherited from BGW, deliberately, for bitwise-matching group boundaries. Both in-repo callers (gw_jax.py:384, :724-740) pass DFT eigenvalues straight from the QE wfn (ascending per k by construction). Non-monotonic input would fragment groups silently, so the suspect's refactor-hazard note stands as a caveat, but as written this is a faithful BGW-convention port, not an accident. A cheap hardening (assert per-k monotonicity) would cost one line if energies ever come from a rotated/QP basis.

item: greens_function_kernel.py:5 — psi_yr conjugated INSIDE build_G
evidence: Documented in three places. (1) greens_function_kernel.py:1-7 module docstring states the formula G_munu = sum_ij psi_i(mu) W_ij psi*_j(nu) and 'psi_yr is conjugated (nu side). This matches the tested COHSEX convention throughout gw_jax.py.' (2) The bispinor code doesn't merely warn — it derives the consequence and satisfies its precondition: sigma_x_bispinor.py:77-83 shows folding gamma^nu into psi_yr BEFORE build_G yields conj(gamma^nu psi) = (gamma^nu)^T psi*, which contracts to the correct Hermitian sandwich psi† gamma^nu psi PROVIDED gamma^nu is Hermitian, and notes gamma^0=I and gamma^i=alpha^i both qualify. (3) All four einsum branches (:21-31) conjugate the same operand, so the convention is uniform. It is a convention with a stated precondition, checked where it matters — not a landmine. The real hazard would be folding a non-Hermitian operator into psi_yr; the docstring names that requirement explicitly.

item: w_isdf.py:122-124 — Hermitian-swap jnp.conj(G) at the chi0 call site, not inside build_G_tau
evidence: The conj is one leg of a documented sharding optimization, derived in the block comment at src/gw/w_isdf.py:60-80: using per-k Hermiticity G_k(mu,nu)=G_k(nu,mu)*, running Gv's k->R transform with the SAME fftn sign as Gc's makes Gv_R equal conj(original Gv_R) with (mu_m,mu_n) swapped into Gc-natural order, collapsing the chi0 einsum to an elementwise product with identical index order on both operands — eliminating two resharding steps ('Verified to machine precision against the original formulation', :74-75). The conj at :122-124 carries its own comment pointing back to that block and stating it 'belongs at the call site, NOT inside build_G_tau' — correct, because build_G_tau's OTHER caller is the Sigma_c path (ppm_sigma.py:548), which uses G_k unconjugated with a different FFT chain (_G_ifftn then _G_fftn, ppm_sigma.py:551-553). Moving the conj into build_G_tau would silently corrupt Sigma. The coupling is real but explicitly fenced with comments on both sides; this is deliberate placement, not cruft.

item: greens_function_kernel.py — no dedicated unit test
evidence: Confirmed: grep 'build_G_tau|greens_function|build_G(' over tests/ = zero hits (tests/ listing has no test_greens*; test_gw_jax_regression.py and test_sigma_x_bispinor.py exercise it only through full pipelines). Given this kernel is the convention chokepoint for three delicate contracts verified above — internal conj of psi_yr (bispinor Hermitian-gamma precondition), the phases sign in build_G_tau (t=-tau/e_ref=vmax vs t=+tau/e_ref=cmin at w_isdf.py:114-121), and the caller-side Hermitian-swap conj — a direct unit test (dense reference G = psi diag(phase) psi† compared per element, plus the Gij branch) is the cheapest guard against exactly the refactor hazards flagged in the other findings. Test gap; code itself verified consistent.

item: gw_config.py:782-783 — crossing_max_nodes=max(500, sigma_max_nodes), crossing_eps_q=1.0e-3 hardcoded at construction
evidence: Suspect's 'shadowing identical defaults' is only half right. crossing_eps_q=1.0e-3 (gw_config.py:783) does duplicate the dataclass default (minimax_config.py:29) — redundant kwarg. But crossing_max_nodes=max(500, self.ppm.sigma_max_nodes) (gw_config.py:782) is NOT the default: it deliberately raises the crossing-quadrature node cap when the user sets sigma_max_nodes>500 (default 500 at minimax_config.py:28 would silently cap below the sigma budget). Both values are genuinely consumed: ppm_sigma.py:1518-1519 reads them from sigma_window_quad and threads them into the pole-crossing quadrature (:856-857 max_nodes/eps_q). Note a third copy of the magic pair at ppm_sigma.py:1523 (`crossing_max_nodes, crossing_eps_q = 500, 1e-3`) as the sigma_window_quad-is-None fallback — the constants live in three places. No user knob for crossing_eps_q exists (grep of gw_config input parsing: none), consistent with deferred exposure. Correct behavior; minor duplication a future touch could consolidate to the dataclass defaults.

item: gw/__init__.py:6 — import-time os.environ.setdefault('JAX_ENABLE_X64','1')
evidence: src/gw/__init__.py:4-6, with comment 'Ensure double precision is enabled before any module in this package imports JAX.' Three mitigations blunt the ordering foot-gun: (1) it is setdefault, not an overwrite — an explicit user env setting wins; (2) every real entry point ALSO hard-sets the config after import: gw_jax.py:11 jax.config.update('jax_enable_x64', True), bse/bse_jax.py:10+42 (both patterns), centroid/kmeans_cli.py:9 ('Must precede any jax import so JAX_ENABLE_X64 etc. take effect'), bandstructure/htransform.py:6, plus ~10 more config.update sites in src/bse — so even if some other package imports jax before gw, the drivers still flip x64 explicitly; (3) it is a repo-wide established pattern, not a one-off. Residual risk is only for a hypothetical library consumer who imports a gw submodule directly (bypassing __init__? not possible — package __init__ always runs first) after jax is initialized WITHOUT going through a driver — and even then jax.config.update in the driver modules covers the shipped entry points. Correct and conventional here; the ordering sensitivity is inherent to JAX_ENABLE_X64 itself, not to this line.
