item: src/ffi/phdf5/read.py — ffi_read_kchunk_call + C++ target lorrax_phdf5_read_kchunk
evidence: ffi_read_kchunk_call (read.py:128) has exactly one caller, the dead read_kchunk_sharded (read.py:293); nothing else in the repo dispatches the string 'lorrax_phdf5_read_kchunk' (only the definition read.py:60 and the handler registration ffi_loader.py:50). The C++ chain is self-contained: ReadKchunkDispatch (cpp/read_ffi.cc:413) + ReadKchunkImpl (~:317-407) implement the sequential-H5Dread path, while the union handler ReadKchunkUnionDispatch (:773, registered :988-989) has its own separate H5S_SELECT_OR implementation (:563-700) — I verified the union path does not call ReadKchunkImpl. Dead down through the C++ handler as claimed; only shared statics (unravel_rank, dtype switch scaffolding) must survive deletion. Registration at ffi_loader.py:49-51 would also need the line-50 entry dropped.

item: src/ffi/phdf5/read.py:348 — lru_cache(maxsize=None) keyed on raw int fh, ds_id baked as compile-time Attr, no invalidation from close_file
evidence: All three sub-claims verified: (1) _read_kchunk_union_sharded_cached (read.py:348-349) is keyed on fh:int — the raw C++ PhdfCtx address per context.py:7 — plus ds_name/mesh/specs; (2) ds_id and ctx_handle are baked as compile-time FFI Attrs inside the closure (read.py:425); (3) close_file (context.py:80-94) touches only _FILE_CTXS, never this cache, and maxsize=None pins Mesh objects forever. Mitigations that keep it latent rather than live: a reopened file gets a fresh malloc'd ctx (new fh → new cache key, so growth not corruption in the common case), and C++ guards ds_id with H5Iis_valid (cpp/read_ffi.cc:80,344). But if glibc recycles the freed PhdfCtx address for a new ctx, the stale closure's ctx_handle deref hits the WRONG live context before any check can fire (and HDF5 hid_t values are also recycled, so H5Iis_valid is not airtight) — silent wrong-file reads are possible in principle. Same deliberate identity-stability pattern exists at _slab_io_ffi.py:280 (_get_read_sm/_get_write_sm) with an explicit rationale comment ('identity-stable via lru_cache so JAX's trace cache hits'), so the caching itself is intentional; the missing close_file→cache invalidation hook is the refactor.

item: src/ffi/phdf5/read.py:74-78 — low-level ffi package lazy-imports the sharding-axis encoder from file_io._slab_io_ffi
evidence: Confirmed inversion at read.py:67-78: _encode_sharding_axes does 'from file_io._slab_io_ffi import _sharding_to_axis_info' with the stated justification 'exactly one source of truth' and a lazy import excused by 'file_io may not be on sys.path in some minimal test harnesses'. Meanwhile file_io._slab_io_ffi itself lazy-imports back from ffi.phdf5 (read/write/context) at _slab_io_ffi.py:286,311,337 — a two-way dependency held together only by lazy imports. The encoder output feeds the C++ attr encoding (axis_count_per_dim/axis_flat consumed at cpp/read_ffi.cc:477-500), i.e., it is intrinsically part of the FFI wire format, so it belongs in src/ffi (e.g. ffi/phdf5 or ffi/common), with file_io importing downward. Single-source-of-truth intent is right (matches the no-redundancy house rule); the direction is wrong.

item: src/ffi/phdf5/read.py:362-372 — union-read preconditions (pairwise-disjoint windows, ascending row-major sort) docstring-only
evidence: Confirmed: the two preconditions live only in the docstring (read.py:366-372); neither the Python wrapper nor ReadKchunkUnionDispatch validates ordering/disjointness, and HDF5's SELECT_OR union hands back elements in file order, so a violation garbles data silently rather than erroring — the reader's failure hypothesis is correct. The sole caller satisfies both by construction: wfn_loader.py builds ibz_unique_sorted = np.unique(...) (ascending + deduplicated) and position_in_reads via np.searchsorted, with per-k counts clamped by _build_phdf5_clamped_counts (covered by tests/test_wfn_loader_phdf5_clamp.py). Cheap enforcement is available: the C++ union handler already D2H-copies the full offset/count tables, so an ascending + non-overlap scan over (n_kchunk, ndim) ints costs microseconds against a multi-GB H5Dread. A silent-corruption precondition with a nearly-free check is a refactor-target, not acceptable convention.

item: src/ffi/phdf5/read.py:117 — reads have no has_side_effect/token, unlike writes
evidence: Duplicate angle of the write.py:60-61 finding, verified from the read side: ffi_read_call (read.py:117), ffi_read_kchunk_call (:143) and ffi_read_kchunk_union_call (:174) all omit has_side_effect — reads are modeled as pure, so consumers are ordered by data dependence; DCE of an unused read and CSE of identical reads are both semantically harmless. Cross-dispatch read-after-write is sequenced in Python by design: _FfiBackend's FIFO worker calls block_until_ready per write task and drains the queue before close (_slab_io_ffi.py init comment block), and production flows read (WFN.h5, mode 'r') and write (zeta/eps outputs, mode 'w') different files. Consistent, deliberate asymmetry matching the C++ side (write returns ffi::Future, cpp/write_ffi.cc:22-36; plain reads return ffi::Error).

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py: smoke_allreduce_sum (L265)
evidence: Zero live references via any mechanism. Full-repo grep (py/sh/md/toml/cc/h): only the def (ffi_loader.py:265), its own argtype decls (L124-127), and the C implementation (src/ffi/common/cpp/api.cc:104, src/ffi/cusolvermp/cpp/context.cc:405). Enumerated all `ffi_loader.<attr>` accesses across src — smoke_allreduce_sum absent; no from-import, no getattr dispatch, no `import *` anywhere in src, not in pyproject [project.scripts] (only gw_jax/lorrax-gw/lorrax-centroids), zero hits in tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/, misc/, config/, and zero hits in sandbox skills/, runs/, tools/ (only dnn_version_info noise in xla_dump pbtxt files). The underlying C symbol lrx_smoke_allreduce_sum also has no other Python caller (no direct get_lib().lrx_smoke_allreduce_sum outside ffi_loader). Diagnostic NCCL smoke test from the 2026-04-16 WIP scaffolding commit 7474086.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py: version_info (L270)
evidence: Same enumeration as smoke_allreduce_sum: only the def (ffi_loader.py:270), argtype decls (L129-134), and C impl (api.cc:112). No `ffi_loader.version_info` attribute access anywhere (full enumeration of module-attr uses: create/destroy contexts, fill_nccl_unique_id, nccl_unique_id_bytes, get_lib, phdf5_* only), no from-import, no string dispatch, no CLI entry, no test/tool/doc/sandbox reference. Underlying lrx_version_info has no direct get_lib() caller either.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py: slate_init_mpi wrapper (L407)
evidence: The Python wrapper has zero callers. All 5 sites that need this functionality bypass it via get_lib().lrx_slate_init_mpi(): src/common/slate_cholesky_trsm_test.py:37, slate_trsm_isolated_test.py:29, slate_eigh_test.py:36, slate_chol_trsm_bench.py:37, slate_batched_test.py:39 — all test/bench files, so even the underlying C entry point is test-only. Production slate paths never need it: create_slate_context's contract (ffi_loader.py:368-370) and slate/cpp/context.cc:177 area show lrx_slate_context_create inits MPI_THREAD_MULTIPLE itself; src/ffi/slate/context.py:10 documents exactly this. No from-import, no ffi_loader.slate_init_mpi attr access, no sandbox/docs usage (phdf5/ARCHITECTURE.md:219 mentions the C symbol, not the wrapper). Note: deleting the wrapper should also consider L191-192 argtype decls, which stay live for the direct get_lib() test callers.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py L12-14: docstring claims handlers 'EighF64, EighC128'
evidence: Confirmed stale. Actual registered symbols in _FFI_TARGET_SYMBOLS (L38-57) are EighMpFfi, CusolverMpBatchedPotrfFfi, SlateEighFfi, etc., with an explicit comment at L39-40: 'One handler per routine covers all supported dtypes — dispatch is done inside the .so'. Git history: 'EighF64' dates to the initial WIP commit 7474086 (2026-04-16); the same-day refactor 8b9c041 ('cuSOLVERMp refactor: jaxlib-style 3-layer handler') moved to one-handler-per-routine but left the module docstring untouched. Doc-only defect; the parenthetical example is illustrative and nothing parses it. One-line doc fix.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py L204-206: _register_ffi_targets swallows exceptions matching 'already registered'
evidence: Functional today but fragile by construction. Verified the string exists in this checkout's jaxlib: strings on .venv/.../jaxlib/libjax_common.so contains "' is already registered." (XLA custom-call registry error), so the substring match catches the intended case. Failure mode if JAX rewords is LOUD (the except re-raises the original), not silent — so not a bug. Also note the guard is nearly unreachable: get_lib() caches _LIB (L211-213) so registration runs once per process; the except only fires if the module is imported under two names (plausible here: callers use both 'from ffi.common...' and 'from ..common...' paths) or another component registers the same target. Better: a module-level _REGISTERED flag, or tolerate duplicates explicitly. Low priority.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py L311-319: _DTYPE_TAG hardcoded ints 'matching xla::ffi::DataType'
evidence: Two of the reader's three claims are wrong, but the surviving issue is real. (1) There IS a shared C header: src/ffi/phdf5/cpp/phdf5_interface.h:25-32 defines enum Tag {kF32=1, kF64=2, kS32=3, kS64=4, kC64=5, kC128=6} — element-by-element identical to the Python dict {float32:1, float64:2, int32:3, int64:4, complex64:5, complex128:6}, so the Python↔C contract is consistent today (Python dict is an unchecked duplicate of it, though). (2) The 'mirror xla::ffi::DataType' comment is FALSE in both files: real XLA_FFI_DataType values are S32=4, S64=5, F32=11, F64=12, C64=15, C128=18 — only S32/S64 accidentally... no, not even those (LORRAX kS32=3 vs XLA S32=4). (3) No code path converts an actual xla::ffi::DataType into a Tag: write_ffi.cc:401+ switches on A.element_type() (real ffi::DataType cases) directly and never calls h5_native_for_tag; h5_native_for_tag is consumed only in context.cc:357 with the Python-supplied tag. So the mismatch is inert. The hazard: the wrong comment invites someone to 'align' one side with XLA's true constants, which would silently remap dtypes (e.g., tag 4 = LORRAX int64 vs XLA int32) and corrupt HDF5 datasets with no error for same-size types. Fix the comment on both sides; ideally generate the Python dict from the header or add a startup assertion.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py L33: __all__=['get_lib'] vs ~15 public wrappers
evidence: Stale but harmless. __all__ only affects star-imports and doc tooling; grep confirms zero 'import *' in src/, and all consumers use explicit named imports (e.g., src/gw/gw_driver_helpers.py:156 'from ffi.common.ffi_loader import phdf5_init_mpi'; wfn_loader.py:284, _slab_io_ffi.py:338 use module-attr access) — none of which __all__ restricts. __all__ dates from the initial commit before the 'Thin Pythonic helpers' section (L222+) grew. Update or delete it; no runtime consequence either way.

item: src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py L215 RTLD_GLOBAL + dual error protocols (rc+err_buf vs handle==0+err_buf)
evidence: Both halves check out as deliberate. RTLD_GLOBAL has been there since the first FFI commit (7474086, verified via git log -S) and has in-repo precedent: src/runtime/aot_memory.py:327 loads libcufft with RTLD_GLOBAL too; global visibility is the standard requirement for a .so that drags in MPI/NCCL (Cray MPICH resolves plugin/transport symbols from the global namespace — cf. the phdf5-on-Cray-MPICH stack this repo runs on). Dual protocols: cusolvermp/phdf5 wrappers use rc:int + out-param + err_buf checked by _check_err (L225-228; restype c_int at L119/145/160/168), while slate context creators return the handle directly with 0=failure (restype c_int64 at L179/L188, checked at L377-380 and L396-399). Git dates confirm the reader's hypothesis: cusolvermp protocol 2026-04-16 (7474086), slate 2026-04-20 (259352e, 'slate FFI scaffold'). Each Python wrapper exactly matches its C signature (api.cc / slate/cpp/context.cc), both protocols carry the same err_buf diagnostics, and the handle-return style is a natural fit for constructors. Cosmetic inconsistency only; unifying would touch working C ABI for zero behavior change.

item: src/ffi/common/broadcast.py L58: imports jax._src.distributed.global_state
evidence: Necessary private-API use, explicitly motivated in the module docstring (L7-12): the public alternative jax.experimental.multihost_utils.broadcast_one_to_all silently promotes uint8→uint64 under jax_enable_x64, scrambling opaque byte payloads like ncclUniqueId. Checked the installed jax (.venv): jax/distributed.py exposes no public global_state or KV-client accessor, so jax._src.distributed.global_state.client is the only route — the same pattern used ecosystem-wide (orbax, maxtext). Real but accepted fragility: breaks only on a JAX internal reorg, at import time (loud). Revisit if/when JAX ships a public KV accessor.

item: src/ffi/common/broadcast.py L61-63: hex doubling + set-once KV keys
evidence: Both concerns verified negligible/contained in the only production usage. Hex doubling: payload is one ncclUniqueId (128 B → 256 B string) per (p,q,layout) per job — irrelevant. Stale-key risk: sole caller is src/ffi/cusolvermp/context.py:83-85 with key 'lorrax_ffi/cusolvermp/nccl_unique_id/v0/{p}x{q}/{col|row}'; the per-process _CACHE + _LOCK in get_or_init_context (context.py:104-111) guarantees _make_ctx — hence the KV set — runs at most once per key per process, and contexts are only destroyed at atexit (L116-127), so a same-key/different-payload reuse cannot occur in current code. The KV store's lifetime is one jax.distributed session, so there is no cross-job staleness. The '/v0' suffix is indeed the manual versioning affordance, and the broadcast_bytes docstring (L42-44) pushes key-uniqueness onto callers explicitly. Fine as designed for small opaque handles; only worth revisiting if broadcast_bytes gains callers that rebroadcast under one key.

item: sigma_output.write_chunked_complex_dataset_h5
evidence: Whole-repo grep (src/tests/tools/scripts/docs, *.py/*.sh/*.md/*.toml/*.yaml) plus lorrax_sandbox/skills and lorrax_sandbox/runs: only the def (src/file_io/sigma_output.py:206) and the re-export (src/file_io/__init__.py:38). No `import *` anywhere, no getattr/globals() string dispatch on file_io names, not reachable from pyproject [project.scripts] (lorrax-gw/gw_jax/lorrax-centroids only), no docs usage. Zero live references.

item: tagged_arrays.save_restart_state_per_proc output
evidence: The FUNCTION is alive (called every chunked-ISDF run, src/gw/gw_init.py:1302) but its output is write-only: the {prefix}.rank{r}.x{cx}.y{cy}.h5 files' datasets (V_local, psi_full_local, V0_noG0_local, S_local) have zero readers in src/tests/tools/scripts/docs and in sandbox skills/runs (the only runs/ hits are compile.log and MLIR dumps, i.e. traces of the writer executing, not readers). The restart LOAD path (gw_init.py:1308-1311) reads the SlabIO tensors file via load_restart_state_from_h5 instead. Corroborating: `del meta` at tagged_arrays.py:280, and the sole call site passes S_qmunu=None and never passes V0_noG0_munu. Dead artifact + dead meta arg; safe to delete writer and call.

item: tagged_arrays.load_restart_state_from_h5 band_slices arg
evidence: tagged_arrays.py:210 `del band_slices  # retained for call-site compatibility`; the parameter has no effect on any code path. Sole caller src/gw/gw_init.py:1310-1311 passes band_slices=band_slices as a kwarg, so removing the parameter requires touching exactly one call site. No other callers (grep across src/tests/tools/scripts + sandbox).

item: sigma_output._val complex fallback (sigma_output.py:175-182)
evidence: Enumerated all _val call sites: line 198 passes v.real and v.imag (real python floats, taken only when np.iscomplexobj(arr) at 197), line 200 passes v from an arr where iscomplexobj is False, i.e. real dtype. So the isinstance(x,complex)/iscomplexobj(x) branch is unreachable from this module and the 'should never reach here' comment is accurate. The silent imag-drop would only bite on a contrived object-dtype column. Defensive dead branch, harmless.

item: sigma_output local `import h5py` (sigma_output.py:360)
evidence: Module-level `import h5py` at sigma_output.py:4 is used by write_chunked_complex_dataset_h5 (line 223); the local re-import inside copy_sigma_kij_h5_to_omega_h5 (line 360) shadows it with the identical module. No behavioral effect (same module object from sys.modules); pure copy-paste residue. One-line deletion.

item: sigma_output use_ffi_io legacy alias (sigma_output.py:265,280)
evidence: Alive and coherent: coercion happens in exactly one place, slab_io._normalize_slab_backend (slab_io.py:61-88; backend enum wins, use_ffi_io=True maps to PHDF5_FFI, documented at slab_io.py:23-24). But the bool is threaded as a parallel kwarg next to `backend` through ~10 modules (sigma_x_bispinor.py:111, cohsex_sigma.py:163/272, v_q_bispinor.py:261/500/774, tagged_arrays.py:27/105, ...) and `use_ffi_io` is also the user-facing config key (gw_config.py:207,984-1012) that auto-routes to a concrete enum. The config key stays; the per-function bool alias is the sweep candidate — dual parallel knobs violate the single-source-of-truth rule.

item: qp_wfn dual Hartree+Rydberg energy storage (qp_wfn.py:56-57)
evidence: E_qp_nk arrives in Hartree (sc_iteration.py:730 converts Ry->Ha with *0.5 at the call); writer stores E_qp_nk_hartree and E_qp_nk_rydberg = E*2.0 (1 Ha = 2 Ry, factor correct). The Rydberg copy is the one actually consumed (src/postprocess/rotate_wfn_to_qp.py:92 reads E_qp_nk_rydberg; nothing reads the Hartree copy). Units documented in the file itself (attrs['energy_units'], qp_wfn.py:76). Convenience duplication for mixed consumers, self-documenting, correct.

item: qp_wfn fresh WfnLoader(wfn._filename) full re-slurp (qp_wfn.py:159-167)
evidence: Both call sites are rank-0-gated (gw_jax.py:760 `if jax.process_index()==0`, sc_iteration.py:721) and the top-level loader's .load is a COLLECTIVE on the phdf5 backend (comment qp_wfn.py:154-158) — calling it under a rank-0 gate would hang. A fresh mesh-less WfnLoader gets the eager h5py backend, so rank-0 can read alone; cost is one-shot end-of-run. `_filename` is not raw private-attr poking: wfn_loader.py:133 defines it as 'legacy WFNReader compat' and file_io/__init__.py:19 documents it as an exposed surface. Necessary under current SlabIO/loader semantics; the coupling could be cleaned by a public .filename property but is deliberate today.

item: qp_wfn ifmin/ifmax inheritance + nosym=False (qp_wfn.py:123-131,176)
evidence: Not 'silent': the limitation is documented in the writer's own docstring (qp_wfn.py:123-131 spells out exactly the metal/gap-closure failure and the fix). ifmax=nelec is less wrong than it looks: WfnLoader defines nelec = int(np.max(self.ifmax)) of the SOURCE file (wfn_loader.py:196), so WFNWriter's ifmax=np.full((nspin,nk), nelec) (wfn_writer.py:135-137) round-trips the source occupancy for k-independent-ifmax insulators — the only regime SC-QP rotation is claimed for. nosym=False is deliberate, not leftover: output stays on the irreducible-k grid, so mtrx/tnp must be copied through (qp_wfn.py:114-118; wfn_writer.py else-branch copies crystal.sym_matrices) for downstream BZ unfolding. Documented deferred limitation, not a bug.

item: tagged_arrays triple try/except-pass sync_global_devices (tagged_arrays.py:96-100,120-124,148-153+167-171)
evidence: Actually FOUR copies (write_head_scalars_to_h5 has one on the early-return non-rank-0 path at 148-153 and one at the end at 167-171), plus a fifth sibling in gw_jax.py:767-771. Purpose is real: rank-0-only h5py writes need a barrier so other ranks see the file, and the try/except tolerates single-process/CPU-test environments. But `except Exception: pass` is broader than the ImportError it exists for, and 5 duplicated blocks violate the no-redundancy rule. Extract one `_barrier(tag)` helper with a narrow except; the swallow cannot cause the classic hang (a rank that raises past the barrier while others wait hangs regardless), so this is hygiene, not a live bug.

item: tagged_arrays 8-D/6-D V_qmunu collapse + G0 row-pick (tagged_arrays.py:226-241)
evidence: Explicit, commented back-compat for retired restart layouts (comments at 214-217 name the old (1,npol,npol,...) and (nkx,nky,nkz) splits; 224-225 says 'Back-compat'; 237-239 explains the (nqz,n_rmu) G0 row-pick). Index math checks out: 8-D [0,0,0] strips the (1,npol,npol) singleton/pol axes leaving (nkx,nky,nkz,mu,mu), then reshape(-1,mu,mu) gives flat-q in C row-major = the flat-q convention; 6-D [0,0,0] is the already-flat mid-vintage form. Note old files lacking psi_full_y die earlier anyway (read_restart_state_from_h5:177-181 raises), so this only serves a narrow vintage window — removable once pre-flat-q restart files are retired, harmless until then.

item: tagged_arrays W0_ready attr written post-SlabIO with raw h5py (tagged_arrays.py:90-95, also 116-124)
evidence: Verified the protocol constraint is real: consumers read a PER-DATASET HDF5 attribute — bse_io.py:375,778 and bse/test_bse.py:64 do f['W0_qmunu'].attrs.get('W0_ready') — while SlabIO.write_attr (slab_io.py:198-204) writes a rank-0 small DATASET, not an attribute; SlabIO has no per-dataset-attr API. So the rank-0 h5py write after SlabIO releases the file is the only way to satisfy the existing reader. Correctly gated (process_index()==0) and barriered. A SlabIO write_dataset_attr API would unify the two layers, but the current split is a documented, working workaround.

item: kin_ion raw-h5py shape peek + SlabIO replicated read (kin_ion.py:63-85)
evidence: The peek is genuinely required by the FFI backend: read_slab needs an explicit (shape, offset, dtype) request (comment kin_ion.py:61-62) because the C-level phdf5 FFI path cannot introspect; the peek is a read-only h5py open on all ranks, safe. But the payload is a fully-replicated (nk,nb,nb) with P(None,None,None) — no slab decomposition happens; the docstring itself admits the routing is for 'backend parity' (kin_ion.py:34-35), not necessity. A plain all-ranks h5py read + jnp.asarray would produce the identical replicated array with less machinery. Works correctly today; simplification candidate in the flat-k/IO refactor.

item: paths.resolve_input_paths hard-coded 6-key list (paths.py:20-23)
evidence: The drift is real and produces the claimed cwd-vs-input-dir split, though not via the keys the first pass named: sigma_omega_h5_file and sigma_kij_h5_file ARE input-dir-resolved, just elsewhere (ppm_pipeline.py:254-256 explicit join; gw_driver_helpers.py:281 _resolve_input_path). The confirmed holes: (1) sigma_freq_debug_file is resolved at gw_driver_helpers.py:284 for one consumer but gw_jax.py:908 writes config.debug.sigma_freq_debug_file, which gw_config.py:1051 stores UNRESOLVED -> that output lands cwd-relative while eqp0/sigma_diag land input-dir-relative; (2) centroids_file_current is never resolved anywhere — gw_config.py:903-904 does str(cents_curr) with no input_dir join (despite the variable being named cents_curr_resolved) and gw_init.py:962 opens it directly -> bispinor transverse-centroid path breaks whenever cwd != input dir. Masked because runs normally launch from the input directory. Resolution logic is scattered across three sites (paths.py, gw_driver_helpers.py:45-48, ppm_pipeline.py:255); fold into gw_config with a schema-driven key set.

item: centroids.load_centroids minimal periodic wrap (centroids.py:22-24)
evidence: Correct under the sanctioned generator contract: kmeans_isdf.py:409,431 and orbit_syms.py:85,153,161 wrap all emitted coords with % 1.0, so frac in [0,1) -> round(frac*n) in [0,n] and only the ==n case needs the line-24 wrap. For out-of-contract files: frac in (-1,0) gives indices in [-n,-1] which numpy negative indexing coincidentally maps to the right periodic image; frac >= 1+0.5/n breaks. Single-row correction to the first pass: it fails LOUDLY, not silently — centroid_indices is 1-D, so `centroid_indices[:, i]` at line 24 raises IndexError before n_rmu=3 can propagate. The robust duplicate at htransform.py:473-479 (loadtxt ndmin=2 + np.mod(idx, fft_grid)) is a no-redundancy violation — htransform even imports the shared loader 100 lines later (htransform.py:578,588). Fold the robust version into file_io.centroids and delete the private copy.

item: file_io/__init__ stale module-list docstring (__init__.py:4-11)
evidence: Confirmed stale: the docstring lists 7 modules; the package has 20 (dir listing) and the docstring omits slab_io (+3 backend modules), zeta_loader, zeta_reader, sigma_output, paths, read_bgw_vcoul, isdf_header, mf_header — several of which are imported in the very same file (lines 15-16, 34-41, 45-46). Pure documentation rot; trivial fix, no behavioral impact.

item: file_io export surface drift: write_qp_wfn_h5 unexported, dead symbol exported (__init__.py:38,42)
evidence: Confirmed both halves: __init__.py:42 exports only write_qp_rotations_h5, so both write_qp_wfn_h5 callers bypass the package surface with full-path imports (gw_jax.py:758 and sc_iteration.py:715 `from file_io.qp_wfn import ...`), while __init__.py:38 exports write_chunked_complex_dataset_h5 which has zero callers (see dead verdict above). No __all__ exists, so the export list is purely conventional — but it is the advertised surface and has inverted signal (dead exported, live hidden). Fix together with the dead-code sweep: drop line 38, add write_qp_wfn_h5.

item: batched.py: cholesky_handle_to_natural_L
evidence: Enumerated mechanisms: (a) direct/from-imports — only src/common/chol_natural_test.py:26,55 (a manual GPU correctness driver, `python -m common.chol_natural_test`) plus the re-export in src/ffi/cusolvermp/__init__.py:14,23 which no one else imports; (b) string dispatch — isdf_fitting's solver_kind strings ('cusolvermp_cholesky'/'cusolvermp_lu') dispatch to batched_distributed_potrs/solve_lu directly (isdf_fitting.py:1286-1341), never to this; getattr/importlib grep in isdf_fitting hits only sharding/sym attrs; (c) CLI — pyproject.toml [project.scripts] has only lorrax-gw/gw_jax/lorrax-centroids; (d) sandbox skills/ and runs/ — zero hits; (e) docs — zero hits. The docstring's claimed consumer (symmetric W-solve W = X H^-1 X^dagger, batched.py:273-274) is now the fused cuBLASMp FFI batched_fused_w_solve (w_isdf.py:269-299), which runs entirely in-device and never materializes a natural L. Not confirmed-dead because the chol_natural_test driver exercises it as part of the FFI validation suite.

item: batched.py:182-189, 316-321 — cuSOLVERMp 0.6.0 silent-wrong-answer landmines, claimed 'no runtime version check anywhere in package'
evidence: The first-pass claim is partly WRONG: cpp/context.cc:189-196 calls cusolverMpGetVersion at context creation, stores ctx->cusolvermp_version, selects the CAL-vs-NCCL comm ABI on it (context.cc:257-298, threshold 700), prints a rank-0 banner (209-217), and emits a rank-0 WARNING for 0.6.x on Px>1&&Py>1 grids covering getrf/getrs (context.cc:232-241). Remaining real exposure: (1) the potrs NRHS<=N landmine has NO runtime guard — batched.py:203-205 validates only Mrhs%Py==0, and the isdf_fitting caller pads only to Py-divisibility, not N+Py (isdf_fitting.py:1290-1296); (2) internal docs contradict each other: batched_solve_lu_ffi.cc:10-13 states the potrf/potrs path 'worked correctly at all tested sizes' on 0.6.0, while batched.py:182-189 says potrs is quietly wrong for NRHS<=N on 0.6.0, and context.cc:222-226 argues the 0.7.0 fix was the CAL->NCCL ABI shift for all 2D grids — so whether the potrs caveat still applies on the pinned 0.7.2 default (stage_pypi.sh:31, CUSOLVERMP_VERSION=0.7.2.888) is undetermined in-tree. The docstring should be reconciled and the potrs path given the same rank-0 warning treatment as LU.

item: batched.py:187,324 — stale reference to w_isdf.solve_w_low_mem
evidence: Confirmed stale: grep 'solve_w_low_mem' across src/ hits only batched.py:187,324 (plus the prose 'w_isdf's low_mem W-solve' in batched_solve_lu_ffi.cc:23). The surviving code is w_isdf.solve_w (w_isdf.py:354) dispatching via ScreeningSolver.JAX_NATIVE / CUBLASMP_FFI (_resolve_w_solve_fn, w_isdf.py:333-352); the low-mem branch is _get_w_solve_fn_low_mem (w_isdf.py:269) which calls the fused cuBLASMp FFI batched_fused_w_solve — it does NOT use batched_distributed_potrs and contains no NRHS zero-pad workaround, so the pointer 'see w_isdf.solve_w_low_mem for a concrete workaround' is doubly stale (wrong name AND the referenced workaround no longer exists there). Doc-only defect; the only real NRHS-padding exemplar now lives at isdf_fitting.py:1290-1296.

item: batched.py:73-97 + isdf_fitting.py:1103-1301 — handle .raw round-trip / metadata re-manufacture
evidence: Verified both ends: factor_c_q returns L_handle.raw, deliberately dropping the dataclass (isdf_fitting.py:1101-1105, with an explicit comment 'Downstream solve_zeta rebuilds the CusolverMpBatchedLowerL handle and dispatches to potrs'); solve_zeta rebuilds it at isdf_fitting.py:1297-1300 with n=n_rmu, mb=n_rmu//Px, nb=n_rmu//Py, nbatch=nq. Index math checks out: batched_distributed_cholesky sets mb=n//Px, nb=n//Py (batched.py:138-139) on the same mesh_xy object that solve_zeta uses, so the reconstruction is exact, and potrs re-derives descB blocks from the handle (batched.py:217-220) consistently. The byte-layout invariant (raw = P(None,'y','x')-sharded col-major L tiles, i.e. row-major-read L^T per slice) is protected only by convention — an intervening reshard or mesh swap would be silently wrong, and the potrs mesh check (batched.py:192-193) is vacuous since the handle is rebuilt from the very mesh being validated. But threading bare arrays between pipeline stages instead of carrying dataclasses is this repo's explicit style (no-new-API-layers), and the factor return is already a solver_kind-discriminated union (raw tiles / dense L at 1310-1324 / unfactored C_q at 1294-1295 for the indefinite path). Deliberate, commented, locally consistent.

item: batched.py:67,141,222,367 — _JIT_CACHE keyed by int(ctx_handle), never evicted
evidence: ctx_handle is a process-lifetime singleton per (p,q,layout): context.py:_CACHE (line 44) is only cleared in the atexit teardown (context.py:117-127), so handles never churn within a run and the ctx_handle key component takes at most a few values. Cache growth is therefore bounded by distinct (target, mesh, dtype, nq, n, mb/nb) call signatures — identical behavior to XLA's own jit cache. The comment at batched.py:64-66 documents the purpose (eager shard_map re-traces per call; wrapping in jax.jit once amortises, per ffi/phdf5/ARCHITECTURE.md 2.4). No eviction needed.

item: context.py: MeshKey (export)
evidence: As a public export: in __all__ (context.py:36) but zero importers anywhere — grep across src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/, the sandbox skills/ and runs/, pyproject scripts: the only other 'MeshKey' in the tree is slate/context.py:44 which defines its OWN (p,q) 2-tuple alias rather than importing this 3-tuple one. No string-based access possible for a bare type alias. Note the alias itself is alive INSIDE context.py as a type annotation (lines 41,44,47) — it is only the __all__ export that is dead; removing it from __all__ (not the file) is the correct cleanup.

item: context.py:97 — col_major default True vs every batched caller passing False
evidence: Fully verified split: get_or_init_context defaults col_major=True (context.py:97); eigh.py:98 is the ONLY caller using the default; all five production-path call sites pass col_major=False (cusolvermp/batched.py:136,210,360; cublasmp/batched.py:129,251). MeshKey includes the layout bool (context.py:41,54), so a process using both eigh and any batched solver on the same (p,q) creates two full contexts — two NCCL comms, two streams, two cusolverMp handles/grids (one _make_ctx each, context.py:104-111). Mitigations: distributed_eigh is test/bench-only (see its verdict), so production runs only ever hold the row-major context; and the layout genuinely changes tile->rank ownership (CUSOLVERMP_GRID_MAPPING_COL_MAJOR at context.cc:253-255 maps tile(i,j)->rank j*Px+i vs row-major i*Py+j), so flipping eigh to False is not a pure no-op for the returned Q layout — for eigenvalues it is safe (rank relabeling = symmetric block permutation + transpose of a Hermitian matrix, spectrum-preserving), but Q would need revalidation. Default should be flipped to False (the production convention) with eigh passing True explicitly, or the default removed entirely to force the choice.

item: context.py:117-130 — atexit teardown swallows all exceptions
evidence: Explicitly documented as best-effort at context.py:114-116: 'On abnormal exits (signals, segfaults) this may not run, but that's acceptable — the OS reclaims GPU memory when the process dies. The destroy functions are idempotent/guarded against null.' Swallowing exceptions during interpreter shutdown is the standard pattern (raising in atexit only prints noise and can mask the real exit cause); the guarded get_lib() probe (119-121) handles the case where the .so was never loaded. Nothing to fix.

item: eigh.py: distributed_eigh (production)
evidence: Enumerated mechanisms: (a) imports — only src/common/cusolvermp_eigh_test.py:68,117,166, src/common/eigh_benchmark.py:125,145,149, src/common/eigh_block_sweep.py:48,73-84 (all manual `python -m` drivers), plus the package re-export (__init__.py:16); (b) string dispatch — none found; (c) CLI — not in pyproject [project.scripts]; (d) sandbox skills/ and runs/ — zero hits; (e) docs — referenced as the FFI-port template (eigh.py:3-6, src/ffi/TEMPLATE.md:45) and its smoke test is a mandated validation gate (src/ffi/AGENTS.md:9, src/ffi/PORTING.md:102, docs/architecture/codebase.md:485,625, config/README.md:152, docs/dev/plans/phdf5_cray_mpich_migration.md:169 'must still pass after migration'). No production GW/ISDF consumer exists — the batched solvers took that role. Validated infrastructure + living template, exercised only by test/bench drivers.

item: eigh.py:62-69 — non-default block_size returns eigenvectors in block-cyclic permutation
evidence: Documented in the docstring itself (eigh.py:64-69): default mb=nb=n/p 'one tile per rank — matches JAX's block sharding, eigenvectors come out in-place'; smaller blocks '(block=256 gives 2.4x at n=16k) but return eigenvectors in a block-cyclic permutation of the input basis'. This is inherent ScaLAPACK/cuSOLVERMp semantics: with nb != n/p the output descriptor is genuinely block-cyclic and a redistribution pass would be needed to undo it — not a bug the wrapper introduced, and eigh_block_sweep.py exists precisely to characterize the trade-off. Since the only consumers are eigenvalue-checking test/bench drivers, the knob is safe as shipped; if distributed_eigh ever gains a production Q consumer, the caveat should be promoted from docstring to a runtime error/redistribution.

item: eigh.py:61-63,47 — compute_evecs=False still allocates and returns garbage Q
evidence: XLA FFI calls have a static output signature: the shard_map out_specs and jax.ffi.ffi_call result tuple (W_local, Q_local) are fixed at trace time (eigh.py:106-107,117,120-121), so Q must always be allocated regardless of the runtime flag; cusolverMpSyevd similarly requires the output buffer. The docstring states the contract explicitly (eigh.py:61-63: 'Q is still returned but its contents should be ignored'). Making the signature dynamic would require two traced variants for marginal benefit in a test-only routine. Standard FFI-wrapper pattern, correctly documented.

item: eigh.py:16-23,98 — row-major shard fed as col-major (= A^T); Q basis convention unargued; col_major=True context
evidence: Per-element reasoning: local row-major buffer reinterpreted col-major gives the transposed tile, and with the col-major grid mapping the global matrix cuSOLVERMp factors is a rank-relabeled A^T = conj(A) (A Hermitian). eig(conj(A)): conj(A)u = lambda*u with lambda real, so eigenvalues match A exactly — the in-file argument (eigh.py:18-22) is sound for W. For Q it is NOT sound as stated: u is an eigenvector of conj(A), i.e. conj(u) is the eigenvector of A, and the returned buffer read back row-major is additionally transposed — so the physical eigenvectors of A are conj of the raw Q rows-vs-columns depending on gather convention, and no code or comment pins this down. The smoke test does not gate it either: cusolvermp_eigh_test.py:138-150 computes a Q residual but only PRINTS it inside a try/except ('Not strictly required') — PASS/FAIL is eigenvalue error only (lines 130-135). Harmless today (no production Q consumer), but the Q convention must be derived, asserted, and test-gated before first real use; folding this into the col_major unification (see context.py:97 finding) is the natural refactor.

item: __init__.py:1-8 — docstring advertises only distributed_eigh
evidence: Confirmed stale: the docstring's 'Public API' shows only distributed_eigh (__init__.py:3-7), while lines 9-16 import and __all__ (18-25) exports the five batched symbols, and the actual production consumers import the batched API (isdf_fitting.py:1103,1286,1316: batched_distributed_cholesky/potrs/solve_lu, CusolverMpBatchedLowerL) — distributed_eigh itself is test-only. Two-line doc fix, no behavioral impact.

item: profile_batched.py (whole file)
evidence: No Python imports of it anywhere (grep across src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/: only self-references in its own docstring, lines 18,29, and its default --sync-label at 264). It is a __main__ CLI (`python -m ffi.cusolvermp.profile_batched`, guard at line 335-336), not in pyproject scripts, not referenced by sandbox skills/ or runs/*.sh. However it is NOT abandoned: the sandbox initiative that motivated it references it (/pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox/reports/cusolvermp_ffi_profile_2026-05-12/report.md, reports/gw_refactor_map_2026-07-01/files/ffi_cusolvermp.md, and CHANGELOG.md all mention profile_batched), confirming it is the kept-by-design Nsight reproduction harness. Bench-only, alive as a manual tool.

item: profile_batched.py:35-37 — set_default_env() before the jax import block
evidence: This is the repo-wide entry-point pattern, not a one-off fragility: gw/gw_jax.py:1-2 ('set_default_env()  # BEFORE `import jax` — JAX reads env at import time'), centroid/kmeans_cli.py:10-11, psp/run_nscf.py:23-24, psp/run_sternheimer.py:48-49, psp/tests/test_sternheimer_jvp.py:41-42 all do exactly the same, and runtime/__init__.py:17-18 documents the contract ('set_default_env()   # BEFORE ``import jax``') with the implementation at runtime/__init__.py:47. Yes, an import re-sort would break it — equally true of every LORRAX driver; any auto-formatter config for this repo must already respect the pattern. Established convention.

item: profile_batched.py:246 — --mode lacks the batched_distributed_solve_lu path
evidence: Confirmed: choices=["potrf", "potrf_potrs"] (line 246), and the file imports only batched_distributed_cholesky/potrs (lines 55-58). Timeline supports the hypothesis: the tool served the 2026-05-12 potrf/potrs Nsight initiative (reports/cusolvermp_ffi_profile_2026-05-12), while the LU path was validated later on cuSOLVERMp 0.7.2 (batched_solve_lu_ffi.cc:15-21) and is now the production transverse dispatch (isdf_fitting.py:1316-1341). A coverage gap in a manual bench with no correctness consequence; add a solve_lu mode (and drop the PD shift for it, since transverse CCT is indefinite) only when someone next profiles the transverse channels.

item: profile_batched.py:13-31 — docstring hardcodes lorrax_D path and lxrun invocation
evidence: Line 17 hardcodes `cd /global/homes/j/jackm/scratchperl/lorrax_sandbox/sources/lorrax_D`; the sandbox runs four checkouts A-D so the example goes stale for the other letters, and lxrun/SLURM_JOBID conventions live in the sandbox overlay, not this repo. It is a usage example in a comment, executed by nothing; worst case a human copy-pastes into the wrong checkout and notices immediately (wrong branch/dirty tree). Cosmetic.

item: profile_batched.py:157,187,306 — magic constants (2.0*n PD shift, 1e-12 donation eps, step0=1000)
evidence: All three are inert benchmark plumbing, each locally explained: (1) `a += (2.0*n)*I` (line 157, comment 'Strongly positive-definite, like the CCT charge-channel solve') guarantees potrf succeeds on a random Hermitian whose spectral radius is O(sqrt(n)) — same recipe as the correctness tests (e.g. cusolvermp_eigh_test.py run_real_test uses `np.eye(n)*(2*n)`); (2) eps=1e-12*step (lines 186-189) exists solely to force XLA to materialize a fresh buffer because the wrappers donate their inputs (fresh_for_donation docstring, lines 178-184) — the value only needs to be nonzero-ish and tiny so timing is shape-identical; (3) step0=1000 (line 306 vs step0=0 at line 295) merely disjoins warmup and timed perturbation indices so no donated buffer is reused across the two phases. None affect any measured result beyond keeping the solves well-posed.

item: src/ffi/slate/__init__.py — entire ffi.slate Python package
evidence: Enumerated all call mechanisms. (1) Imports: only src/common/slate_cholesky_trsm_test.py:50, slate_trsm_isolated_test.py:42, slate_eigh_test.py:53, slate_batched_test.py:52-54, slate_chol_trsm_bench.py:50, slate_vs_cusolvermp_bench.py:107 import ffi.slate; zero hits in src/gw, src/psp, src/bse, src/centroid, tests/, tools/, scripts/, misc/. (2) String dispatch: the only 'slate' strings in production src/ are src/ffi/common/ffi_loader.py's FFI-target registration table and ctypes bindings (lines 52-56, 366-411) — package-internal plumbing, not external dispatch. (3) CLI: pyproject.toml [project.scripts] has only lorrax-gw, gw_jax, lorrax-centroids. (4) Sandbox skills/run.sh: /pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox/skills/execute_workflow/SKILL.md:151-155 and runs/*/run.sh reference SLATE only via --volume/LD_LIBRARY_PATH/LD_PRELOAD — load-bearing for the lorrax_ffi.so shared library link (slate cpp/*.cc compile into the same lorrax_ffi.so as cusolvermp per runs/FFI/.../build_nvtx_potrf.log), NOT for the Python API. (5) Production ISDF path uses the cusolvermp twin: src/common/isdf_fitting.py:1103-1104 'from ffi.cusolvermp import batched_distributed_cholesky'; docs/theory/isdf-zeta-vq.md:404 names ffi.cusolvermp. Caveat for the refactor: the Python package (cholesky.py/trsm.py/eigh.py/batched.py/context.py/__init__.py) is test-only, but src/ffi/slate/cpp/ is linked into lorrax_ffi.so, so deleting cpp/ requires a rebuild and touching the runtime LD paths in every run.sh/skill. README.md:127-128 confirms intent: SLATE kept as future AMD/Frontier fallback.

item: src/ffi/slate/eigh.py:77-78 — w_dtype float32 fallback
evidence: eigh.py:77-78: w_dtype = float64 if A.dtype in (complex128, float64) else float32. Only callers of distributed_eigh are slate_eigh_test.py (argparse choices=['f64','c128'] at line 92) and slate_vs_cusolvermp_bench.py (choices=['f64','c128'] at line 78); no string dispatch, no other imports anywhere in repo/skills/runs. Even if an f32/c64 array were passed, the C++ dispatch in cpp/eigh_ffi.cc rejects it: default case returns kInvalidArgument 'unsupported dtype ... (supported: F64, C128)' (~line 242-247), so the f32 W buffer could never be filled. Ironic detail: eigh_ffi.cc:220 contains matching C64→F32 wdtype-validation logic that is equally unreachable because the same function's dtype switch handles only F64/C128. Both sides of this fallback are dead by construction.

item: src/ffi/slate/batched.py:203-211, 283-292 — batched_distributed_trsm plain-array A branch
evidence: The only caller of batched_distributed_trsm in the entire repo (plus skills/, runs/) is src/common/slate_batched_test.py, which calls it exclusively with the SlateBatchedLowerL handle from batched_distributed_cholesky: slate_batched_test.py:150 builds L_handle; lines 165 and 171 pass L_handle for op='N' and op='C'. Zero callers pass a plain (Nbatch,N,N) array, so the isinstance-else branch (batched.py:203-211) and its non-handle shard_map (283-292, in_specs=(P('x',None,'y'),)*2) have zero call sites and zero test coverage. Note the parallel plain-array branch in single-matrix trsm.py IS exercised (slate_trsm_isolated_test.py imports only distributed_trsm, no cholesky handle) — the deadness is specific to the batched variant.

item: src/ffi/slate/context.py:44-48, 88 — context cache keyed only (p,q)
evidence: Confirmed: MeshKey = (p,q) (context.py:44), _CACHE keyed by validate_mesh's (p,q) (line 88), and _make_ctx passes only (rank, world, p, q) to the C++ side (lines 76-80) — the mesh's device permutation never reaches the FFI at all. This is not a cache-collision bug but a design-wide assumption: the C++ rank remap (README.md:51-57, key = jax_rank/q + (jax_rank%q)*p) hardcodes JAX's default C-order shard-to-rank mapping (shard (mx,my) on process mx*q+my), which only holds for Mesh(np.asarray(jax.devices()).reshape(p,q)). A permuted-device mesh would be wrong even with a per-mesh cache, since the handle encodes nothing layout-specific. validate_mesh enforces p*q == jax.process_count() (context.py:63-67) and the sub-mesh restriction is documented three times (context.py:17-25, README.md:77-80, 120-124). Verdict: consistent, documented single-layout convention; would need rework if sub-mesh/partial-world support lands, but the cache key is not the weak point — the remap formula is.

item: src/ffi/slate/context.py:126-142 — bare except in _atexit_teardown
evidence: context.py:126-145: registered via atexit.register (line 145). At interpreter exit, MPI may already be finalized (mpi4py or phdf5 registered their own atexit hooks in arbitrary order per Python's LIFO-within-registration-order rules), the ctypes lib may fail to load (guarded 'except Exception: return' at 128-130), and destroy_slate_context (ffi_loader.py:403-404) can abort inside MPI_Comm_free after MPI_Finalize. Swallowing per-handle failures and continuing the loop so remaining handles are attempted is standard shutdown hygiene; a leaked dup'd communicator at process death has no consequence. Nothing is hidden during normal operation — teardown runs only at exit.

item: src/ffi/slate/cholesky.py:47-49 — SlateLowerL.raw holds col-major bytes sharded P('y','x')
evidence: Deliberate zero-copy handle protocol, explicitly documented as such: the attribute docstring itself warns 'JAX reading this directly would give L.T (or L.conj().T for complex)' (cholesky.py:47-49), and the module docstring (lines 11-16) prescribes exactly two consumers: distributed_trsm, which detects the handle (trsm.py:64-77) and feeds raw straight back to SLATE skipping the local transpose (trsm.py:127-135, in_specs P('y','x')), and to_jax_lower (cholesky.py:62-73) which does the local transpose + tril for conventional consumption. Index check of to_jax_lower: raw shard on rank (my,mx) is the (n/q, n/p) row-major transpose of that rank's L block; _local_T flips it back to (n/p, n/q) row-major and out_specs P('x','y') reassigns axes — pure local op, correct. The foot-gun exists only for a caller that bypasses both documented consumption paths; frozen dataclass + docstring is the chosen guard.

item: src/ffi/slate/cholesky.py:76-116 — no Python dtype validation, no jit/trace cache
evidence: Two sub-claims verified separately. (a) Dtype: no Python-side check exists (cholesky.py:82-89 validates only squareness/divisibility), but validation happens at the FFI boundary — cpp/potrf_ffi.cc:137-161 checks L/A dtype match and its dispatch switch returns kInvalidArgument 'unsupported dtype ... (supported: F64, C128)' for anything but F64/C128, so an f32 caller gets a clear error, just later. Not a correctness hole. (b) Trace cache: distributed_cholesky and distributed_trsm build fresh eager shard_maps per call, which re-trace every time (batched.py:61-64 comment documents this cost and why _JIT_CACHE was added there — 'when the user loops the same-shape op'). A one-shot factorization amortizes this; the inconsistency is perf-only in an evaluation-grade, test-only package. If the package survives, aligning with batched.py's _JIT_CACHE is a small mechanical change.

item: src/ffi/slate/trsm.py:148-153 — _untranspose as a separate eager shard_map pass
evidence: Confirmed divergence: trsm.py runs the FFI shard_map with out_specs=P('y','x') (lines 128-145) then a second eager shard_map _untranspose (148-153) to flip back to P('x','y'), while batched.py:277-281 transposes inside the same shard_map body ('Untranspose inside the shard_map → skip a second pass') with out_specs already P('x',None,'y'). There is no blocker to folding it in trsm.py — batched.py proves the pattern works for the identical op; the local transpose is rank-local either way. Cost is real per README.md:87-89 ('3 local transposes per cholesky+trsm chain ... below ~1k the transposes are a visible fraction'), plus each eager shard_map re-traces per call. batched.py is the later, improved copy; trsm.py never got the improvement. Pure consolidation, no behavior change.

item: src/ffi/slate/trsm.py:69 — handle path silently overwrites side/uplo
evidence: trsm.py:64-69: on the SlateLowerL path, op is validated (raises for op not in ('N','C'), lines 66-68) but side/uplo are unconditionally assigned 'L','L' (line 69). This pinning is documented twice: module docstring lines 11-15 ('side/uplo are pinned by the handle') and function docstring lines 58-60; the batched twin does the identical thing (batched.py:189-193) with the same documentation (batched.py:180-181). Since the parameter defaults are already 'L','L', silent override only bites a caller who explicitly passes side='R' or uplo='U' together with a handle — a contradiction that arguably deserves a ValueError like op gets, but the asymmetry is deliberate (op is meaningful for a Cholesky factor, side/uplo are not). Minor foot-gun inside a documented convention.

item: src/ffi/slate/trsm.py:35-38 vs batched.py:56-59 — duplicated _SIDE/_UPLO/_OP/_DIAG maps
evidence: Verified verbatim duplication: trsm.py:35-38 and batched.py:56-59 define identical four dicts ({'L':0,'R':1}, {'L':0,'U':1}, {'N':0,'T':1,'C':2}, {'N':0,'U':1}). The integer codes are the shared wire protocol with cpp/trsm_ffi.cc and cpp/batched_trsm_ffi.cc, so a one-sided edit would silently desynchronize Python and C++. Textbook single-source-of-truth violation (also against the sandbox no-redundancy rule); hoist to context.py or a shared _enums module if the package survives the refactor — though note the package is test-only, so this may be mooted by deletion.

item: src/ffi/slate/eigh.py — heev eigenvectors wrong by unpinned layout transform
evidence: Confirmed as a known, documented, unresolved bug, and the reader's mechanism is essentially right. Facts: (1) eigh.py applies NO local transpose on input (lines 93-95 pass local_A straight to the FFI) and NO untranspose on output Q (lines 97-98), unlike cholesky.py:112 (input transpose) and cholesky.py:68-73/trsm.py:148-153 (output untranspose). (2) cpp/eigh_ffi.cc's own header comment (lines 22-28) gives the flawed justification: 'For a Hermitian A, transposition is a no-op (A = A^H)'. Per-element: reading a row-major buffer as col-major yields the transpose; for complex Hermitian A, A^T = conj(A) ≠ A — transposition is only a no-op up to conjugation. eigvals(conj(A)) = conj(eigvals(A)) = eigvals(A) since the spectrum is real, so W is correct (matches README.md:83 'eigvals are correct'). Eigenvectors of conj(A) are conj(v), and Q is additionally written back in SLATE tile layout with no Python-side transform (eigh_ffi.cc:125-130 notes SLATE manages Z internally and tiles are copied back), so Q differs from truth by conjugation composed with a tile-layout transform — exactly 'wrong by a layout transform we haven't fully pinned down' (README.md:82-84). Caveat: with nb=n/p one-tile-per-rank the exact global transform also depends on how block positions map under the rank remap; I could not fully pin the composed transform without running code (login node), which matches the authors' own statement. README already says 'Use cholesky/trsm if at all possible' and lists it under Future work (129-130).

item: src/ffi/slate/eigh.py:42-45, 79-80 — compute_evecs=False still returns Q
evidence: Documented contract: eigh.py:43-44 'Q is still returned but its contents are unspecified'. The FFI handler is bound with a fixed two-output signature (W_local, Q_local at lines 79-80, ffi_call at 94-95), so the Q buffer must be allocated regardless; cpp/eigh_ffi.cc branches on compute_evecs internally (line 125). The module docstring (lines 3-4) states the shape contract deliberately mirrors ffi.cusolvermp.distributed_eigh 'so call sites can swap backends with a one-line import change' — fixed arity is part of that interchangeability. Wasteful of one (n,n) allocation when only eigenvalues are wanted, but explicit and consistent with the twin backend.

item: src/ffi/slate/batched.py:65, 133-134, 249-253 — unbounded _JIT_CACHE keyed on raw ctx_handle int
evidence: Confirmed: module-level dict (batched.py:65), keys include int(ctx_handle) (134, 253), no eviction. Stale-handle scenario requires a context to be destroyed and recreated mid-process, but no such path exists: contexts are cached per (p,q) in context.py:47-48/_SUBROW_CACHE and destroyed only in the atexit hook (context.py:126-145), which also clears the caches — after which no more JAX calls run. Key-space growth is bounded in practice by distinct (mesh shape, dtype, matrix dims, enums, alpha) combos in a process. The rationale comment (batched.py:61-64) shows this is a deliberate perf fix for eager shard_map re-tracing. Harmless today; becomes a foot-gun only if manual context teardown is ever added, at which point the cache and the context registry need coupled invalidation.

item: src/ffi/slate/batched.py:22-24 — 'ffi.cusolvermp.batched_* (forthcoming)' docstring
evidence: Confirmed stale. The twin exists and is the production path: src/ffi/cusolvermp/batched.py defines batched_distributed_cholesky (line 112), batched_distributed_potrs (line 170), batched_distributed_solve_lu (line 310), exported via src/ffi/cusolvermp/__init__.py:9-18; it is used in production by src/common/isdf_fitting.py:1103-1104 ('from ffi.cusolvermp import batched_distributed_cholesky') and referenced in docs/theory/isdf-zeta-vq.md:404. The slate README.md:109-111/125-128 similarly says the cuSOLVERMp batched implementation 'is planned' — also stale in the same way. One-word doc fixes, or moot if the package is deleted.

item: src/ffi/slate/batched.py:193, 198 — handle path side/uplo overwrite + axis_names-only mesh check; 1x4 SLATE assert
evidence: Three bundled claims, split verdicts; the sharp one drives the classification. (a) side/uplo overwrite (batched.py:193): documented convention, same as trsm.py:69 (docstring batched.py:180-181) — intentional. (b) 1x4 mesh SLATE internal assert internal_batch.hh:290: explicitly documented out-of-scope with cuSOLVERMp as the answer (README.md:113-116) — intentional restriction. (c) The mesh-compat check IS a genuine validation gap: batched.py:198 compares only mesh.axis_names != A.mesh.axis_names, never mesh shape (trsm.py:73 has the identical gap). Failure scenario: build L_handle on a 2x2 mesh (raw bytes laid out for Py=2, per-slice col-major with n//2 local cols per batched.py:137-141), then call batched_distributed_trsm with a 4x1 mesh — axis_names match ('x','y'), validate_mesh passes (4*1 == process_count), nb is inherited from the handle (196-197), and jax.jit silently reshards A.raw onto the new mesh, feeding SLATE bytes whose tile layout was fixed by the old Py → wrong X with no error. Mitigations: package is test-only, the single existing caller uses one mesh throughout, and rectangular meshes may crash in SLATE first — hence latent. Fix is one line: also compare mesh shapes (or mesh device tuples).

item: read_bgw_vcoul.py:108/121 — q keyed by round(mod(q,1)*1e8), q_fracs reconstructed from key
evidence: Index math checks out end-to-end. Store: key=round(mod(q,1)e8) wraps q into [0,1) and quantizes at 1e-8 (BGW vcoul text has fewer decimals). Recover: find_q_index (lines 59-79) matches q_frac = S@q_tbl + kg0 with kg0=rint(diff), so a signed request like q=-0.001 matches stored 0.999 with kg0=-1. Value correctness: fill_v_grid_for_q maps G_input = S@G - kg0, so q_frac + G_input = S@q_tbl + kg0 + S@G - kg0 = S(q_tbl+G); |S(q+G)| is metric-invariant, so v=8pi/<|q+G+dq|^2> lands on the right grid entry. Signed info is not lost, it is recovered via umklapp exactly as the first-pass reader hypothesized. Quantization (1e-8) is far below match tol (1e-4).

item: read_bgw_vcoul.py:113-119 — first-repeated-q truncation heuristic
evidence: Deliberately documented at src/file_io/read_bgw_vcoul.py:87-100: later blocks for the same q are fresh mini-BZ MC draws ('MC-noise siblings') and keeping only the first block per q matches BGW's internal behaviour at outer-k=rk(1); non-IBZ q's are reconstructed via the symmetry path in find_q_index. The contiguity assumption matches BGW's write order (vcoul emitted inside the q-loop per outer k). Failure mode is not fully silent: a q missing from the first pass with no sym-equivalent raises ValueError at read_bgw_vcoul.py:81; a sym-equivalent hit returns physically equivalent v (metric-invariant) up to MC noise. Residual risk (a hypothetical BGW version interleaving q-blocks) is real but the docstring records the assumption. This is a BGW-comparison debug path (gw_driver_helpers.py:185-206, gated on head.use_bgw_vcoul).

item: read_bgw_vcoul.py:176 — einsum('ij,gj->gi',S,G) - kg0 duplicating canonical sym action
evidence: Confirmed duplication of the canonical integer transform G_full = S@G_irr - kg0, which now lives inlined in src/file_io/wfn_loader.py:364-380 ('g_rot = np.einsum(\'ij,kj->ki\', sym_krep, k_gvecs) - Gkk' at line 379 — identical operation, only index letters differ; per-element: G'_i = sum_j S_ij G_j - kg0_i, correct for k_full = S k_bar + kg0 since exp(i S(k_bar+G) r) = exp(i (k_full + (S G - kg0)) r)). Additionally the comment at read_bgw_vcoul.py:170-171 cites 'common/symmetry_maps.py:get_gvecs_kfull (lines 689-690)' which is STALE: symmetry_maps.py:2-8 says that function moved to file_io.wfn_loader.WfnLoader in refactor P5. Third copy in epsreader.unfold_eps_comps:136 (dead). Route through one helper per the unified-sym-action principle.

item: read_bgw_vcoul.py:185-192 — zero v[0,0,0] only when q wraps to 0
evidence: Physics is correct and explicitly documented (lines 185-188 comment; docstring lines 160-161): the q->0, G=0 head is singular and handled by the rank-1 head correction (gw_jax.py:296 'rank-1 head update via head_correction.apply_q0_head_rank1'), while for q!=0 the G=0 entry v(q,0)=8pi/|q|^2 is a finite body term that must survive. The wrap np.mod(q+0.5,1)-0.5 maps components to [-0.5,0.5) so any lattice-equivalent-to-Gamma q (including q0 shifts below tol=1e-4... note q0 shifts are ~1e-3 > tol, so they correctly keep their G=0 body value) is detected. Intentional head/body split, matching the split in the main V_q pipeline.

item: epsreader.get_eps_minus_delta_matrix
evidence: Enumerated: not exported by src/file_io/__init__.py (only EPSReader class is); no direct/from-import, no getattr/string dispatch (grepped), not in pyproject console_scripts, not in the __main__ demo (epsreader.py:153-162 uses only get_eps_matrix), zero hits in tests/, tools/, scripts/, docs/, and sandbox skills/+runs/ (sandbox-wide grep). Sole references: misc/archived_tests/cohsex_noisdf.py:420 — an archived test NOT collected by pytest (pyproject.toml:60 testpaths=["tests"], norecursedirs=["archive"]) — plus its blitz-workspace clones. Note it is also a verbatim copy of get_eps_matrix plus one diagonal-subtract line.

item: epsreader.unfold_eps_comps
evidence: Same enumeration as above: only callers are misc/archived_tests/cohsex_noisdf.py:401,455 (archived, never collected — pyproject.toml:60-61) and blitz-workspace clones of the same file. No exports, no dynamic dispatch, no CLI, no sandbox skills/runs usage (runs/MoS2/.../compute_head.py uses EPSReader but only the constructor/epshead).

item: epsreader.get_eps_diagonal
evidence: Zero references of any kind: repo-wide grep (src, tests, tools, scripts, docs, misc — including archived tests, which call the other two methods but never this one), sandbox-wide grep over skills/ and runs/, no __init__ export, no getattr/dict dispatch, no console_script, not used in the module's own __main__ demo. item: epsreader subspace attrs (matrix_subspace/matrix_eigenvec/matrix_fulleps0)
evidence: Assigned conditionally at epsreader.py:74-80 but repo-wide + sandbox-wide grep finds zero reads of self.matrix_subspace / matrix_eigenvec / matrix_fulleps0; the only other hits are the BGW HDF5 format spec docs/dev/archive/misc/epsmat.h5.txt:261-288 (documentation of the file format, not a consumer). Write-only attributes: dead as data.

item: epsreader.py:67 — eager full mats/matrix slurp in __init__
evidence: Confirmed real cost on a live path: gw/head_correction.py:130-136 constructs EPSReader(eps0_path) solely to read the single complex scalar eps0.epshead (= matrix[0,0,0,0,0,:] at epsreader.py:71), but __init__ line 67 loads the full (nq,nmat,nfreq,ng,ng,2) dataset — for a production eps0mat with ng~10^4 that is multi-GB of host RAM for one scalar. The other live consumers (scripts/checks/sigma_direct_check.py:167, w_from_eps0_0d_check.py:439) do use full matrices, so the fix is lazy/sliced access (get_eps_matrix already slices per-iq and could read from the h5 dataset directly), not deletion.

item: epsreader.py:70 — 'TODO: frequency dep.' static-only epshead
evidence: Known, documented, and guarded limitation rather than a silent bug: the consumer head_correction.py:126-128 explicitly warns 'wcoul0_source=epshead is static-only; using epshead(0) for omega=...' and tags the source string 'epshead(0)' (line 140); the resolver also has an s_tensor fallback ordering (lines 191-193). Dynamic-head support is future work, and the runtime warning makes the approximation visible in logs.

item: epsreader.py:121-135 — commented asserts + Sinv/matmul alternative in unfold_eps_comps, 'NO SUPPORT FOR TAU'
evidence: The enclosing method unfold_eps_comps is itself dead (only archived-test callers, see above), so the commented-out assert lines 121-122, commented Sinv path at 131/135, and the tau limitation note at 127 are cruft inside dead code. The live einsum at 136 is the same S@G - Gq transform duplicated from the canonical wfn_loader.py:379 (index math verified there). Delete the whole method when sweeping; nothing to fix.

item: epsreader.py:82-85 — __del__-based h5 close, no context manager
evidence: Fragile in principle (close time depends on GC; h5py handles held through interpreter shutdown can warn) but harmless in every live usage: head_correction.py:133 and the two scripts/checks create short-lived readers on rank 0, read-only mode, CPython refcounting closes promptly. hasattr guard at line 84 protects against partial-__init__ failure. Adding __enter__/__exit__ would be nicer (WFNWriter at wfn_writer.py:214-218 already does) but there is no observed failure scenario.

item: qe_save_reader.validate_against_wfn
evidence: Single caller: src/psp/tests/test_dft_hamiltonian.py:67 — and note src/psp/tests is not even collected by default pytest (pyproject.toml:60 testpaths=["tests"] at repo root); that file is an argparse-driven manual check script (uses args.save at line 65). No other reference in repo, sandbox skills/, or runs/ (blitz-workspace hits are clones of the same checkout). No dynamic dispatch. Test/diagnostic-only.

item: qe_save_reader.py:24-26,163 — translations stored as -tau*2pi with admitted 24/48 sign mismatch vs pw2bgw
evidence: Self-admitted unresolved convention bug (docstring line 25: '24/48 non-symmorphic translations have a sign mismatch vs pw2bgw; rotation matrices match exactly') and validate_against_wfn (lines 301-322) checks sym_matrices but conspicuously omits translations, so the mismatch is never caught. Propagation path is real: psp/run_nscf.py:265/383 constructs WFNWriter with nosym defaulting to False (run_nscf.py:132), and wfn_writer.py:162 writes crystal.translations verbatim into mf_header/symmetry/tnp; downstream tnp consumers apply non-symmorphic tau phases when unfolding IBZ->full-BZ psi (wfn_loader.py:607-619 exp(-i (S G) tau), symmetry_maps.py:1100, centroid/orbit_syms.py:57, isdf_fitting.py:2057). Concrete failure: a run_nscf-produced IBZ WFN.h5 for a non-symmorphic crystal (e.g. MoS2 or Si with the wrong-sign half of the 48 ops) gives wrong unfold phases for the affected ops. Only nosym=True runs (identity, tnp=0) are safe.

item: qe_save_reader.py:214 — nkpts=0 always in from_qe_save
evidence: The first-pass claim that nkpts is 'an advertised duck-type field' is wrong: the docstring's duck-type list (lines 4-6) enumerates bdot..sym_matrices,translations and does NOT include nkpts, and lines 8-10 explicitly say CrystalData 'cannot be used with SymMaps or the GW pipeline' (the only .nkpts consumers — symmetry_maps.py:861-871, qp_wfn.py:136-179, zeta_loader — all take WFN/mf objects). Grep over all CrystalData consumers (run_nscf, kpm_dos, scf_potential, dft_operators, vnl_ops, centroid/charge_density) shows none reads .nkpts. A placeholder field that could carry a comment, nothing more.

item: qe_save_reader.py:1 — docstring says psp/qe_save_reader.py, file in src/file_io/
evidence: Confirmed stale header from the psp->file_io move: qe_save_reader.py:2 says 'psp/qe_save_reader.py' while the file lives at src/file_io/qe_save_reader.py; src/psp/dev_status.md:16-17 still lists both qe_save_reader.py and wfn_writer.py under psp/. Same pattern as wfn_writer.py:2. Cosmetic; fix docstrings + dev_status.md in the refactor.

item: qe_save_reader.py:137 — alat = |a1| assumption
evidence: Latent inconsistency, verified by unit analysis: line 136 sets alat=|a1| (ignoring the XML's own alat attribute), line 137 blat=2pi/|a1|, but line 139-140 reads b1..b3 from the XML — which QE writes in units of 2pi/alat_QE (=celldm(1)) — and forms bdot = bvec@bvec.T*blat^2. If alat_QE != |a1| (e.g. ibrav=2 fcc where celldm(1)=a but |a1|=a/sqrt(2)), bdot is wrong by (alat_QE/|a1|)^2 = 2x -> kinetic energies double. Checked the actual sandbox Si save (runs/Si/00_si_4x4x4_60band/qe/scf/silicon.save/data-file-schema.xml): alat attribute 7.2558 == |a1|=5.1306*sqrt(2), i.e. sandbox inputs are ibrav=0 where QE sets alat=|a1|, so it has never fired here. Robust fix: read the atomic_structure alat attribute instead of assuming |a1|.

item: qe_save_reader.py:315-321,345 — _EPS=1e-5 and tol=0.5 magic tolerances
evidence: _EPS=1e-5 at line 345 is a faithful clone of QE's kpoint_grid.f90 eps=1.0d-5 (the module explicitly claims to reproduce that routine, docstring lines 22-23 and _reduce_mp_to_ibz docstring line 334) — changing it would break bit-parity with QE's IBZ reduction. tol=0.5 in _chk (lines 315-319) applied to integer-valued fields (atom_types, nspinor, fft_grid, ntran, sym_matrices) is exact-integer-equality expressed through the shared max|delta| float comparator — slightly odd idiom but correct: two distinct integers differ by >=1 > 0.5. Cosmetic at worst; not a bug.

item: mf_header.py — eager [()]/[:] reads, no shape validation
evidence: Deliberate design stated in the module docstring (mf_header.py:15-18): 'deliberately does NOT expose any derived quantities... MfHeader is a pure 1:1 mirror of the on-disk group', with field-by-field BGW dataset names (lines 30-82). The header group is KB-scale so eager reads are free; a malformed artifact fails with a h5py KeyError naming the missing dataset at read time (lines 92-131), which is an acceptable error surface for a mirror type. Adding shape checks would duplicate what consumers (e.g. qp_wfn.py:136-144) already assert where shapes matter.

item: wfn_writer.write_wfn_h5 (batch, lines 225-248)
evidence: All call mechanisms enumerated: (1) not exported — file_io/__init__.py:24 exports only WFNWriter; (2) every src grep hit for 'write_wfn_h5' is the unrelated boolean config flag (gw_config.py:343,657,1052; gw_jax.py:574,757) whose True-branch calls dump_qp_wfn_artifacts / file_io.qp_wfn.write_qp_wfn_h5, both routed through the WFNWriter class (qp_wfn.py:133), never the batch function; (3) src/psp/run_nscf.py:265,383 uses WFNWriter directly; (4) no console_script, no getattr dispatch, no active test (tests/ testpaths grep clean); (5) the single external reference, runs/Si/04_si_4x4x4_davidson/00_davidson/run_nscf.py:34 'from psp.wfn_writer import write_wfn_h5', is a historical run-dir script whose import path is broken — src/psp contains no wfn_writer.py (moved to file_io), so it cannot execute today; (6) stale doc psp/dev_status.md:168. Bonus confirmed: its 'occupations' kwarg (line 235) is never referenced in the body (243-248) — silent no-op even in the dead code.

item: wfn_writer.py:99-100,135-137 — occ[0,:,:nelec]=1 and ifmax=nelec
evidence: Confirmed latent factor-of-2 bug on the nspinor=1 path. BGW convention: occ in [0,1] per band and ifmax = index of highest occupied band; for nspinor=1 non-spin-polarized each band holds 2 electrons, so ifmax should be nelec/2 — wfn_writer.py:99-100 sets n_occ=int(crystal.nelec), _occ[0,:,:n_occ]=1.0 and line 137 ifmax=nelec, i.e. 2x too many bands marked occupied. Correct only for nspinor=2 (one electron/band). Two live producers hit this: psp/run_nscf.py:265/383 (supports scalar crystals — nspinor from QE noncolin flag, qe_save_reader.py:182) and the GW WFN_qp.h5 dump via qp_wfn.write_qp_wfn_h5 (qp_wfn.py:133), where the docstring (qp_wfn.py:123-125) acknowledges the ifmax=nelec convention but only discusses band-reordering risk, not the spin-degeneracy factor — and LORRAX runs plenty of nspinor=1 GW (runs/Si NONBISPINOR sweeps). Any downstream consumer trusting occ/ifmax (BSE, BGW) on a scalar-relativistic LORRAX-written WFN gets wrong occupations.

item: wfn_writer.py:199 — write_k writes _el[0, ik], spin channel 0 hardcoded
evidence: LORRAX-wide scope is nspin=1: magnetic systems are handled as noncolinear bispinors (nspinor=2, nspin=1; qe_save_reader.py:183 sets nspin=2 only for lsda-without-noncolin, and no LORRAX pipeline supports lsda — cf. the known 'V_xc is spin-blind' limitation). Both producers (run_nscf Davidson, qp_wfn QP rotation) are nspin=1 by construction. Caveat worth one line in the refactor: WFNWriter accepts crystal.nspin=2 in the header (line 120) while write_k/close would silently leave the second channel's el/occ zero — an assert nspin==1 in __init__ would convert a hypothetical silent-wrong-output into a loud error, but no live path reaches it.

item: wfn_writer.py:155,160 — cell_symmetry=0 hardcoded (both branches); flavor=2 at 116
evidence: Checked actual consumers on both sides. BGW: grep over sources/BerkeleyGW shows cell_symmetry is read/broadcast (wfn_io_hdf5_inc.f90:250) and only bounds-checked — wfn_rho_vxc_io_inc.f90:240 'if(cell_symmetry /= 0 .and. /= 1) die' — plus one debug print (Sigma/sympert_utils.f90:86); no computational use, and 0 passes the bounds check even for hexagonal cells. LORRAX: wfn_loader.py:175 / zeta_loader.py:138 / zeta_reader.py:106 mirror it into attributes nobody computes with. flavor=2 (complex) is correct always — LORRAX wavefunctions are complex. Wrong metadata for hexagonal cells, but provably inert; fix opportunistically.

item: wfn_writer.py:2 — docstring says psp/wfn_writer.py
evidence: Same stale-move header as qe_save_reader: wfn_writer.py:2 claims 'psp/wfn_writer.py', actual location src/file_io/wfn_writer.py; psp/dev_status.md:17,168 still documents it under psp/. Purely cosmetic; also the broken 'from psp.wfn_writer import ...' in runs/Si/04_si_4x4x4_davidson/00_davidson/run_nscf.py:34 shows the move happened after that run was archived.

item: wfn_writer.py:46 — round(|G|^2 * 1e8) lexsort key + metric einsum
evidence: Documented QE-parity requirement: _build_gspace_components docstring (wfn_writer.py:33-35) states 'sorted by (round(|G|^2 x 1e8), g1, g2, g3). Matches QE ggen.f90' — the integer quantization groups G-vectors into degenerate |G|^2 shells exactly as QE does, so the written gspace ordering is byte-compatible with QE/pw2bgw output. The identical constant and idiom exist at src/psp/gvec_utils.py:31 (shared convention, and a consolidation candidate — two copies of the same shell-key logic). The einsum 'gi,ij,gj->g' with bdot is the standard per-vector quadratic form |G|^2 = G_i bdot_ij G_j (per element g: sum_ij G[g,i] bdot[i,j] G[g,j]) — verified index-wise, symmetric metric, no transposition hazard.

item: src/ffi/cublasmp/batched.py: batched_distributed_gemm
