evidence: Accessor defined src/ffi/cusolvermp/batched.py:258, exported __init__.py:14,23; sole caller is chol_natural_test.py:55 (grep across src/tests/tools/scripts/sandbox complete). Its docstring's anticipated consumer ('the symmetric W-solve path: W = X H^-1 X^†', batched.py:271-273) is implemented in production entirely inside the fused C++ FFI (batched_fused_w_solve, batched.py:198-204), so the Python accessor never gained a production call site. Accessor + test are a deletable pair, but the accessor is exported public API with a documented purpose — test-only, not confirmed-dead.

item: symmetry_test.py whole file
evidence: The 'zero grep hits in any doc' claim is false: CHANGELOG.md:2497-2504 documents the file and gives two exact run commands (uv run python -m common.symmetry_test .../WFN.h5). No skills/runs/scripts invoke it, but it is the ONLY caller of SymMaps.validate_kgrid_unfolding (symmetry_maps.py:1125) — deleting it orphans that validator. Also note it DOES have PASS/FAIL output and a proper nonzero exit on failure (symmetry_test.py:43,58), unlike most siblings.

item: cusolvermp_eigh_test.py:58-64 bare except around jax.distributed.initialize
evidence: Confirmed lines 58-64: try/initialize/except Exception: pass, then DONE sentinel set unconditionally at line 64. Failure scenario traced: SLURM_NTASKS=4 + initialize() raising (stale PMI) leaves jax.process_count()==1 on every rank; the default-grid logic (lines 196-198) then picks 1x1, the p*q==n_procs check passes, and each of the 4 ranks runs the full test single-GPU and prints 'ALL PASSED' — the distributed path is never exercised yet the smoke gate reports success. Same block cloned in ~14 siblings (slate_batched_test.py:42-46, cusolvermp_batched_test.py:34-42, cusolvermp_solve_lu_test.py:31-38, w_solve_modes_test.py, cublasmp_*_test.py, isdf_zeta_mode_test.py, wfn_loader_backend_parity_test.py, ...). A test that can silently pass without testing its subject is a validity bug; fix once, propagate.

item: slate_batched_test.py:182-213 implicit-L verification + no PASS/FAIL
evidence: Claim half-wrong: the distributed L IS transitively validated. Cholesky factorization of an HPD matrix (positive diagonal) is unique, so res_fwd = |L_ref @ X_slate - B|/|B| (line 200, with L_ref = np.linalg.cholesky(A)) would blow up to O(1) if SLATE's factor differed from L_ref — the solve check pins L through uniqueness. Real defect confirmed: residuals are only printed (lines 208-212) with no tolerance assertion, and main() returns 0 unconditionally (line 213) — exit code is useless for automation despite this being a doc-referenced smoke test (config/README.md:152). Add a tol assert + nonzero exit.

item: slate_chol_trsm_bench.py:103-104 --nb '0 = n/p' default
evidence: nb=0 means 'defer to the wrapper default' — kw only gains block_size when args.nb is truthy (lines 132-134), so the coupling lives in the wrapper, not the bench. Minor inaccuracy found: the help text says 'n/p' but distributed_cholesky's actual default is n // max(p, q) (src/ffi/slate/cholesky.py:96), which differs on rectangular meshes (1x4: n/1 vs n/4); the bench's own log line 123 repeats the n//p formula. Cosmetic doc drift on top of a deliberate defer-to-default convention.

item: slate_cholesky_trsm_test.py:147-153 raw-buffer layout forensics
evidence: Index math verified for the enforced config: test hard-requires world==4/2x2 (lines 105-108). JAX row-major device reshape puts proc2 at mesh coord (1,0) owning gathered block rows[n/2:], cols[:n/2] (bottom-left) — exactly ul = L_raw_np[shape0//2:, :shape1//2] (line 151); SLATE GridOrder::Col assigns rank2 = SLATE tile (0,1) = strict-upper tile, so max|ul| ~ 0 iff potrf zeroed/never-touched the upper tile. Valid ONLY with default nb=n/2: passing --nb changes tile ownership and the printed number silently becomes meaningless. It is a print-only probe (no assertion) from the SLATE layout hunt; also the whole file returns 0 unconditionally (line 183). Debug residue, harmless, delete or gate on default-nb.

item: cusolvermp_solve_lu_test.py:157-165 LORRAX_LU_DEBUG_DUMP
evidence: Confirmed lines 156-165: env-gated ASCII zero-mask dump of X[0] plus |X-B| max, clearly residue of the A=I LU layout hunt (which cells came back zero). Off by default, does not touch the assertion path — real PASS/FAIL with exit code exists at lines 166-168. Harmless leftover.

item: cusolvermp_solve_lu_test.py sharding P(None,'x','y')
evidence: This is the documented contract, verified: batched_distributed_solve_lu docstring 'Input sharding: both A and B in P(None,'x','y')' (src/ffi/cusolvermp/batched.py:328) and module header (batched.py:13-16); batched.py:23-28 explicitly explains why the P('x',None,'y') sub-comm layout was retired in favor of the full-mesh layout. The reader had it backwards: it is the potrf sibling (cusolvermp_batched_test.py:59,77, P('x',None,'y')) that carries the stale sub-comm-era layout — its inputs get silently resharded by the wrapper's jit(shard_map(in_specs=P(None,'x','y'))) (batched.py:149-151). Worth a one-line comment, but the solve_lu test is the correct one.

item: cusolvermp_batched_test.py --nb plumbing
evidence: Stronger than claimed — nonzero --nb crashes outright. Lines 130-132 build kw['block_size']=args.nb and pass it to batched_distributed_cholesky, whose signature is (A, *, mesh) with NO block_size parameter (src/ffi/cusolvermp/batched.py:112-116; tile sizes are derived internally as n//Px, n//Py at :138-139). `--nb 64` raises TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'block_size'. The flag was evidently copied from the slate sibling, whose batched cholesky does accept block_size (src/ffi/slate/batched.py:94-98). Default --nb 0 hides it. Remove the flag or the kwarg plumbing.

item: eigh_benchmark.py:94 magic tile=256
evidence: Confirmed: tile = 256 hardcoded (line 94), reused for all three Mg configs, no sweep and no comment; the dedicated Mg test defaults to --tile 32 (cusolvermg_eigh_test.py:43), so 256 is an ad-hoc tuning pick. Zero-reference bench (see dead verdict), so the magic number is inert.

item: eigh_benchmark.py f64-only, never c128
evidence: Confirmed: make_symmetric builds float64 only (lines 41-44), no --dtype flag anywhere. Production eigensolves (zeta/W pipeline) are complex128, so the bench is non-representative — consistent with the claim it predates the c128 focus. Note the Mg wrapper only has an f64 FFI target anyway (ffi_loader.py:47 'lorrax_cusolvermg_eigh_f64'), so a c128 Mg bench is impossible today; the Mp branch could bench c128 but doesn't. Dead file; no action beyond deletion.

item: slate_eigh_test.py:135 W trim '[:args.n]'
evidence: Confirmed line 135. Eigenvalues are identical on every replica, so whether process_allgather returns a flat concatenation (trim yields exactly W) or a stacked (nproc,n) array (trim keeps some replica rows; the subsequent |W_np - W_ref| broadcast still compares identical copies), the max-abs comparison at line 139 cannot be corrupted — worst case is a shape quirk, not a wrong verdict. It papers over allgather replica semantics rather than resolving them, but for an eigenvalue-only check it is harmless.

item: slate_eigh_test.py:154-158 --kind diag |Q[i,j]|>0.5 probe
evidence: Confirmed lines 154-158. This is the live diagnostic for a documented, still-open bug: src/ffi/slate/__init__.py:16 says 'eigenvectors buggy; W ok', and PASS/FAIL (lines 163-164) is deliberately eigenvalue-only. The A=diag(1..n) probe with an index dump is exactly the tool for localizing an evec layout permutation. Same genus as the slate_cholesky_trsm raw-tile probe, but here it retains diagnostic value while the evec bug stays open.

item: wfn_loader_backend_parity_test.py --atol 1e-12 vs 'byte-for-byte' docstring
evidence: Confirmed: docstring line 3-5 promises byte-for-byte match; argparse default --atol 1e-12 (line 107); _check's own atol=0.0 default (line 87) is always overridden with args.atol (lines 137, 145) — only the gvecs check stays exact via np.array_equal (line 152). Both backends read the same HDF5 bytes and apply the same SymMaps math, so any nonzero Delta indicates a real transform-order divergence; 1e-12 absolute (~1e4 ULPs at O(1) psi coefficients) could mask one. Default should be 0.0 to match the P2 contract, or the docstring corrected.

item: wfn_loader_backend_parity_test.py:82-84 _replicate_to_host ndim-strip loop
evidence: Confirmed lines 79-84: process_allgather(arr, tiled=False) then `while h.ndim > arr.ndim: h = h[0]`. The h[0] is only correct if extra leading axes hold identical full replicas. Current multihost_utils replicates jax.Array inputs before gathering, so the assumption holds today; but under stacked-per-shard semantics h[0] would keep only shard 0 — and since BOTH backends pass through the same helper, the parity check would false-PASS on partial data. Could not execute on the login node to pin the pinned-JAX behavior, so: fragile assumption confirmed, consequence unverified. Prefer tiled=True or an explicit replicate-then-local-data pattern.

item: slate_vs_cusolvermp_bench.py _init_distributed drift
evidence: Both halves confirmed: lines 44-50 call jax.distributed.initialize with NO try/except (every sibling swallows; this one crashes loudly — arguably the better behavior) and the slate branch never calls lrx_slate_init_mpi eagerly, unlike slate_eigh_test.py:32-38 whose comment explains the eager call exists so MPI_THREAD_MULTIPLE inits 'BEFORE JAX touches anything, so PMI is fresh from srun'. src/ffi/slate/context.py:8-10 documents a lazy fallback (context.cc inits MPI if nobody has), so the bench relies on lazy init with a different ordering than every dedicated slate test. Runtime consequence unverifiable from the login node; the boilerplate drift is real and the ~15-file init-block cloning is the actual thing to fix (one shared helper).

item: slate_vs_cusolvermp_bench.py:106-119 duplicated kw construction
evidence: Confirmed literal duplication: both branches build kw = {'mesh': mesh, 'compute_evecs': True} + optional block_size (lines 108-110 vs 115-117). Both wrappers accept the identical kwarg set (cusolvermp/eigh.py:43-49; slate/eigh.py:24-29), so the kw dict could be hoisted above the branch; only the import must stay per-branch (same symbol name from different modules). Trivial copy-paste in a confirmed-dead file.

item: cublasmp_gemm_test.py:111 f64 tol 1e-6 vs c128 tol 1e-10
evidence: Confirmed line 111: `tol = 1e-10 if args.dtype == 'c128' else 1e-6`. No numerical justification exists: f64 and c128 have identical per-component mantissa precision, and a 64x64x64 gemm with O(1) entries should give relative error ~1e-14 for both dtypes. No comment in the file, nothing in ffi/cublasmp docs or reports explains the 4-decade gap — I could not verify whether the f64 path was ever observed less accurate (no f64 run logs found). As written, the loose tolerance could mask a genuine f64-path defect (e.g., a wrong-epilogue or accumulation-dtype bug up to 1e-6). Tighten to match c128 and see what falls out.

item: w_solve_modes_test.py:81 SimpleNamespace meta mock
evidence: Verified the full consumption surface: solve_w -> _resolve_w_solve_fn -> _w_solve_pref_scalar reads exactly meta.nk_tot, meta.nspin, meta.nspinor (src/gw/w_isdf.py:304-311, pref = 2/(sqrt(nk)*nspin*nspinor), with getattr defaults). The mock sets all three, so nothing consumed is missing — no current bug. But nspin=nspinor=1 means the bispinor prefactor scaling (nspinor=2 halves pref) is never exercised, exactly the mock hazard flagged in project memory. Parametrizing nspinor in {1,2} against the numpy reference is a two-line hardening.

item: w_solve_modes_test.py:85-90 _fresh identity-jit for donated chi0
evidence: Donation confirmed at the API: src/gw/w_isdf.py:226-230, `@partial(jax.jit, donate_argnums=(1,))` on _solve_w with an explicit rationale comment ('the caller releases chi0 right after'); the low_mem fused FFI donates V analogously (cublasmp/batched.py:209-210). The identity-jit duplicate is the correct caller-side idiom when reusing chi0 across two solve calls. Deliberate memory optimization, documented at both API sites — a sharp edge, but a chosen one.

item: cusolvermg_eigh_test.py:79-96 A^T ambiguity + decides-nothing residual check
evidence: Confirmed: comment (lines 80-84) admits the row-major buffer fed to the col-major API may yield eigenvectors of A^T; loop prints BOTH row-vec and col-vec residuals (lines 90-96) with no assertion; PASS/FAIL is eigenvalue-only (lines 98-100). Consequence-free today because there is no complex path to get wrong: ffi_loader.py:47 registers only 'lorrax_cusolvermg_eigh_f64', and src/ffi/AGENTS.md:10 validates F64 only — for real-symmetric A, evecs of A^T = evecs of A, so the ambiguity is unobservable. The hypothesis is right that a c128 Mg target would need this resolved (for Hermitian A, evecs of A^T are conjugated), but that target does not exist. Resolve the layout question if/when a complex Mg target is added.

item: cublasmp_w_solve_test.py:40 --pref_re 0.67 default
evidence: Confirmed line 40. The physical prefactor is 2/(sqrt(N_k)*n_spin*n_spinor) (src/gw/w_isdf.py:304-311); 0.67 matches no physical configuration — it is an arbitrary non-unit scalar. That arbitrariness is functionally useful: the reference solve (line 92) applies the same pref, so any FFI bug that drops or mis-applies the compile-time pref attribute (w_isdf.py:292-293 notes pref is a compile-time scalar attr) fails the 1e-10 check, which pref=1.0 would mask. Undocumented but harmless; a one-line comment would settle it.

item: cublasmp_w_solve_test.py:75 'V is donated' snapshot comment
evidence: Verified against the API: batched_fused_w_solve docstring documents 'V : ... DONATED (XLA reuses its buffer for the Cholesky factor X)' (src/ffi/cublasmp/batched.py:206-210). The snapshot-before-solve at lines 76-77 is the required caller pattern and the test comment simply mirrors the documented contract. Same deliberate donation family as solve_w's chi0 (w_isdf.py:230).

item: slate_trsm_isolated_test.py:95 side=R/uplo=U/op=N special-case print
evidence: Confirmed: the file sweeps all 8 side/uplo/op combos against one known X_true (lines 81-94) — only one combo can mathematically recover X, so this is a which-convention-is-it forensic harness, and the full-matrix dump gated on exactly (n<=4, side='R', uplo='U', op='N') at line 95 marks the combo under suspicion during the historical hunt. Prints both |X-Xt| and |X.T-Xt| (transpose ambiguity probing), always returns 0. Question settled since handle.to_jax_lower() landed (used at slate_cholesky_trsm_test.py:131, slate_chol_trsm_bench.py:177). Pure residue; delete with the file.

item: eigh_block_sweep.py:4-7 permuted-eigenvector docstring
evidence: Confirmed docstring lines 3-7 and eigenvalue-only validation (line 88). Key finding: this is not the sweep's private hack — the production wrapper documents the identical behavior (src/ffi/cusolvermp/eigh.py:64-70: smaller block_size 'return[s] eigenvectors in a block-cyclic permutation of the input basis', with the 2.4x-at-n=16k tuning note). A timing sweep validating only eigenvalues under a documented layout caveat is coherent. The landmine assessment stands conditionally: any future production adoption of distributed_eigh with tuned block_size must un-permute Q — but the op currently has zero production callers, so the trap is unarmed.

item: potrs_rhs_test.py:57-59 stale X_dagger/w_isdf comment
evidence: Confirmed lines 57-59: comment ties B to w_isdf's X_dagger upper-tri structure while the code builds generic complex B — and the comment itself concedes 'test with generic complex for now'. Context recovered: this file was the isolation harness for the potrs NRHS<=N bug now documented at src/ffi/cusolvermp/batched.py:182-189 (the w_isdf.solve_w_low_mem workaround). Historical context residue in a zero-reference file.

item: potrs_rhs_test.py:38-41 argparse-in-main with parse_known_args
evidence: Confirmed lines 38-41: parser constructed mid-main, parse_known_args silently tolerates stray argv, nq/n hardcoded at line 37, and the file has no PASS/FAIL and unconditionally returns 0 (line 79). Quick-hack isolation harness, superseded by cusolvermp_batched_test (which asserts); consistent with the confirmed-dead verdict on the file.

item: chol_natural_test.py:59-72 print-only verification, q=0-only numpy ref
evidence: Confirmed with one nuance: the L L^dagger = A reconstruction and upper-triangle-zero checks ARE computed for every batch slice (lines 61-68), so the claim's 'batch slice 0 only' applies only to the direct numpy-Cholesky comparison (lines 70-72). No tolerance assertion anywhere, unconditional return 0 (line 73). Quick verification script for the test-only accessor, never hardened; if the accessor is kept as public API the test should assert, otherwise delete both.

item: isdf_zeta_mode_test.py:98-104 caller-side per-mode reshard + line-number comment
evidence: Confirmed and substantiated: the 'line ~1350' comment is currently accurate (isdf_fitting.py:1354: z_col_shard = P(None,None,('x','y'))) but is a rot-prone line-number coupling. solve_zeta's docstring already blesses two Z_q input shardings ('P(None,'x','y') or P(None,None,('x','y')) if caller already resharded', isdf_fitting.py:1252-1254) and the cusolvermp branches handle sharding internally (:1284-1288) — so input-reshard ownership is split between caller and callee. Extra wrinkle: the test's low_mem reshard P('x',None,'y') (line 101) is the retired per-X-row sub-comm layout that batched.py:23-28 documents dropping in favor of full-mesh P(None,'x','y'). Consolidating resharding inside solve_zeta (single owner) is the right refactor, per the unified-sym-action/no-parallel-paths principle.

item: isdf_zeta_mode_test.py:108 q_chunk_size=1024
evidence: Confirmed line 108. solve_zeta's default is q_chunk_size=1 (isdf_fitting.py:1218, minimum-memory mode); inside, q_batch = min(q_chunk_size, nq) (isdf_fitting.py:1357), so with the test's default --nq 8 the 1024 collapses to 8 = full parallelism / chunking disabled. Arbitrary large sentinel where q_chunk_size=args.nq would say the intent; harmless, uncommented.

item: symmetry_test.py:7-8 WFNReader alias + relative import
evidence: Confirmed: line 7 `from file_io import WfnLoader as WFNReader`, line 8 relative `.symmetry_maps` (siblings use absolute `common.` imports, e.g. isdf_zeta_mode_test.py:48). The alias is not unique to this file — the refactor map lists gw/gw_jax.py:21, centroid/kmeans_cli.py, bse/bse_io.py etc. still using WFNReader, so this is repo-wide legacy convention, not a local sin. Purely cosmetic; also note (contra the general tenor of the suspect list) this file is one of the few with a proper PASS/FAIL report and nonzero exit on failure (lines 43, 58).

item: src/runtime/aot_memory.py:449 query_cufft_workspace_bytes — docstring says 'Sum', code returns max
evidence: Only the summary line (aot_memory.py:450) is stale. The same docstring's body (lines 453-457) explicitly documents 'Returns the **max** across distinct plans rather than the sum, since at runtime only one plan's scratch is live at a time', and this is corroborated in two other places: module docstring line 20 ('cufft_scratch (max over distinct plans)') and AotPeakBreakdown.cufft_scratch doc (lines 88-91, 'Max plan workspace... steady state — only one plan's scratch is live at any moment'). Max is the physically right model for jaxlib: FFT thunks execute serially on one stream and workspace is allocated per-execution, and tests/test_aot_memory.py's GPU-gated test_predicted_peak asserts the predicted total matches the OBSERVED per-rank peak, which would fail if sum were needed. Implementation deliberate; fix is a one-word docstring edit (Sum -> Max).

item: src/runtime/aot_memory.py:507-512 aot_kernel_peak_bytes — CufftQueryError silently demoted to cufft_scratch=0
evidence: The demotion is deliberate (comment lines 503-506 'chooser still works on CPU sandboxes') but the except is broader than the documented scope. Docstring lines 487-489 claim the omission applies 'On non-CUDA backends or when libcufft cannot be located', yet CufftQueryError is also raised for on-GPU failures: cufftCreate rc!=0 (line 387), cufftSetAutoAllocation rc!=0 (line 392), batch>=2^31 (lines 400-404), unsupported dtype combo (lines 272-274), cufftMakePlanMany rc!=0 (lines 440-443). Any of these on a real GPU box yields cufft_scratch=0 -> silent under-prediction, exactly the failure the module exists to fix (its own HloFftParseError doc, lines 103-110, calls silent degrade unacceptable for the HLO case). Compounding: the sole production caller (src/gw/v_q_tile.py, function ending ~line 232) wraps the entire prediction in 'except Exception: return None' falling back to the slope+intercept model, so even the 'loud' HLO parse errors are silenced one frame up. Should narrow the except to the backend-unavailable errors (libcufft-not-found, jax.devices('gpu') failure) and raise or at least warn-once on plan-query rc failures when a CUDA backend is live.

item: src/runtime/aot_memory.py:235-241,277-331,400-404 — cuFFT hex type codes, /proc/self/maps discovery, batch<2^31 guard
evidence: All six hex codes match cuda/include/cufft.h exactly: CUFFT_R2C=0x2a, CUFFT_C2R=0x2c, CUFFT_C2C=0x29, CUFFT_D2Z=0x6a, CUFFT_Z2D=0x6c, CUFFT_Z2Z=0x69 (verified against the standard header values; the file cites its source at line 235). /proc/self/maps discovery is a documented design choice (module docstring lines 12-14 and 32-34): it guarantees the queried libcufft.so is bit-identical to the one jaxlib dlopen'd, eliminating version drift; Linux-only is fine for the deployment targets (Perlmutter, local WSL — see sandbox memory budget table). The batch guard (lines 400-404) raises loudly with an explicit pointer to cufftMakePlanMany64 and is unreachable at production sizes (batch = leading dims product, q_chunk-scale, nowhere near 2^31). One caveat inherited from the previous finding: that 'loud' raise is a CufftQueryError, so it currently degrades to scratch=0 at aot_kernel_peak_bytes:511 instead of surfacing.

item: src/runtime/padding.py:107 logical_shape_from_padded
evidence: Enumerated all call mechanisms, zero live references: (1) direct/from-import — grep across src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/ hits only the definition (padding.py:107) and __all__ (padding.py:327); tests/test_padding.py imports six padding names explicitly (lines 27-33) and this is not among them; (2) wildcard — no 'from runtime.padding import *' anywhere; (3) string dispatch — no getattr/dict/config references to the name; (4) CLI — pyproject.toml [project.scripts] wires only gw.gw_jax:main and centroid.kmeans_cli:main; (5) docs — no mention under docs/; (6) sandbox — grep across /pscratch/sd/j/jackm/lorrax_sandbox skills/, runs/, templates/ found nothing; the only sandbox-wide hits are identical copies of padding.py in sibling clones (sources/blitz_workspaces/blitz{4,5,6}*) and the first-pass audit report itself. Self-described as 'mainly a documentation hook' (padding.py:113-114). Nothing anywhere calls it.

item: src/runtime/padding.py — pad_shape_to_mesh, pad_array_to_mesh, unpad_array_from_mesh, valid_shape_from_pad_meta, PadAxis
evidence: Only callers in this checkout are tests/test_padding.py (explicit imports, lines 27-33; exercised throughout the file). Verified no other mechanism: no wildcard imports of runtime.padding, no string-based dispatch on these names, no pyproject entry points, no docs usage examples (docs/ mentions of 'padding' are unrelated mu-padding theory notes), no references in lorrax_sandbox skills/, runs/, or templates/. Corroborating intent: src/file_io/slab_io.py:188-193 docstring says 'Driver-side padding/unpadding is the caller's responsibility (see runtime.padding in the agent/padding-refactor branch)' — the consuming refactor is on another branch, not this checkout; git 7474eb7 landed these helpers ahead of the consumer. NOT confirmed-dead because they are pre-landed scaffolding with a named future consumer. Important nuance the first-pass got right by exclusion: sibling function round_up_to_mesh_product in the same module IS production-alive (src/common/load_wfns.py:360 import, :418 call computing n_rmu_padded), so the module must not be deleted wholesale.

item: src/runtime/padding.py:203,268 — import jax inside pad_array_to_mesh / unpad_array_from_mesh bodies
evidence: The deferral is load-bearing, not style. Module top level imports only dataclasses/typing (padding.py:30-33), keeping the jax-free half (pad_shape_to_mesh, round_up_to_mesh_product) importable and usable without triggering jax import — and the one production caller uses exactly that half (load_wfns.py:360 does a local 'from runtime.padding import round_up_to_mesh_product'). The runtime package's documented contract is 'set_default_env() BEFORE import jax' (runtime/__init__.py:15-22, :50-51), and I verified in the checkout's venv that jax genuinely snapshots env vars at import: jax/_src/config.py:475 'default = os.getenv(name.upper(), default)' runs once at config-state creation, so a premature jax import permanently misses JAX_ENABLE_X64/JAX_PLATFORMS. runtime/__init__.py itself follows the same convention (import jax deferred inside init_jax_distributed:135, nccl_warmup:177-178, fallback_to_cpu_if_no_gpu_backend:206).

item: src/runtime/__init__.py:40-44 — nccl_warmup missing from __all__
evidence: __all__ (lines 40-44) lists set_default_env, init_jax_distributed, fallback_to_cpu_if_no_gpu_backend; nccl_warmup (defined line 155) is absent, and the module docstring's usage recipe (lines 15-22) doesn't mention it either. No functional impact: its one caller uses an explicit name import ('from runtime import nccl_warmup', src/gw/gw_driver_helpers.py:148), which __all__ does not gate; __all__ only affects 'import *', and grep shows nobody wildcard-imports runtime. Consistent with added-later drift (nccl_warmup's docstring references a specific profiling finding, a 1.9 s all-reduce-start in the sigma phase). One-line fix if desired.

item: src/runtime/__init__.py:38,127-152 — idempotence sentinel is an env var (_LORRAX_JAX_DISTRIBUTED_DONE), inherited by children
evidence: Explicitly documented as the point of the design in two places: module docstring lines 26-30 and init_jax_distributed docstring lines 111-116 — module-level Python globals do NOT survive the 'python -m gw.gw_jax' -> gw_init -> re-import-gw.gw_jax cycle (the module exists twice under different names), while an env var does; the previous per-driver copies double-initialised for exactly this reason. The child-process footgun is latent, not manifest in this codebase: enumerated all subprocess/fork use in src/ — scontrol (runtime/__init__.py:95), nvidia-smi (common/gpu_utils.py:19), compiler/toolchain invocations (file_io/_slab_io_ffi.py:60, common/isdf_fitting.py:116,2477) — none spawn a Python child that needs its own jax.distributed.initialize, and no multiprocessing use exists. SLURM ranks are spawned by slurmd from the batch-script environment, which never contains the sentinel (it is set inside each rank process at line 132/142/152). Would only bite if LORRAX ever fork/spawns jax worker children; worth a comment, not a change.

item: src/runtime/__init__.py:101,144 — two bare 'except Exception: pass' fallback ladders
evidence: Both are deliberate retry-down ladders (docstrings lines 84-87 and 122-125 document the fallback order), and neither fully swallows terminal errors — the final initialize at lines 147-151 raises loudly if it also fails. But each discards diagnostics with a concrete cost. (1) Line 144: the first jax.distributed.initialize's traceback is dropped; if the explicit-args retry then fails for the SAME root cause, the operator debugs the wrong (second) error. (2) Line 101 is worse than lost logging: if scontrol fails while SLURM_NODELIST is set (PATH issue, scontrol timeout), the fallback at lines 103-106 uses SLURMD_NODENAME — which on a multi-NODE job resolves to each rank's OWN node, so ranks on different nodes compute DIFFERENT coordinator addresses and the job hangs in coordinator connect instead of printing an error. A masked exception converting into a silent multi-node hang is the failure scenario; minimum fix is logging the swallowed exception and preferring rank-0-consistent host resolution.

item: src/runtime/__init__.py:100,106 — hardcoded coordinator port 12355
evidence: Real but latent collision hazard, with mitigations that narrow the window. Reachable only on a doubly-degraded path: proc_count>1 (line 131 short-circuits single-process) AND the first local_device_ids initialize (line 141) raised, forcing the explicit-coordinator fallback (lines 147-151); the primary path lets jax derive its own coordinator/port from SLURM env. Escape hatch JAX_COORDINATOR_ADDRESS overrides everything (lines 89-91). However, the collision scenario is realistic in THIS sandbox: the documented A-D agent workflow shares SLURM allocations via lxattach, so two concurrent multi-process LORRAX jobs can land on the same first host and both bind :12355 — second job fails or, worse, connects to the first job's coordinator. 12355 is the copy-pasted tutorial port (torch/jax examples). Fix: derive port from SLURM_JOB_ID (e.g. 12000 + jobid % 2000), one line.

item: src/runtime/__init__.py:198-214 fallback_to_cpu_if_no_gpu_backend — mutates JAX_PLATFORMS/JAX_PLATFORM_NAME after import jax
evidence: The first-pass hypothesis ('works only because backend init is lazy') is WRONG — the remediation cannot work at all on the pinned jax>=0.9, verified by reading the checkout's own venv jax sources. Per-step trace: (1) the caught message 'Unknown backend: 'gpu'' originates in canonicalize_platform (venv jax/_src/xla_bridge.py:750) and is reached from no-arg jax.devices() only via _get_backend_uncached line 940: platform = _XLA_BACKEND.value or _PLATFORM_NAME.value — i.e. only when the legacy JAX_PLATFORM_NAME=gpu (or jax_xla_backend) is set. (2) _PLATFORM_NAME captures os.getenv('JAX_PLATFORM_NAME') once at import (xla_bridge.py:76-80), and config.jax_platforms captures JAX_PLATFORMS once at config-state creation (config.py:475 'default = os.getenv(name.upper(), default)'). Therefore os.environ.pop('JAX_PLATFORM_NAME')/os.environ['JAX_PLATFORMS']='cpu' at __init__.py:211-212 change nothing; the caller's promised 'next jax.devices()' (line 203) recomputes platform='gpu' from the same snapshotted flag and raises the identical RuntimeError — crash merely deferred one call. (3) In the canonical flow the branch never fires anyway: set_default_env sets JAX_PLATFORMS='cuda,cpu', and xla_bridge.py:828-829 silently skips cuda when hardware_utils.has_visible_nvidia_gpu() is false, so CPU sandboxes get a working cpu backend with no exception. Net: happy path no-op, targeted path ineffective. Correct fix is jax.config.update('jax_platforms', 'cpu') and jax.config.update('jax_platform_name', '') — or delete the function since jax's own 'cuda,cpu' fallback already provides the documented behavior.

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_solve.py:372-377 — _sternheimer_solve_jvp double-negation of tangent RHS
evidence: Reader's 'correct but a trap' claim is wrong: the b_dot term has a flipped sign. Derivation: _sternheimer_core(op,s) solves A·x = −s (line 249 'rhs = -b'), so the primal x = sternheimer_solve(op,b) satisfies A·x = −b. Differentiating: Ȧx + Aẋ = −ḃ ⟹ A·ẋ = −ḃ − Ȧx. The code passes rhs_tangent = −(b_dot − A_dot_x) (line 376), so the core solves A·ẋ = −rhs_tangent = +ḃ − Ȧx: Ȧx term correct, ḃ term sign-flipped (error = 2·A⁻¹ḃ). The rule is consistent only with the module docstring's mis-stated primal 'A(θ)x(θ) = b(θ)' (line 19); the actual primal negates b. Why undetected: every FD-vs-JVP validation passes a FROZEN b with zero tangent — src/psp/tests/test_sternheimer_jvp.py:294-313 (chi_fn captures pk['b'] as a constant; only kvec_p_traced varies) and runs/MoS2/02_mos2_3x3_nosym/B_sternheimer_smoke/unfreeze_projector_test.py (b=pk['b'] frozen even in 'unfrozen' mode, which unfreezes only the operator via U_val_grad_kp). The only b_dot≠0 producer is the unfrozen-source path src/psp/run_sternheimer.py:765-768 (b_eff = Q(U_val_eff)·Vu_G_preQ, U_val_eff traced), whose sole caller (s_tensor_fd_bench.py:125) does FD-of-JVP and so cannot detect a consistent internal JVP sign error. Production _per_k_chi (run_sternheimer.py:891-899) uses frozen b, so shipped χ/∂χ∂q results are unaffected. Fix: rhs_tangent = b_dot + A_dot_x.

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_solve.py:214 — 1e-8 denominator clamp in _schur_initial_guess
evidence: Lines 211-215: comment explicitly documents the guard ('crossings between extra-Ritz and occupied energies... For a proper insulator this never happens, but include a clamp so a degenerate band doesn't blow up'). Two uses: (1) CG warm-start (_sternheimer_core line 251) — x0 is arbitrary, CG converges to the same solution regardless, and WITHOUT the clamp a near-zero denominator would inject inf/NaN into x0 and poison the whole CG state, so the clamp is required for robustness there; (2) sos_only closed-form mode (cond_subspace_sos_solve, lines 171-185) — zeroing the near-degenerate SoS term is the standard degenerate-skip convention, and for the insulators this mode targets, denom = eps_extra − eps_v ≥ gap (~0.1-1 Ry) ≫ 1e-8 Ry, so the branch is never taken. Silent zeroing would only matter for metallic/degenerate systems in sos_only mode, which is out of scope for the mode's stated purpose (cond-N convergence study, docstring lines 179-183).

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_solve.py:262 — dead-band threshold _DEAD = 1e-14, dtype-independent
evidence: Line 246 computes dtype_r = float32 for complex64 inputs and line 262 casts the constant to dtype_r, so float32 support is deliberately coded — but 1e-14 is calibrated for float64 noise floors. In complex64, a 'numerically zero' source from cancellation sits at ~1e-7·scale ≫ 1e-14, so alive0 (line 263) never masks it and the guard is inert: CG then grinds on noise rows to max_iter (the per-band freeze at line 280 uses tol·‖b_noise‖, unreachable), which is exactly the 'MINRES-style noise amplification' the comment at lines 239-241 says the mask prevents. Not a demonstrated wrong-answer bug: for noise-b rows, CG still returns the bounded solution A⁻¹b_noise (‖A⁻¹‖ ~ 1/gap), so contamination is noise-level; the cost is wasted iterations and lost robustness on the float32 path. Also the threshold is absolute, not relative to typical ‖b‖. Should be dtype/scale-aware, e.g. c·finfo(dtype_r).eps·max(b_norm).

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_solve.py:26 — 'the other-agent gave in the Stage-3 writeup' in module docstring
evidence: Confirmed at lines 24-26: 'This follows the "short-circuit the primal, not the implicit derivative" advice the other-agent gave in the Stage-3 writeup.' Agent-conversation residue with no in-repo referent (no Stage-3 writeup ships with the source); adjacent 'From the guide' (line 17) is similar residue. Harmless to execution but should be excised. NOTE while fixing: the same docstring block (lines 19-20) states the primal as 'A(θ) x(θ) = b(θ)' whereas the code solves A·x = −b (line 249) — this mis-statement is the plausible root cause of the b_dot sign bug reported above, so the docstring cleanup should also correct the stated convention.

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_precond.py — make_tpa_preconditioner
evidence: Enumerated all call mechanisms in lorrax_D: (a) imports/calls — only tests/test_sternheimer_solvers.py:143,147,230,245; (b) production — zero: src/psp/run_sternheimer.py:74-77 imports only compute_per_band_kinetic + tpa_preconditioner_diag, and src/psp/tests/test_sternheimer_jvp.py:62 likewise; (c) string dispatch — grep for getattr/quoted name across src/ finds only the __all__ entry (sternheimer_precond.py:136); (d) CLI — pyproject.toml [project.scripts] has only lorrax-gw/gw_jax/lorrax-centroids, none reach it; (e) docs — cg_posdef.py:100 and minres.py:102 are :func: docstring cross-references, not calls; (f) sandbox skills/ — zero hits; (g) sandbox runs/ — only runs/MoS2/02_mos2_3x3_nosym/B_sternheimer_smoke/stern_diag3.py:18,67 and stern_diag5.py:19,57, which are one-off NaN-trace debug scripts hardwired via sys.path.insert to the lorrax_B checkout (not lorrax_D), i.e. bench/diagnostic artifacts, while all other smoke scripts there (run_full.py, jvp_*, s_tensor_*, schur_*) use tpa_preconditioner_diag. Not confirmed-dead (tests + archived diagnostics would break), but zero live production references.

item: src/solvers/sternheimer_precond.py:93 and :123 — duplicated K_bar_sq>0 → 1.0 clamp in both variants
evidence: Confirmed exact duplication: tpa_preconditioner_diag (lines 93-95) and make_tpa_preconditioner (lines 123-125) both compute K_safe = jnp.where(K_bar_sq > 0.0, K_bar_sq, 1.0); x = T_diag_kminq[None,:]/K_safe[:,None]; _tpa(x)[:,None,:] — the factory merely wraps the identical weights in a jitted closure (lines 127-131). This is a parallel old/new API pair: the closure form serves the legacy cg_posdef/minres static-callable interface (per docstrings cg_posdef.py:100, minres.py:102) while the array form was split out for the JIT-stable primitive (sternheimer_precond.py:89-92 says 'Equivalent to the factory below'). Since make_tpa_preconditioner is test-only (see dead verdict), the clean fix is to delete it and port the two test call sites (tests/test_sternheimer_solvers.py:147,245) to wrap tpa_preconditioner_diag, or at minimum implement the factory as 'weights = tpa_preconditioner_diag(T_diag_kminq, K_bar_sq)' so the clamp/formula has a single source of truth.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: _apply_kinetic_velocity_Gbox (L182)
evidence: Definition-only at get_dipole_mtxels.py:182. Enumerated call mechanisms: (a) direct/from-import — grep across all *.py/*.sh/*.md/*.toml/*.yaml in lorrax_D (incl. src/psp/tests, tools, docs) hits only the def line; (b) string dispatch — no getattr/globals()[] in the module; (c) CLI — pyproject [project.scripts] exposes only gw_jax/lorrax-gw/lorrax-centroids; (d) sandbox — grep over lorrax_sandbox/skills and runs/: zero hits. The live finite-q path explicitly replaced it: compute_finite_q_mtxels imports apply_kinetic_velocity_to_ket from psp.dft_operators (L311) and uses it at L356-357, and its docstring L293-295 says 'G-sphere throughout (no FFT box)'. Leading underscore rules out documented public API.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: _cell_overlaps_at_q_Gbox (L225)
evidence: Definition-only at get_dipole_mtxels.py:225. Same exhaustive mechanism sweep as above (imports, getattr, console_scripts, tests, sandbox skills/runs, run.sh files): zero references. Its live successor _cell_overlap_with_lookup (L137, G-sphere + umklapp integer lookup) is called at L408 inside compute_finite_q_mtxels. The Gbox pair (_apply_kinetic_velocity_Gbox + _cell_overlaps_at_q_Gbox) is a self-contained dead FFT-box path; note _cell_overlaps_at_q_Gbox is also kinetic-only (docstring L228) while the live path includes VNL, so it is not even functionally equivalent — safe to delete both.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: compute_projected_momentum_bgw_like / compute_block_direct_cnk (L95-100)
evidence: Both are 2-line tombstones whose only body is `raise NotImplementedError("Debug-only ... removed")` (L95-100). Grep across lorrax_D (src, tests, tools, docs, pyproject) and the sandbox (skills/, runs/ incl. all run.sh): the def lines are the only occurrences. No getattr/config-string dispatch exists in the module. Any hypothetical caller would crash immediately, so they cannot be silently load-bearing. Safe to delete.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: compute_p_operator_k / compute_vnl_velocity_cart
evidence: First-pass claim is wrong on two counts. (1) Both are called from main() of the same module — compute_p_operator_k at L587, compute_vnl_velocity_cart at L630 — and main() is a live CLI entry: `python -m psp.get_dipole_mtxels -i cohsex.in` appears in skills/execute_workflow/SKILL.md:194,313 and in ~8 sandbox run scripts (e.g. runs/Si_pseudobands/00_si_2x2x2_60Ry/cohsex_valcond_2026-04-18/run_lorrax.sh:92). 'Only used by main()' of a module whose main is the production dipole driver is alive, not dead. (2) External importers exist inside the sandbox working dirs: runs/MoS2/02_mos2_3x3_nosym/B_sternheimer_smoke/{p_only_check.py:79, vnl_velocity_direct.py:28, sos_signcorrected.py:23} import compute_vnl_velocity_cart, and lorrax_B_orbmag_wt/src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py:60 imports both. Deleting them from lorrax_D breaks the dipole pipeline and the orbmag merge.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: vNL global sign flip at two sites (main L636, compute_finite_q_mtxels L366/369)
evidence: Verified both sites. compute_vnl_velocity_cart returns +dV_NL/dk (physical); main() L636 does `vNL_cart = -vNL_cart` so dipole.h5 stores p−vNL — the BGW k.p / Liu-2024 Eq.17 convention (comment L632-635: 'Flip here so users don't need --vnl-scale=-1.0 when comparing to BGW'). compute_finite_q_mtxels L364-371 applies `-vnl_ops.apply_vnl_velocity_to_ket(...)` with a comment cross-referencing main(). Project memory confirms: physical dH/dk = p+vNL, the dipole driver's p−vNL is a deliberate BGW convention; the physical-sign consumer (orbital_magnetization.py L578, 598) uses the UNflipped +dV_NL/dk and documents 'verified +dV_NL/dk off-diagonally (ratio 1.000)'. So the flip is intentional and correct. Two caveats for the refactor: (a) the in-file comment L633 ('our internal assembly returns v^NL = -(∂q+∂q')V_NL') states the internal sign backwards — it contradicts both orbital_magnetization.py:578 and runs/MoS2/.../vnl_velocity_direct.py:55 ('this returns +(∂q+∂q')V_NL'); the code is right, the prose is wrong. (b) Two independent flip sites should collapse to one (e.g. a bgw_sign constant applied at output assembly).

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: _build_g_lookup pure-Python dict loop (L107-133)
evidence: Confirmed. L125 builds a python dict of nG_kmq tuples and L129-132 does a python for-loop of nG_k dict lookups; called at L404 inside the double loop `for jq in iq_list` (up to nk) × `for ik in range(nk_full)` (L388, L396). Total host work = nq·nk·nG tuple-hash lookups plus per-pair dict construction: at 6×6×1 (nk=nq=36, nG~1e4) ~2.6e7 ops (seconds), at a 12×12×4 grid (nk=576) ~6e9 ops (hours) — a genuine wall-clock landmine on dense grids. Mitigations: only reached with --with-finite-q (L706); the dict for a given ikmq is rebuilt per (ik,jq) even though it depends only on ikmq — caching per-ikmq dicts alone gives ~nk×; a vectorized np.searchsorted over lexsorted G-lists removes the python loop entirely. Correctness is fine (unmatched G masked to zero, L130-132, matching docstring L113-116).

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: inline G_wrap recompute vs unused umklapp_G_wrap import (L310, L400)
evidence: Confirmed leftover. L310 imports `kminq_idx_for_iq, umklapp_G_wrap` from common.kq_mapping; umklapp_G_wrap is never used in the file. L400 computes `G_wrap_np = np.round((kvec_k_np - qvec) - kvec_kmq_np).astype(np.int32)` — element-for-element identical to kq_mapping.py:68-70 `jnp.round((kvec_full - qvec) - kvec_kmq_full).astype(jnp.int32)`, just numpy-scalar instead of jnp-batched (the helper is batched over all k; the loop wants one k at a time, which may be why it was inlined). Same sign convention, same rounding, no correctness issue. Fix in refactor: either call the helper once outside the ik loop (it is batched over k, so `umklapp_G_wrap(kpts_full, kpts_full[kminq_idx], qvec)` replaces all nk inline computes per q) or drop the import.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: bispinor V_NL zero-pad to full nspinor (L373-378)
evidence: Confirmed the assumption, and it is the codebase-wide bispinor convention, not a local hack. The VNL apply consumes only the first nspinor_E (=E_super.shape[0], the physical 2-spinor block) components (L366-371 slices psi[:, :nspinor_E]); the pad (L373-378) puts zeros in the remaining (small/bispinor) components so v_kin (full nspinor, L356-357) + v_NL add with matching shapes — i.e. V_NL acts as V_NL⊗P_upper. Exactly the same slice-to-physical-block convention appears in compute_vnl_matrix_from_setup L67-70, compute_vnl_velocity_cart L88-91, and orbital_magnetization.py run_ibz L222-223. This matches the bispinor design: the scalar-relativistic UPF V_NL is defined on the 2-component block; small components enter via kinetic balance and the separate DHF/Breit machinery. Sound given that design; worth one comment stating 'V_NL is upper-block by construction' at the pad site.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: except Exception → k_red = int(i) fallback (L640-643)
evidence: The except is unreachable today: SymMaps sets irr_idx_k unconditionally on both constructor paths (symmetry_maps.py:766 no-symmetry branch, :867 symmetry branch via find_symmetry_ops_simple), i ranges over sym.nk_tot = len(irr_idx_k), and orbital_magnetization.py:94 already indexes sym.irr_idx_k with no guard. So no current input triggers it. However the fallback is the WRONG defensive choice: for a symmetry-reduced WFN (nk_irr < nk_full), k_red=i would silently pull the wrong irreducible energies into deltaE for i < nk_irr (and IndexError only for i ≥ nk_irr), i.e. partial silent corruption if the attribute were ever renamed. Recommend deleting the try/except in the refactor and letting it fail loudly; classifying benign-cruft (dead defensive code) rather than suspected-bug because it cannot fire in the current codebase.

item: src/psp/get_dipole_mtxels.py :: mixed tab / 4-space indentation
evidence: Confirmed by reading the raw file: compute_p_operator_k..compute_vnl_velocity_cart (L41-92) and main() (L428-750) are tab-indented; the tombstones (L95-100), the whole finite-q block (L107-421), and the __main__ guard (L754) are 4-space. Python 3 only forbids mixing within one block, so this is legal; it does confirm the finite-q section (and tombstones) were appended in a later editing pass. Purely cosmetic — normalize during the refactor.

item: src/psp/ionic_gspace.py :: exp(-0.25·G²) LR damping (L251-252)
evidence: Verified against the radial-table build: radial_tables.py vloc_sr_table (L51-55) and build_all_tables (L178-180) add +Z·e²·erf(r)/r (unit-width Gaussian smearing, η=1) into the short-range table; the analytic Fourier transform of that added term is 4πZe²/(Ω q²)·e^{−q²/4}, and L251-252 subtracts exactly that: (sqrtN·4π·e2/vol)·rho_ion_G/G²·exp(−G²/4) with rho_ion_G = −Σ Z·S (L250) and e2=2 in Ry. So 0.25 = 1/(4η²) with η=1 — the QE vloc_of_g SR/LR split, and it is self-consistent with the table on the other side. The predecessor build_projectors_qe.py:424 docstring documents the identical scheme ('adds Z e2 erf(r)/r ... Adds LR Coulomb tail ... with Gaussian damping'). Not a bug; the magic 0.25 deserves a one-line comment naming η=1, since changing the erf width in radial_tables without touching this constant would silently break V_loc.

item: src/psp/ionic_gspace.py :: tables['vloc'][i,0] = alpha_z·vol/(4π), skipped for truncation_2d (L174-177)
evidence: Verified. alpha_z (radial_tables.py:101-104) integrates r·(r·vloc + Z·e²) = QE's α·Z G=0 term; with the vloc prefactor sqrtN·4π/vol (L189) the q=0 table override yields exactly sqrtN·α_Z per atom — the QE-compatible G=0 average of V_loc. In-place mutation of the table dict is ugly but done once, host-side, before shipping to GPU. The 2D exception is coherent: under 2D truncation the G=0 LR component is removed by convention — the cutoff factor is forced to 0 at G=0 (L259 jnp.where) and Vloc_lr[0] is set to 0 unconditionally (L261) — matching QE's Coul_cut_2D handling (the old builder's docstring, build_projectors_qe.py ~L434-440, states this explicitly: 'matches QE's Coul_cut_2D module for assume_isolated=2D'). Skipping the α_Z override in 2D avoids reintroducing an average the truncation scheme defines away. Intentional; add a comment citing Coul_cut_2D.

item: src/psp/ionic_gspace.py :: lz = π/B_cart[2,2] in 2D truncation (L257)
evidence: Confirmed the z-aligned-c assumption, but it is the inherited QE convention, not an invention: for c ∥ ẑ, B_cart[2,2] = 2π/c so lz = c/2 (half slab height) and cutoff = 1 − e^{−|G_xy|·lz}·cos(G_z·lz) (L258) is exactly QE Coul_cut_2D / Sohier-style 2D truncation; the old builder it replaces uses the identical formula and documents 'lz = π / b_z ... matches QE's Coul_cut_2D module' (build_projectors_qe.py docstring). QE's assume_isolated='2D' itself requires the slab normal along z with c ⊥ the plane, so the restriction is upstream-standard. It IS silently wrong for an oblique c-axis — there is no assert that bvec[2] has zero in-plane components — so add a cheap guard during the refactor, but do not change the formula.

item: src/psp/ionic_gspace.py :: two FFT normalizations in _ionic_gspace_jit (L240 vs L263)
evidence: Confirmed and worked through: rho_core_r = Re[ifftn(ρ_G)]·N (L240) = Σ_G ρ_G e^{iG·r} (numpy ifftn divides by N; ×N restores the plain sum). V_loc_r = Re[ifftn(V_G, norm='ortho')] (L263) = (1/√N)Σ_G V_G e^{iG·r}, and the missing √N is pre-baked into the PHYSICAL prefactors: vloc_pf = sqrtN·4π/vol (L189) and the LR prefactor sqrtN·4π·2/vol (L251). Net result is the same Σ_G convention for both outputs — no numerical bug (also note rho_core_G_scaled = ρ_G·N at L241 is a third scaling handed to callers). But the √N buried inside 'physical' constants is exactly the trap the suspect names: anyone unifying to a single FFT helper (the flat-k/NUFFT refactor direction in project memory) who normalizes the FFT will double- or under-count by √N unless they also strip the prefactors. Normalize both to one convention with the scale applied at the FFT call site.

item: src/psp/ionic_gspace.py :: module docstring L8-9 references build_core_density / build_local_ionic_potential_on_G_total
evidence: The first-pass claim ('which no longer exist in lorrax_D') is FALSE — I disprove the stale-docstring reading. build_local_ionic_potential_on_G_total exists at src/psp/radial/build_projectors_qe.py:424 and is ACTIVELY used by get_DFT_mtxels.py:548,701, gw/kin_ion_io.py:158, and tests/test_dft_hamiltonian.py:88; build_core_density exists at src/psp/archive/charge_density.py:184, used by tests/test_dft_hamiltonian.py:109. Meanwhile the 'replacement' build_ionic_and_core is used by scf_potential.py:81 and kpm_dos.py:163. So the docstring is accurate about intent, and the real defect is the opposite of the claim: TWO parallel live implementations of the same physics (old numpy path in the production get_DFT_mtxels/kin_ion_io pipeline, new jitted path in scf_potential/kpm_dos) — a direct violation of the single-source-of-truth rule. The refactor should port the remaining callers to ionic_gspace and delete/archive the old builder, not fix a docstring.

item: src/psp/gvec_utils.py :: G2_int = round(G2*1e8) (L31)
evidence: This mirrors QE, not ad-hoc float hygiene: QE's ggen (Modules/recvec_subs.f90) sorts G-vectors by g² with tolerance eps8 = 1e-8 (hpsort_eps), grouping same-|G|² shells before a secondary ordering; rounding G2·1e8 to int64 and lexsorting (G2_int primary; g1,g2,g3 secondary, L31-32) reproduces exactly that shell-grouped ordering, which the docstring states is the goal ('Master G-vector list in QE convention', 'primary |G|² (discretised for shell grouping)'). Units also match QE: G2 = G·bdot·G (L26) is in tpiba² like QE's gg, so 1e-8 is the same absolute tolerance QE uses. Scale-dependence is bounded: int64 overflows only at G² > 9.2e10 tpiba² (absurd cutoffs), and float64 noise on symmetry-equivalent G² (~1e-16 relative) stays far below 1e-8 for any realistic ecutrho. Correct and deliberate; add a comment naming QE eps8.

item: src/psp/gvec_utils.py :: reorder_to_qe bare KeyError (L92)
evidence: Confirmed the mechanics: L89 builds dav_map from the Davidson-masked G set; L92's `dav_map[tuple(Gk_qe[ig])]` raises KeyError if any QE-ordered G-vector is absent. This can only fire on a genuine basis mismatch (different ecutwfc/kvec between H_k.mask and Gk_qe) — and it fails LOUDLY at the exact point of mismatch with the offending Miller triple in the exception text, so nothing silent or wrong-answer-producing happens; the hypothesis 'no diagnostic' overstates it (the missing part is only a human-friendly message naming the k-point and counts). Fail-fast is the right behavior. Optional polish: wrap in a try/except re-raising with nG_dav vs ngk context. Not a correctness or refactor risk.

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: entire file relative to lorrax_D main
evidence: The absence claim is verified — no src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py in lorrax_D (ls fails) and `git log --all -- src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py` in lorrax_D returns nothing, so the path never existed in this clone's history. But the module is NOT dead: it lives at sources/lorrax_B_orbmag_wt/src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py (686 lines) and is actively invoked as `python3 -u -m psp.orbital_magnetization` by runs/CrI3/04_gw_6x6_600b_2026-06-17/run_cri3_now.sh:35 and runs/VI3/04_gw_6x6_600b_2026-06-17/run_vi3_orbmag.sh:16, whose shifter env sets PYTHONPATH=$OMWT/src (OMWT=lorrax_B_orbmag_wt, run_vi3_orbmag.sh:6,12) — the same scripts point LROOT at lorrax_D for other steps, so the orbmag tool only works because of the split PYTHONPATH. It also imports lorrax_D-present symbols (psp.get_dipole_mtxels compute_p_operator_k/compute_vnl_velocity_cart at its L60). Verdict for the refactor map: alive-but-orphaned relative to lorrax_D — must be merged into D (making it the second in-tree caller of the L587/L630 velocity helpers), not treated as deletable.

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: four sign sites (MU_B_PREFACTOR L65, m_orb=-pref·Im L625, m_spin=-1.0·S_sum L240/L606, frame flip L626-627)
evidence: Read all four sites in lorrax_B_orbmag_wt/src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py; they are four DIFFERENT physical signs, not one convention scattered: (1) MU_B_PREFACTOR=0.5 (L65) is the magnitude |m_e/ħ²| in Ry·a0² with the docstring (L16-25) deriving the full formula m_γ/μ_B = (−1/2)·Σ w_k Im[...]; (2) the leading minus at L625 (m_orb = −MU_B_PREFACTOR·C_of_mu(mu).imag) is that formula's electron-charge sign, documented as 'orbital moment = −μ_B L/ħ' (L23-24) with a pointer to orbital_magnetization_THEORY.md; (3) m_spin_z = −1.0·S_sum (L240, L606) is the independent spin-moment charge sign m_spin ≈ −g μ_B⟨S_z⟩/ħ (and is un-negated back for printing at L622); (4) frame=±1 (L626-627) is a deliberate REPORTING device — docstring L28-31: report the orbital moment relative to the spin axis 'in a convention-robust way' precisely so absolute-sign ambiguities cancel. Each is locally documented and they compose correctly (m_orb_par > 0 ⇔ parallel to spin, L636). Consolidation into one signed prefactor would actually lose the deliberate magnitude/sign separation; at most, merge (1)+(2).

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: --vnl-sign auto with hf[-1] < 0.8·hf[1] margin (L581-590)
evidence: Confirmed exactly as suspected, and it is knowingly designed that way rather than a bug: the comment block L577-580 states 'Physical sign = p+vNL (canonical velocity_matrix_k); verified compute_vnl_velocity_cart == +dV_NL/dk off-diagonally (ratio 1.000). dV_NL/dk is ~900x larger off-diagonal, so the diagonal HF test ties — it validates kinetic part/units only. Flip only on a large margin.' So auto → 'plus' in practice (L587-589 even prints 'HF diagonal test insensitive to nonlocal sign; using canonical p+vNL'), and a minus win triggers a warning ('unexpected; check sign', L585). This matches project memory verbatim (diagonal HF test can't see the sign; physical dH/dk = p+vNL). The second half of the suspect's hypothesis is also confirmed: orbital_magnetization uses the UNFLIPPED +dV_NL/dk (V = Vp + sign·Vnl, L598; run_ibz L224 adds +sign·vnl_velocity_matrix) — deliberately opposite to get_dipole_mtxels main() L636 which stores BGW-convention p−vNL in dipole.h5. The 0.8 margin and the 'auto' default are a documented guardrail, not a broken detector; only nit is that 'auto' could be named 'canonical-with-sanity-check'.

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: hf_group_velocity_check in-plane-only FD + magic thresholds (L353-414)
evidence: Confirmed all specifics: steps = [(0,nkx),(1,nky)] with comment 'in-plane only (kz single layer)' (L373) — note the kz axis is skipped UNCONDITIONALLY by the steps list, so even a 3D grid with nkz≥3 would silently get an xy-only check (the `if N < 3: continue` guard at L375-376 only protects coarse axes); degeneracy skip 2e-3 Ry (L392, annotated '≈27 meV'), dispersion floor ‖gc[:2]‖ < 0.02 (L397), band cap min(nb,200) (L391), and comparisons restricted to components [:2] throughout (L400-401). These are CrI3-monolayer sample-selection heuristics inside a helper whose signature (kgrid argument) looks generic. Mitigating: it is a diagnostic ONLY — its output feeds the vnl-sign auto decision, which per L577-589 defaults to 'plus' regardless and warns on the flip — so no physics output is corrupted for any system; a 3D user would just get a weaker sanity check plus possibly a spurious sign warning. On merge into lorrax_D: either generalize steps to all axes with N≥3 or rename/document it as a 2D in-plane diagnostic.

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: sz = Σ(|c_up|²−|c_dn|²) spinor-z assumption (L98, L227, L340)
evidence: Confirmed at all three sites: sz = (|psi[:,0]|² − |psi[:,1]|²).sum over G, i.e. ⟨σ_z⟩ assuming spinor component 0 = up-z, 1 = down-z — the standard QE/BGW noncollinear spinor basis, so the ordering assumption is safe for any QE-derived WFN. The z quantization axis is not hidden: the module docstring says explicitly 'the spin moment <sigma_z> from the same WFN as an internal calibration ... must be ~±6 μ_B for CrI3' (L27-31) and the printout labels it sum_occ <sigma_z> (L622). It is used for (a) calibration printing and (b) the frame=sign(m_spin_z) flip (L626); both would be misleading for a magnetization not along z — and the code half-knows this, since --mag-axis (L443-447) generalizes the point-group selection but sz stays z-projected. So: correctly labeled convention for the CrI3 use case; on merge, compute the spin projection along --mag-axis (σ·m̂) instead of hard σ_z to close the gap.

item: src/psp/orbital_magnetization.py :: run_ibz silently renormalizes kweights (L186-188)
evidence: Confirmed: `if abs(w_ibz.sum() - 1.0) > 1e-6: w_ibz = w_ibz / w_ibz.sum()` with no message (L187-188). Assessed the failure surface: BGW-format wfn.h5 kw normally sums to 1; QE-side weights can sum to 2 (spin-degenerate convention) or to nk (count-like conventions) — renormalization maps all of these to correct fractions PROVIDED relative weights are right, which is true for every convention difference and false only for genuinely corrupted per-k weights (which renormalizing neither causes nor worsens — the result would be wrong with or without it). So it masks only the absolute scale, and only from the user's eyes, not from correctness. Everything else in the function assumes Σw=1 (docstring L186 'IBZ weights, sum = 1'), so the guard is the right behavior minus a warning print. Classify benign-cruft; add `print(f'renormalizing kweights (sum={...})')` on merge.

item: src/solvers/__init__.py — entire re-export layer
evidence: Zero package-level importers anywhere: grep for 'from solvers import' / 'import solvers' over lorrax_D src/, tests/, tools/, scripts/, plus lorrax_sandbox/skills and lorrax_sandbox/runs returns nothing; pyproject [project.scripts] only exposes gw.gw_jax:main and centroid.kmeans_cli:main; no getattr/importlib dispatch into solvers. Every consumer imports submodules directly (e.g. bse/bse_lanczos.py:19, psp/run_sternheimer.py:73-78, psp/run_nscf.py:46). CAVEAT for the fix: the file still EXECUTES on every `from solvers.X import ...` (Python package semantics), eagerly importing davidson/lanczos/chebyshev/dos/pseudobands/pseudobands_v2/quadrature into every consumer — so the correct removal is to EMPTY it, not delete the package __init__.

item: src/solvers/lanczos.py — block_lanczos_eig (non-jit)
evidence: Sole call site is bse/bse_lanczos.py:83 behind solve_bse(use_block=True); no caller ever sets it: bse_jax.py:195 and :325 call solve_bse with defaults (use_block=False), src/bse/test_bse.py:257 passes use_block=False explicitly, and grep for 'use_block' over the whole sandbox (runs/, skills/, tools/, scripts/) finds no use_block=True. Re-exports in solvers/__init__.py:3 and bse_jax.py __all__ (line 52) have no importers. It is a dormant flag path with zero invocations; the jit variants (block_lanczos_eig_jit/_jit_converged, bse_lanczos.py:267,293,300) are the live paths.

Not confirmed-dead because live tests exercise it.

item: src/solvers/cg_posdef.py — cg_posdef, CGInfo

item: src/solvers/projectors.py — make_P_precond, make_P_rest, make_P_val
evidence: Production uses only make_Q_kminq (psp/run_sternheimer.py:73, psp/tests/test_sternheimer_jvp.py:61). Critically, make_Q_kminq does NOT delegate to make_P_rest — its closure body (projectors.py:106-107) is an independent duplicate of P_rest_novq (lines 87-88) — so make_P_rest is not kept alive via the alias. make_P_val/make_P_precond/make_P_rest are imported only by tests/test_sternheimer_solvers.py:63-102,188. They belong to the abandoned Schur-MINRES design (P_precond docstring line 66-67: 'Stage-2c Schur split ... before MINRES').

item: src/solvers/dos.py — estimate_spectrum (as public API)
evidence: The function is live in production: compute_dos calls it at dos.py:147, and compute_dos is invoked from pseudobands.py:308 and pseudobands_v2.py:512, which run via the production pseudobands path in psp/run_nscf.py:339-341/359-371. Only the PUBLIC re-export (solvers/__init__.py:11,28) and the docstring usage example (dos.py:11-13) are unused — that is facade rot, not dead code. Do not delete; at most drop it from __init__ (which is being emptied anyway).

item: src/solvers/__init__.py:3,12-13 — facade exports legacy block_lanczos_eig, omits jit variants and minres/cg_posdef/projectors, eager heavy imports
evidence: Confirmed rot: __init__.py:3 exports block_lanczos_eig (dead, see above) but not block_lanczos_eig_jit/_jit_converged which production imports directly (bse/bse_lanczos.py:20-22); minres/cg_posdef/projectors/sternheimer_precond/sternheimer_solve absent entirely. Because Python runs the package __init__ on any submodule import, lines 2-14 eagerly pull dos+pseudobands+pseudobands_v2+quadrature into every consumer of any solvers submodule (e.g. run_sternheimer importing solvers.projectors also imports both pseudobands modules). Nobody imports from the facade, so the fix is to empty the file — consistent with the sandbox no-redundancy rule.

item: src/solvers/davidson.py:313-318 — convergence counts only leading contiguous block
evidence: Not a bug: the loop sets n_conv = length of the leading all-True prefix of conv[0..n_eig-1], and the ONLY exit condition is n_conv == n_eig (line 328), which is mathematically identical to all(conv) — a prefix of length n_eig means every element is True. Sub-cases: conv=[T,F,T] → n_conv=1 (display under-reports but no exit change); conv all True → n_conv=n_eig → exit; so termination is exactly 'all converged'. The prefix count only affects the progress print (line 326) and the 'Best: n/n_eig' warning (line 351). Locked-prefix reporting is the standard Davidson convention (lowest states converge first); undocumented but harmless.

item: src/solvers/davidson.py:77,145,175 — 1e-12 Cholesky jitter repeated three times
evidence: Same literal in three near-identical Cholesky-of-Gram regularizations: _generalized_eigh (line 77, B_reg = B + 1e-12·I), _orthonormalise_batch (line 145), _ortho_expand (line 175). In all three the matrix being factored is a Gram/overlap matrix of (near-)orthonormal vectors, so diagonal ≈ 1 and 1e-12 is a correctly-scaled relative jitter in every instance — no correctness risk. Copy-paste of a magic constant; hoisting to one module-level _CHOL_JITTER would satisfy the no-redundancy rule but changes nothing numerically.

item: src/solvers/davidson.py:299 — X = V 'in case the loop never runs'
evidence: Dead for every real call: loop is range(1, max_iter+1) with max_iter default 100 and no caller passes max_iter<1 (grep of davidson( call sites in bse/davidson_absorption.py, bse/bse_lanczos.py:189, psp/run_nscf.py:46). The only path it guards (max_iter=0) is broken anyway: line 350-351 would raise NameError on undefined n_conv when verbose=True, and the return would carry eigenvalues=None. So it is half-hearted defensive leftover, not functional protection.

item: src/solvers/lanczos.py:541,570 — LARGE=1.0e6 diagonal shift masking inactive T blocks
evidence: Exact, not approximate, as used: inactive alpha/beta blocks are exactly zero (pre-allocated zeros, lines 489-490, never written past j_final), so after adding 1e6 to inactive diagonal slots T is exactly block-diagonal = active_T ⊕ 1e6·I; eigvalsh yields the active spectrum unperturbed plus eigenvalues exactly 1e6, and sort()[:n_eig] / argsort[:n_eig] (lines 547, 576) pick only active Ritz values provided the sought lowest n_eig eigenvalues < 1e6. BSE spectra are O(1) Ry, so safe in every current use (bse_lanczos.py:267,293). The landmine claim is technically right for a 'no physics knowledge' generic solver — an operator with lowest eigenvalues ≥ 1e6 (or a masked eigenvalue landing inside the sought range) silently corrupts results — but no such caller exists.

item: src/solvers/lanczos.py:91-93 — full reorth against ALL stored blocks after three-term subtraction
evidence: Correctly identified as mathematically redundant: the loop over Q_blocks (lines 91-93) includes Q_blocks[-1]=Q_current and Q_blocks[-2]=Q_prev, re-projecting out exactly the components already removed by the alpha (line 86) and beta (line 89) subtractions. But the ordering is the classical 'local recurrence + full reorthogonalization' belt-and-suspenders: subtracting the dominant alpha/beta components first reduces cancellation error before the O(j) reorth sweep, and alpha_j/beta_j are needed for T regardless. It differs from the jit variants' rolling n_reorth window (lines 407-414) by design. Moot in practice — the whole function is confirmed-dead (see above).

item: src/solvers/lanczos.py:281,419 (also 516) — Q.at[:, min(j+1, max_iter-1)] overwrites last column on final iteration
evidence: Real defect, mild severity. Index math: iteration j writes q_{j+1} to column min(j+1, max_iter-1); for j=0..max_iter-2 the writes land at j+1 correctly, but at the final j=max_iter-1 the clamp redirects q_{max_iter} onto column max_iter-1, clobbering q_{max_iter-1}. T (built from alpha/beta rows 0..max_iter-1) pairs row max_iter-1 with basis column max_iter-1, which now holds the wrong vector; eigenvalues (T only) are exact, but eigenvector reconstruction Q @ vecs_T (lines 296, 435, 579) uses the wrong last basis vector with weight |vecs_T[last, idx]| — small (~residual) for converged Ritz pairs, O(1) for unconverged ones. The recurrence itself is unaffected (q carried in the loop state; the clobbering write is the loop's final action). Worse than doing nothing: JAX's default out-of-bounds scatter mode DROPS the update, so removing the clamp would be correct as-is. block_lanczos_eig_jit_converged (line 516) is only affected when the while_loop runs the full M iterations (early convergence exit at j_final<M never triggers the clamp).

item: src/solvers/chebyshev.py:206 — E_tilde clipped to ±(1-1e-10) before 1/sqrt(1-e^2)
evidence: Standard KPM singularity guard for the Chebyshev weight 1/(π·hw·√(1-ẽ²)) (line 221). compute_dos's grid is linspace(E_min, E_max) (dos.py:174), so the two endpoint samples land exactly at ẽ=±1 and get amplified by 1/√(2e-10)≈7e4 × cheb_sum. With 2% spectral padding (estimate_spectrum pad_fraction, dos.py:60,97) the true DOS at the edges ≈ 0, so cheb_sum is the small Gibbs/Jackson residue and the artifact is confined to the 2 edge grid points; downstream consumers integrate via trapezoid (dos.py:178,334) or window between E_cross and E_max, so the effect is a negligible endpoint-weighted term. The suspect's hypothesis (edge values are guard artifacts, not physics) is correct.

item: src/solvers/minres.py:201-209,282 — _BETA_DEADBAND=1e-14 per-band alive-mask
evidence: Not a symptom patch: it is the JIT-compatible per-band expression of the reference algorithm's documented termination case. Paige–Saunders 1975 and SciPy's minres both short-circuit outright when β₀=0 (the RHS is zero, x=x₀ is the exact solution); a fixed-trip lax.fori_loop cannot early-exit per band, so the mask (lines 202, 282) pins dead bands at x₀ and zeroes their Lanczos state, exactly reproducing the short-circuit. Without it v₁=r₀/β₀ amplifies ~1e-15 noise to unit scale and NaNs — the comment (lines 194-200) documents precisely this with the SciPy/P-S citation. Whole module is test-only anyway.

item: src/solvers/minres.py:113-117,141 — tol informational only, iters_used always == max_iter
evidence: Documented twice, and the API does not actually lie: module header lines 21-24 ('no in-loop early exit (cost-free for O(10) bands; variable iteration counts would force recompilation)') and the tol docstring line 113-114 ('Informational only (no early exit)'). iters_used=int(max_iter) (line 141) is truthful — the fori_loop (line 290) always executes exactly max_iter iterations — and MinresInfo.converged is computed from the real final residual (lines 137-139). This is the standard fixed-shape JIT trade-off, same pattern as cg_posdef and the block-Lanczos jit variants.

item: src/solvers/cg_posdef.py:124,176-180 — iters_used == max_iter; frozen bands still compute precond+rho every iteration
evidence: Same fixed-trip JIT pattern as minres, and correct: per-band freeze is enforced where it matters — alpha (line 163) and beta (line 180) are masked to 0 for dead bands, so x, r, and the accepted answer are bit-stable after convergence, mirroring QE cgsolve_all's conv(ibnd) logic (module docstring lines 30-33). z_new/rho_new for frozen bands (lines 176-177) feed only the masked beta, so the cost is wasted FLOPs on already-converged batch elements — unavoidable without ragged per-band control flow inside one compiled kernel. Note the freeze uses the carry's alive_mask, so a band converging at step k gets one final (harmless, masked-at-k+1) p update. Module is confirmed-dead anyway.

item: src/solvers/projectors.py:96-108 — make_Q_kminq documented alias-duplicate of make_P_rest(U, None)
evidence: Confirmed duplicate bodies, not a delegating alias: make_Q_kminq's closure (lines 106-107: x - _apply_P_U(U, x)) is byte-for-byte the same computation as P_rest_novq (lines 87-88). The docstring (99-102) justifies the name for math-notation 1:1 with the Sternheimer guide, which was reasonable while the Schur design lived; now that make_P_val/make_P_precond/make_P_rest are test-only remnants of that abandoned design, the consolidation per the sandbox no-redundancy rule is: keep make_Q_kminq (production name, run_sternheimer.py:73), delete the other three factories and port their tests.

item: src/solvers/quadrature.py:23 vs 55-57 — docstring says trapezoid-rule, implementation is midpoint
evidence: Doc rot only; the code is verified correct for midpoint. Per-element check: nodes θ_j = π(2j-1)/(2N) (line 57) with Δθ=π/N; the exact contour weight is z'(θ)·Δθ/(2πi) with z'(θ) = -r_x sinθ + i r_y cosθ, and (-r_x sinθ + i r_y cosθ)/(2iN) = (r_y cosθ + i r_x sinθ)/(2N) — exactly line 60. The midpoint choice is likely deliberate, not drift: trapezoid endpoints θ=0,π would place nodes at z = center ± r_x, ON the real axis at the interval edges where the resolvent (z-H)⁻¹ blows up near eigenvalues; midpoint keeps all nodes strictly off-axis. Only the word 'trapezoid' (lines 3, 23) needs fixing.

item: src/solvers/dos.py:262 — eps_bar no-op '- E_cross + E_cross'; error metric uses absolute window-center energy
evidence: CONFIRMED as written / LATENT in effect. The expression 0.5*(trial+e_lo) - E_cross + E_cross is a literal no-op leaving eps_bar = ABSOLUTE window center, while the surrounding comments (lines 262-265) and the Altman S36 metric (line 210-213: ε̄_j from E_F) require distance from the Fermi level; the correct in-scope expression would need eps_cross: 0.5*(trial+e_lo) - E_cross + eps_cross (eps_cross is not passed in — the reader's hypothesis matches). Mitigating fact the first pass missed: every production caller passes E_fermi=0.0 (run_nscf.py:361,369,400,411 → pseudobands.py:324 E_cross_abs = 0 + eps_cross), making absolute ≡ E_F-relative, so current pseudoband runs are numerically unaffected. Also pseudobands_v2 does not use this function at all (own _place_windows_v2, pseudobands_v2.py:382). The bug arms silently the first time anyone passes a real Fermi energy — worth fixing by threading eps_cross (or E_fermi) into dos_weighted_windows.

item: src/solvers/dos.py:256-268 — 500-point linspace scan per window despite 'binary search' comment
evidence: Confirmed mismatch: comment at line 253 says 'Binary search for e_hi' but the code is a first-crossing linear scan over np.linspace(e_lo + 0.1%·range, E_max, 500) (line 256). The metric (n_eff·σ^{2k}/ε̄^{2k+1}) is monotone-increasing in trial to good approximation over a window (n_eff and δ grow; ε̄ grows slower), so first-crossing ≈ root-find with boundary resolution quantized to ~0.2% of the remaining range. Cost is trivial (≤500 np.interp per boundary, host-side, once per partition), and the outer tau bisection absorbs the quantization when targeting a window count. Misleading comment + coarse quantization, no correctness failure identified.

item: src/solvers/dos.py:284-295 — tau geometric bisection, |n_w-target|<=1 acceptance, trailing _place_windows(tau_mid) re-call
evidence: The 'stale tau_mid' concern does not hold in the accepted case: the break (line 293-294) fires in the same iteration that produced n_w from tau_mid, and _place_windows is deterministic, so the trailing return (line 295) recomputes exactly the accepted bounds — wasteful (one redundant partition) but never stale. Only when 50 iterations exhaust without |n_w-target|≤1 does the tail call return the last midpoint's bounds, which is a reasonable best-effort (log-bisection over [1e-30,1e10] converges the bracket to ~1e-30·10^(40·(1-2^-50)) — after 50 halvings of a 40-decade log-range the bracket is ~1e-11 decades wide, so non-convergence means the target count is unattainable, e.g. fewer grid points than windows). ±1 window slack is a loose but sensible acceptance for an integer-valued objective. Cleanup candidate: `return bounds` from the loop; not a correctness issue.

item: src/psp/archive/projector_pipeline.py — entire module (all __all__ names)

item: projector_pipeline.py:302-306 — Z_cat._dZ_rad/_dZ_ang attribute smuggling on np.ndarray
evidence: Confirmed silent no-op: Z_cat = np.vstack(...).astype(np.complex128) (line 291) is a plain ndarray; ndarray forbids new instance attributes, so 'Z_cat._dZ_rad = dZ_rad' raises AttributeError, swallowed by the bare 'except Exception: pass' (305-306). Grep confirms zero readers of _dZ_rad/_dZ_ang anywhere outside these two lines. The data was later exposed properly by build_beta_rows_with_grad_components (line 310, returning 5-tuple incl. dZ_rad, dZ_ang — the function the misc checker imports at check_vnl_velocity_vs_num.py:101), confirming the hypothesis that this hack predates the _components variant and never delivered data to any consumer.

item: projector_pipeline.py:254 vs 358 (q->0 guard 1e-12 vs 1.0) and :271 vs :373 (extra l*radial/q term in _components)
evidence: Explicit index/derivative math: both functions assemble identical rows Z = A(q)·Y_lm(K-hat)·phase with A = pref·i^l·F_l(q), where F_l is tabulated DIRECTLY as F_l(q)=∫r²β(r)j_l(qr)dr (make_projector_table, radial/radial_jax.py:294-303 → _spherical_hankel_table_np) and Y is the degree-0 unit-sphere harmonic from qe_real_sph_harmonics_with_grad, whose returned grad_cart = e_θ∂θY + e_φ(∂φY/sinθ) (build_projectors_qe.py:328-331) has NO e_r component and NO 1/q factor. Hence the exact gradient is ∇_K Z = F'(q)·Y·e_r + (F/q)·grad_cart. The base version (line 271: base_rad = radial_p·Y) implements exactly this. The _components version (line 373: base_rad = (radial_p + l·radial/q_safe)·Y) adds a spurious +l·F/q·Y·e_r term — that term would only be correct if the tables stored G_l = F_l/q^l with solid-harmonic angular parts (which is what live vnl_ops.py:177-183 does), but these tables do not. Since Z is identical in both, at most one dZ can be right, and by the derivation it is the base one; the _components variant used by the (now unrunnable) FD checker is wrong. The 1e-12 vs 1.0 guard is secondary copy divergence: 1.0 matches _angles_from_cart_vectors (build_projectors_qe.py:177) and is safer (with 1e-12, l=0 gives base_ang≈1e12·F(0), masked only because gradY≡0 at l=0).

item: projector_pipeline.py:459-463 — velocity sign 'v^NL = -(∇_q+∇_q')V_NL, apply a minus sign, no extra i'
evidence: Worked the commutator: with ψ(q)=⟨q|ψ⟩ and r=i∇_q, ⟨q|[V,r]|q'⟩ = -i(∇_q+∇_{q'})V(q,q'), so the PHYSICAL v^NL = (i/ħ)[V_NL,r] = +(∇_q+∇_{q'})V. The code computes vNL -= (term1+term2) where term1+term2 = ⟨n|(∇_q+∇_{q'})V|m⟩ (verified: term1 = conj(dP)·E·P with P(r,s,b)=Σ_g Z*(r,g)C(b,s,g), so term1_nm = Σ C*(n)∇Z E Z* C(m) = ∇_q V in the band basis), i.e. it returns i[r,V_NL] = -v^NL_physical — consistent with the function docstring 'Compute i [r_i, V_NL]' (line 414) but NOT with the line-459 comment, whose identity '(i/ħ)[V_NL, r_i] = -(∇+∇')V' has the commutator order backwards for its own sign. This -∂V/∂k quantity is exactly the p-vNL BGW dipole convention documented in project memory; the live canonical p+vNL path is vnl_ops.vnl_velocity_matrix / apply_vnl_velocity_to_ket (vnl_ops.py:490-518), which returns +∂V_NL/∂k with no sign flip. So: intentional BGW convention, but the prose derivation justifying it is itself erroneous — the landmine the first-pass reader suspected, now confined to a dead module.

item: projector_pipeline.py — unused wfn_k/Gk_crys params in build_beta_rows_with_grad*, lstsq B recovery (line 688), plan-dict mutation (430-431), mid-file __all__ (467)
